source.title: SOC2 evidence scrapbook
source.content:
2026-03-10 — quick personal scratchpad for SOC2 tasks / vendor checks

Top of mind / priorities for next week:
- [ ] Finalize access review CSV for Q1 (exported from Okta, need to scrub service accounts) — ETA: Mon 2026-03-15
- [ ] Confirm encryption-in-transit diagrams updated in infra confluence page (link above) — ping @Theo
- [ ] Collect evidence for change control: list of merged PRs with approvals for last 6 months (PRs + approvals csv) — see GH PR link, ask @Lina for script
- [ ] Vendor due diligence: re-run questionnaire for new CDN vendor (EdgeFlow) — need SOC2 report or ISO cert, collect legal T&Cs
- [ ] Update policy TODO: "Third-party risk" section needs a fill-in for monitoring cadence and SLA expectations

Notes / raw fragments:
- Exported Okta report: okta-users-export-2026-03-09.csv (local, not uploaded yet). Need to remove columns: phone, address, personal_email. Only keep: user_id, email, last_login, groups, is_admin.
- Evidence bucket path proposal: s3://redwood-sec/soc2-evidence/q1-2026/ — confirm lifecycle policy (encrypt, 7y retention?)
- EdgeFlow vendor: ask sales for updated SOC2 report or auditor contact. If not available, fallback: use CloudFront for static assets as temporary (note: TTL/cost tradeoffs).
- Change control evidence: we need a concise matrix mapping JIRA tickets -> PRs -> approvals (who + timestamp). Suggested fields: ticket, pr_url, approvers, merge_time, deploy_tag.
- Pending ask from audit team: provide "logical access" story for service accounts on Dedicated infra. Draft idea: list all service accounts, least-privilege roles, token rotation cadence.

Unresolved questions / todo (brain dump):
- How to capture ephemeral infra changes that only exist in scripts (terraform state drift): should we take a snapshot of modules and drift detection logs? (ask infra-sre)
- Are we comfortable with current KMS key rotation policy? doc exists but I think rotation window is 2y? need to confirm -> lower to 90d? risk: operational overhead.
- Vendor classification: EdgeFlow currently classified as low-medium. Re-evaluate based on access scope (EdgeFlow has ability to read logs -> bump to medium-high).

Meeting follow-ups:
- 2026-03-11 09:30 sync with Theo (audit prep) -> items: OKTA scrub checklist, evidence bucket retention, timeline for vendor questionnaires.
- 2026-03-12 14:00 quick with Lina -> delegating GH PR extraction script + mapping output format.

Quick commands / scripts to run (notes to self):
- GH PR export (local): python tools/pr_export.py --since 2025-09-01 --org redwood-inference --out pr_approvals_q1.csv
- Okta scrub (one-liner): csvcut -c user_id,email,last_login,groups,is_admin okta-export.csv > okta-soc2.csv && python scripts/scrub_pii.py okta-soc2.csv

Draft text to paste into Confluence evidence index (rough):
"Access reviews: monthly user access reviews are performed by engineering managers. Evidence: Okta export (okta-soc2.csv) and review tickets (SEC-827). Review cadence: monthly; next scheduled: 2026-03-15. Owner: Ava Chen.
Change control: All production changes require a JIRA ticket and two engineer approvals on the GitHub PR. Evidence: PR approvals export and deployment tags stored in s3://redwood-sec/soc2-evidence/q1-2026/change-control.csv. Owner: Lina Gomez."

Random notes to self (todo low priority):
- Draft email to legal asking for vendor contract standard clause for log access
- Attach screenshots of KMS key page for evidence (quick win)
- Make a checklist template for "evidence packet" per control (helpful for next audit)

Tags for this doc: quick notes, draft evidence list, don't share externally (contains internal file paths and vendor assessments)

End of scratchpad.

source.title: Veritas Healthcare — cold sequence playbench & account notes
source.content:
Top-line: quick playbench for Veritas Healthcare (regional provider network, ~650 clinics). Good fit for Redwood Private + Dedicated for pilot: PHI sensitivity, EHR integrations, predictable throughput for chart summarization and intake triage. Notes to self: this is a very conservative IT org (infosec-heavy), legal will push for on-prem/VPC and strong KMS/HSM controls. They mention vendor-hosted ML with PHI as a hard stop.

Account snapshot:
- Veritas Healthcare (not the same as national Veritas brand) — multi-state ambulatory network, revenue cycle focus, clinician documentation backlog (nurses/providers spend 2–3 hrs/day documenting). IT: small centralized infra team + vendor management.
- Decision makers: CISO (Amir Rao), Director of Clinical Innovation (Paula Ortega), Head of VBC programs (Greg Lin). Procurement/Legal flagged as slow.
- Existing tech: Epic EHR, on-prem integration layer (Mirth), Okta SSO, Azure AD for identity, internal Kubernetes for non-PHI workloads.

Primary pain / buyer problems:
1) Clinician burnout from documentation -> want accurate chart summarization + suggested note drafts.
2) Call center triage latency -> 95th pct token latency matters when generating summarizations during live calls.
3) PHI/regulatory -> cannot send PHI to unknown public endpoints; need audit logs + KMS + SOC2 type 2 + BAAs.
4) Cost predictability — CFO wants capped spend for pilot.

Key Redwood positioning to test:
- Redwood Private (VPC + customer-managed control plane) or on-prem to meet PHI constraint. Emphasize BAAs + KMS/HSM options and audit logs.
- Dedicated for predictable throughput & latency SLOs — call-center triage needs <300ms tail for short summaries (ambitious but sellable).
- Optimize toolkit for batching + KV caching to reduce per-token cost for repeated templates (notes templates).
- Console for traceability: token-level cost/latency and prompt-level evals to detect quality drift.

Risks / technical questions to confirm early:
- EHR connectivity: can we run a pilot with de‑identified export vs. live Epic integration? Which Epic interfaces (FHIR vs. HL7) are they willing to open?
- Legal: will they accept a VPC control plane hosted in their cloud account or must it be fully air-gapped on-prem?
- Security: HSM integration required? Which KMS vendor? Azure Key Vault or AWS?

Outbound cold sequence (4 touches) — rough drafts:
Touch 1 (LinkedIn short message):
Hi Paula — Maya at Redwood Inference. We help provider networks cut clinician doc time by ~30–50% with private, audit-able LLM inference that meets PHI controls. Quick 10‑min intro?

Touch 2 (email day 3) — subject: Pilot idea to reduce clinician doc time at Veritas
Hi Paula / Amir,
I know vendor risk is a top constraint for any PHI workload — we run inference in customer VPCs or on-prem and offer audit logs + KMS integrations. Quick idea: 6‑week pilot to produce draft notes from a subset of encounter types (de‑identified exports first). Would you be open to a quick call?

Touch 3 (email day 7) — short case + ask
- One-pager: healthcare pilot outline (scope, SLOs, data flows, legal milestones).
Ask: 20‑minute slot to walk through pilot plan and see where legal/procurement would push back.

Touch 4 (call / voicemail day 14) — leave a one‑sentence value prop and ask for best next step.

50–80 word cold email variant for A/B testing:
Subject: Private LLM pilot for Veritas — low-risk PHI controls
Hi Paula — quick note: we’ve run on‑prem/VPC pilots that keep PHI inside customer boundaries while cutting clinician doc time. We can scope a 6‑week trial on a small set of encounters and provide compliance artifacts for your security review. 20 mins to see if it’s sensible?

Call script (discovery + next step focus):
- Intro + one-sentence pitch (Redwood does private, predictable LLM inference).
- Confirm scope: which note types are highest pain? (ED triage, follow-up visit, chronic-care med management)
- Who needs to be involved for security ok? (Legal/security, infra)
- Ask about scale: how many notes/day, avg tokens per note, peak concurrency?
- If they ask about latency/cost: explain Dedicated + batching tradeoffs and Optimize recommendations — we can model expected per‑note cost if they share token estimates.
- Next step: propose pilot plan doc + legal checklist within 5 business days.

Common objections & suggested responses (draft):
- Objection: "We can't send PHI to a vendor-managed API."
  Response: "Understood — Redwood supports private control planes and on-prem options; pilot can run in your VPC with customer-managed keys. We also provide audit logs and a BAA template up-front."
- Objection: "Too risky operationally — if model fails during live triage we'll get incorrect notes."
  Response: "Pilot approach: start with de‑identified exports and human-in-the-loop approval; production rollout only after quality thresholds met and canary policy enabled. Console shows per-prompt regressions so we can block bad rollouts."
- Objection: "Costs are unpredictable with LLMs."
  Response: "Dedicated capacity + quantization recommendations + KV caching reduce unit costs. We'll run a cost model for your token profile during scoping and propose spend caps for the pilot."

- Week 4: Human review loop, quality tuning and prompt/eval iterations.
- Week 5: Canary on limited live traffic (read-only drafting).
- Week 6: Pilot report — quality metrics, latency/cost numbers, recommended next steps + playbook for production rollout.

Metrics to include in pilot report:

title: Veritas Healthcare — cold sequence playbench & account notes
preview:
Top-line: quick playbench for Veritas Healthcare (regional provider network, ~650 clinics). Good fit for Redwood Private + Dedicated for pilot: PHI sensitivity, EHR integrations, predictable throughput for chart summarization and intake triage. Notes to self: this is a very conservative IT org (infosec-heavy), legal will push for on-prem/VPC and strong KMS/HSM controls. They mention vendor-hosted ML with PHI as a hard stop.

Account snapshot:
- Veritas Healthcare (not the same as national Veritas brand) — multi-state ambulatory network, revenue cycle focus, clinician documentation backlog (nurses/providers spend 2–3 hrs/day documenting). IT: small centralized infra team + vendor management.
- Decision makers: CISO (Amir Rao), Director of Clinical Innovation (Paula Ortega), Head of VBC programs (Greg Lin). Procurement/Legal flagged as slow.
- Existing tech: Epic EHR, on-prem integration layer (Mirth), Okta SSO, Azure AD for identity, internal Kubernetes for non-PHI workloads.

Primary pain / buyer problems:
1) Clinician burnout from documentation -> want accurate chart summarization + suggested note drafts.
2) Call center triage latency -> 95th pct token latency matters when generating summarizations during live calls.
3) PHI/regulatory -> cannot send PHI to unknown public endpoints; need audit logs + KMS + SOC2 type 2 + BAAs.
4) Cost predictability — CFO wants capped spend for pilot.

Key Redwood positioning to test:
- Redwood Private (VPC + customer-managed control plane) or on-prem to meet PHI constraint. Emphasize BAAs + KMS/HSM options and audit logs.
- Dedicated for predictable throughput & latency SLOs — call-center triage needs <300ms tail for short summaries (ambitious but sellable).
- Optimize toolkit for batching + KV caching to reduce per-token cost for repeated templates (notes templates).
- Console for traceability: token-level cost/latency and prompt-level evals to detect quality drift.

Risks / technical questions to confirm early:
- EHR connectivity: can we run a pilot with de‑identified export vs. live Epic integration? Which Epic interfaces (FHIR vs. HL7) are they willing to open?
- Legal: will they accept a VPC control plane hosted in their cloud account or must it be fully air-gapped on-prem?
- Security: HSM integration required? Which KMS vendor? Azure Key Vault or AWS?

source.title: Cobalt Retail — UX integration & pilot outreach scratchpad
source.body:
Topline: Cobalt is a mid-market retail POS/loyalty provider (regional in NE US). They’ve been down our outbound list for 6 mos; product emails caught some interest from their Head of Product.

Purpose of this doc: scratchpad for account research, quick outbound drafts, talking points for intro call, and objection handling. Treat as ephemeral; will move polished content to Confluence once we have pilot commitment.

Account background (quick):
- Company: Cobalt Retail (CobaltPOS) — ~220 employees, focus on small chains and boutique stores.
- HQ: Boston.
- Tech stack: SaaS POS (Ruby + Postgres), mobile apps (React Native), internal infra on AWS.
- Recent fundraising: Series B (~2024) — expanding merchant analytics and personalized UX.
- Known challenge: balancing on-device UX latency for recommendations and loyalty prompts; wants to surface contextual product suggestions without heavy server roundtrips.

Why Redwood might fit:
- Low-latency inference at scale across hosted/dedicated/private. For Cobalt they'd likely want Dedicated (predictable throughput for peak shopping hours) or Private (if they insist on VPC for customer data).
- Prefix/KV caching could speed up common prompt patterns (product suggestions, upsell copy) and reduce token cost.
- Rollouts + canary policies to test different prompt templates with minimal risk during holiday peak.

Potential internal champions/contact list (notes):
- Priya Nair — Head of Product (warm via LinkedIn like 2 months ago). Good promoter.
- Aaron Lee — Eng Manager, backend (responsible for APIs and infra). Technical gate.
- Lucia Gomez — Head of Data & Analytics. Interested in embeddings & recommendations.
- Sales Ops -> might care about per-customer latency and costs.

Key value props to highlight per persona:
- Product (Priya): faster end-to-end UX, configurable rollout, A/B prompts easily, lower cognitive load for merchants.
- Eng (Aaron): predictable throughput, integration SDKs (+ example Rails init), model pinning, SLOs and tracing hooks.
- Data (Lucia): embeddings for merchant similarity and reranking, quality monitoring and evals for prompt regressions.

Technical notes / integration constraints (quick):
- They use Ruby on Rails; we should have a lightweight example for: server-side generation for receipts and in-store kiosks.
- Peak concurrency: estimate 2.5k reqs/min during local peaks; need to propose Dedicated sizing options and burst policies.
- Compliance: some merchants require PCI-scope minimization — clarify that Redwood Private or Dedicated with encryption/KMS can meet needs.

Outreach plan (sequence):
1) Short intro email to Priya (personalize on loyalty/ux note) + 1-pager attached (pilot idea).
2) If no reply in 5 days -> LinkedIn message referencing the email + quick stat.
3) If interest, schedule 30-min intro with Priya + Aaron.
4) Pre-call packet: one-pager, rough SLOs/latency numbers, cost estimate for 3-month pilot.
5) Pilot: 3-month pilot, scope = checkout product suggestion + receipt personalization for 50 stores.

Cold email draft (short, two variants):
Subject ideas: "Reduce checkout latency for Cobalt merchants" / "Pilot idea: faster checkout recommendations for Cobalt"
Email v1 (concise):
Hi Priya —
We built a quick idea for how Cobalt could cut checkout recommendation latency and lower per-transaction inference costs. Redwood runs inference with predictable SLOs and dedicated capacity (we also support VPC/private installs). Would you be open to a 20‑min pilot discussion next week?
— Maya

Email v2 (more context):
Priya —
Spotted Cobalt's latest loyalty work — nice stuff. A couple thoughts: many merchants struggle with recommendation latency at POS, which hurts conversion. We’ve helped similar chains reduce time-to-suggestion by ~40% using a combination of KV prefix caching + Dedicated instances during peak hours. Attached is a one-pager with a 3-month pilot outline and rough cost range. If this is interesting I can sync Aaron with a short tech Q&A.

Call prep bullets (for intro call):
- 5 min: quick intros + current pain (Priya explains).
- 10 min: demo/architecture talk (how Redwood serves low-latency recommendations: batching, kv-cache, model pinning).
- 10 min: pilot proposal + success metrics (reduction in suggestion latency, lift in add-to-cart, cost per suggestion).
- 5 min: next steps / timeline.

Objection handling (short map):
- "We don’t want vendor infra touching customer PII/PCI": explain Dedicated (isolation) and Private (VPC + on-prem if needed), KMS, and can scope data sent to inference to tokenized/non-PII fields. Offer security one-pager and SOC2 + contractual SLAs.
- "Cost is too high": present Optimize toolkit (quantization + batching) + pilot scope (50 stores) with expected unit economics and break-even. Offer to run a cost simulation on a sample dataset.
- "We already have a recommendation engine": ask where latency/cost pain points are, propose hybrid flow (local lightweight model for 90% cases + Redwood fallback for long-tail). Emphasize rollouts and A/B testing to prove ROI.
- "Integration work is heavy": show Rails + JS sample endpoints, we can provide a 2‑week integration support from Solutions Eng.

Pilot framing (metrics to measure):
- Primary: median latency for checkout suggestion (ms), conversion lift (%) at checkout.
- Secondary: tokens per suggestion (cost), infra errors/rate limit incidents, merchant satisfaction score.
- Goal: reach ≥25% latency reduction and positive ROI within 3 months.

Open questions / assumptions we must validate before call:
- Confirm whether merchant transaction payloads include PII fields we must avoid sending.
- Real expected concurrency numbers (they gave us a public traffic figure but need more granularity).
- Payment flows: in-store offline vs online — different constraints.

Quick todos (next 7 days):
- [ ] Personalize one-pager + attach quick cost ballpark (Ethan to help with numbers).
- [ ] Draft LinkedIn note and sequence (Sofia).
- [ ] Prepare Rails example snippet (Engineering quickstart PR link to attach).
- [ ] Run short security FAQ doc update for Dedicated/Private options (copy from security-and-compliance notes).

Notes to self / coaching phrases:
- Priya responds to data + concrete numbers. Keep lead with expected latency reduction and a concrete pilot scope.
- Aaron will test guardrails — have tracing/troubleshooting hooks handy and mention our Console.
- Lucia wants embeddings; bring a short example of how merchant segmentation + reranking can be done with our embeddings endpoints.

Useful snippets to copy into Confluence later:
- 3-month pilot outline (scope, acceptance criteria) — one paragraph.
- Quick integration checklist (Rails init, env vars, tracer + SLOs).

Log / activity history (auto):
- 2025-10-12: initial research note (this doc created).
- 2025-11-05: LinkedIn like thread to Priya (no reply).
- 2026-02-18: Ethan ran cost sim draft (linked).
- 2026-03-09: Priya opened email (tracking).

If we get a meeting: immediate deliverable = tailored pre-call packet (one-pager, pilot cost band, Rails snippet), schedule SRE/solutions 30-min follow-up.

Random: keep this lightweight. Move final docs to shared_drives/product/ or confluence when pilot greenlit.

--- end of scratchpad ---

- Quick integration checklist (Rails init, env vars, tracer + SLOs).

Log / activity history (auto):
- 2025-10-12: initial research note (this doc created).
- 2025-11-05: LinkedIn like thread to Priya (no reply).
- 2026-02-18: Ethan ran cost sim draft (linked).
- 2026-03-09: Priya opened email (tracking).

If we get a meeting: immediate deliverable = tailored pre-call packet (one-pager, pilot cost band, Rails snippet), schedule SRE/solutions 30-min follow-up.

Random: keep this lightweight. Move final docs to shared_drives/product/ or confluence when pilot greenlit.

text:
Topline: Cobalt is a mid-market retail POS/loyalty provider (regional in NE US). They’ve been down our outbound list for 6 mos; product emails caught some interest from their Head of Product.

Purpose of this doc: scratchpad for account research, quick outbound drafts, talking points for intro call, and objection handling. Treat as ephemeral; will move polished content to Confluence once we have pilot commitment.

Account background (quick):
- Company: Cobalt Retail (CobaltPOS) — ~220 employees, focus on small chains and boutique stores.
- HQ: Boston.
- Tech stack: SaaS POS (Ruby + Postgres), mobile apps (React Native), internal infra on AWS.
- Recent fundraising: Series B (~2024) — expanding merchant analytics and personalized UX.
- Known challenge: balancing on-device UX latency for recommendations and loyalty prompts; wants to surface contextual product suggestions without heavy server roundtrips.

Why Redwood might fit:
- Low-latency inference at scale across hosted/dedicated/private. For Cobalt they'd likely want Dedicated (predictable throughput for peak shopping hours) or Private (if they insist on VPC for customer data).
- Prefix/KV caching could speed up common prompt patterns (product suggestions, upsell copy) and reduce token cost.
- Rollouts + canary policies to test different prompt templates with minimal risk during holiday peak.

Potential internal champions/contact list (notes):
- Priya Nair — Head of Product (warm via LinkedIn like 2 months ago). Good promoter.
- Aaron Lee — Eng Manager, backend (responsible for APIs and infra). Technical gate.
- Lucia Gomez — Head of Data & Analytics. Interested in embeddings & recommendations.
- Sales Ops -> might care about per-customer latency and costs.

Key value props to highlight per persona:
- Product (Priya): faster end-to-end UX, configurable rollout, A/B prompts easily, lower cognitive load for merchants.
- Eng (Aaron): predictable throughput, integration SDKs (+ example Rails init), model pinning, SLOs and tracing hooks.
- Data (Lucia): embeddings for merchant similarity and reranking, quality monitoring and evals for prompt regressions.

Technical notes / integration constraints (quick):
- They use Ruby on Rails; we should have a lightweight example for: server-side generation for receipts and in-store kiosks.
- Peak concurrency: estimate 2.5k reqs/min during local peaks; need to propose Dedicated sizing options and burst policies.
- Compliance: some merchants require PCI-scope minimization — clarify that Redwood Private or Dedicated with encryption/KMS can meet needs.

Outreach plan (sequence):
1) Short intro email to Priya (personalize on loyalty/ux note) + 1-pager attached (pilot idea).
2) If no reply in 5 days -> LinkedIn message referencing the email + quick stat.
3) If interest, schedule 30-min intro with Priya + Aaron.
4) Pre-call packet: one-pager, rough SLOs/latency numbers, cost estimate for 3-month pilot.
5) Pilot: 3-month pilot, scope = checkout product suggestion + receipt personalization for 50 stores.

Cold email draft (short, two variants):
Subject ideas: "Reduce checkout latency for Cobalt merchants" / "Pilot idea: faster checkout recommendations for Cobalt"
Email v1 (concise):
Hi Priya —
We built a quick idea for how Cobalt could cut checkout recommendation latency and lower per-transaction inference costs. Redwood runs inference with predictable SLOs and dedicated capacity (we also support VPC/private installs). Would you be open to a 20‑min pilot discussion next week?
— Maya

Email v2 (more context):
Priya —
Spotted Cobalt's latest loyalty work — nice stuff. A couple thoughts: many merchants struggle with recommendation latency at POS, which hurts conversion. We’ve helped similar chains reduce time-to-suggestion by ~40% using a combination of KV prefix caching + Dedicated instances during peak hours. Attached is a one-pager with a 3-month pilot outline and rough cost range. If this is interesting I can sync Aaron with a short tech Q&A.

Call prep bullets (for intro call):
- 5 min: quick intros + current pain (Priya explains).
- 10 min: demo/architecture talk (how Redwood serves low-latency recommendations: batching, kv-cache, model pinning).
- 10 min: pilot proposal + success metrics (reduction in suggestion latency, lift in add-to-cart, cost per suggestion).
- 5 min: next steps / timeline.

Objection handling (short map):
- "We don’t want vendor infra touching customer PII/PCI": explain Dedicated (isolation) and Private (VPC + on-prem if needed), KMS, and can scope data sent to inference to tokenized/non-PII fields. Offer security one-pager and SOC2 + contractual SLAs.
- "Cost is too high": present Optimize toolkit (quantization + batching) + pilot scope (50 stores) with expected unit economics and break-even. Offer to run a cost simulation on a sample dataset.
- "We already have a recommendation engine": ask where latency/cost pain points are, propose hybrid flow (local lightweight model for 90% cases + Redwood fallback for long-tail). Emphasize rollouts and A/B testing to prove ROI.
- "Integration work is heavy": show Rails + JS sample endpoints, we can provide a 2‑week integration support from Solutions Eng.

Pilot framing (metrics to measure):
- Primary: median latency for checkout suggestion (ms), conversion lift (%) at checkout.
- Secondary: tokens per suggestion (cost), infra errors/rate limit incidents, merchant satisfaction score.
- Goal: reach ≥25% latency reduction and positive ROI within 3 months.

Open questions / assumptions we must validate before call:
- Confirm whether merchant transaction payloads include PII fields we must avoid sending.
- Real expected concurrency numbers (they gave us a public traffic figure but need more granularity).
- Payment flows: in-store offline vs online — different constraints.

Quick todos (next 7 days):
- [ ] Personalize one-pager + attach quick cost ballpark (Ethan to help with numbers).
- [ ] Draft LinkedIn note and sequence (Sofia).
- [ ] Prepare Rails example snippet (Engineering quickstart PR link to attach).
- [ ] Run short security FAQ doc update for Dedicated/Private options (copy from security-and-compliance notes).

Notes to self / coaching phrases:
- Priya responds to data + concrete numbers. Keep lead with expected latency reduction and a concrete pilot scope.
- Aaron will test guardrails — have tracing/troubleshooting hooks handy and mention our Console.
- Lucia wants embeddings; bring a short example of how merchant segmentation + reranking can be done with our embeddings endpoints.

Useful snippets to copy into Confluence later:
- 3-month pilot outline (scope, acceptance criteria) — one paragraph.
- Quick integration checklist (Rails init, env vars, tracer + SLOs).

Log / activity history (auto):
- 2025-10-12: initial research note (this doc created).
- 2025-11-05: LinkedIn like thread to Priya (no reply).
- 2026-02-18: Ethan ran cost sim draft (linked).
- 2026-03-09: Priya opened email (tracking).

If we get a meeting: immediate deliverable = tailored pre-call packet (one-pager, pilot cost band, Rails snippet), schedule SRE/solutions 30-min follow-up.

Random: keep this lightweight. Move final docs to shared_drives/product/ or confluence when pilot greenlit.

--- end of scratchpad ---

source.title: Briar Ventures - ops prospect onramp journal
source.content:
Context:
- Prospect: Briar Ventures (small biopharma analytics org, ~120 engineers + data scientists). Need private deployment behind their VPC for IP protection. Interested in low-latency token generation for doc summarization and a realtime QA assistant for bench scientists.

Call notes (2026-02-18 intro call):
- Attendees: Ava (CS), Ravi (Solutions Eng), Leah (Sales), Tom R. (Briar, head of infra), Mira S. (Briar, ML lead)
- Briar pain: current in-house distill pipeline is slow (avg 1.2s/token), costs exploding on high-concurrency research queries.
- Security: Must support customer-managed KMS, VPC peering (no egress to public internet for inference traffic).
- Throughput target: ~120 concurrent sessions at peak, average request size ~220 tokens (prompt+completion), goal p95 latency < 250ms per token or approx 55–60ms/step? (we need to sanity-check).
- Availability: They want 99.9% for production inference, dev can be lower.
- Data retention: logs must be retained for 90d and optionally routed to their Splunk instance.

Technical gotchas / red flags from call:
- They use mixed-length prompts (few-shot templates + variable attachments) — need good prefix caching/KV hints.
- Their models include a custom fine-tuned LLaMA-derivative with 8-bit weights; uncertain about export format (they use custom tokenizer tweaks).
- On-prem infra: currently on GCP, strict private networking, but have spare T4s and a couple of A10s in an isolated project. Might want to re-use.
- Compliance: They asked about SOC2 + export controls for model weights — flagged to security.

Initial inference profile (Ravi rough calc):
- Avg tokens per request: prompt 70, completion 150 = 220 tokens.
- QPS target at peak: assume 120 concurrent sessions * 0.5 req/min = 60 req/min -> 1 r/s avg? (this is fuzzy; follow-up to clarify expected arrival pattern)
- Cost sensitivity: they asked about per-token vs reserved capacity. Expecting to compare Dedicated monthly vs Private self-hosted.

Model and runtime notes:
- Candidate base model: LLaMA-2-13B fine-tuned (their fork). We should confirm architecture and attention variants.
- Quantization: They asked about int8/in8/4-bit. Explain tradeoffs: 4-bit reduces memory & increases throughput but may need accuracy testing on their eval set.
- KV/prefix caching: High value — many requests reuse same preamble. Suggest enabling prefix caching on conversation templates (can save 30–50% tokens served per prompt in trials).
- Batching: Their workflow includes interactive sessions; continuous batching helps but must respect p95 latency SLOs. Propose conservative batch windows (5–10ms) initially.

Action items (owners + rough ETA):
- AVA: Draft POC plan (this doc + expected infra diagram + minimal requirements) — due 2026-02-23.
- RAVI: Validate model format compatibility (HF/ggml/llama.cpp/export) with runtime; attempt a conversion test with their sample weights (they will share) — due 2026-02-25.
- LEAH: Send pricing comparison template (hosted vs dedicated vs private) and capture their monthly spend ceiling — due 2026-02-22.
- TOM (Briar): Share small anonymized model artifact + tokenizer spec, and a sample workload log (2 days) — requested by 2026-02-21.
- SECURITY (Diego): Quick check on export control implications and KMS integration requirements — due 2026-02-26.

POC proposal (sketch):
- Stage 0 (lab): we convert model -> run on Redwood private runtime in a sandbox with synthetic traffic. Measure p95 latency and throughput; enable prefix caching and quantization A/B tests. (2 weeks)
- Stage 1 (pilot): deploy to Briar's VPC in their GCP project with a small Dedicated pool (1 A10 equiv) + fallback to host. Integrate KMS and log shipping to Splunk. (3–4 weeks)
- Stage 2 (scale): tune autoscaling policies, rollout SLO dashboard, trial 90d retention, cost monitoring.

Risk register (quick):
- Tokenization mismatch: if their tokenizer stuff is incompatible we may need custom tokenization plugin; could add 1–2 weeks.
- Model export restrictions: legal/regulatory delays could block weight transfer — coordinate with legal.
- Performance surprises: fine-tuned model might have longer generation steps due to decoder layers; plan baseline tests early.

Open questions (to ask on follow-up):
1) Precise QPS/arrival pattern at peak (burst vs steady).
2) Confirm model architecture + number of layers + kv-cache format.
3) Do they expect streaming responses for scientist UX? (they indicated yes for chat flows)
4) Which KMS provider (GCP KMS vs HashiCorp HSM) and any VPN constraints?

Meeting cadence suggestion:
- Weekly sync at Tue 10am PT for first 6 weeks. Replace with biweekly once pilot stable.

Quick reference prompts (samples they want to run in POC):
- "Summarize this protocol in 5 bullets, highlight novel reagents and potential biosafety concerns."
- "Given these 6 experiment notes, suggest three likely next experiments and estimate time-to-result."

Notes to self / informality:
- Their latency target (250ms/p95) feels aggressive for a 13B model on T4-class GPUs; call out need for A10/A100 for realistic target, or heavy quantization + caching.
- Pricing convo will likely pivot them to Dedicated reserved if sustained usage > 1M tokens/mo.
- Watch for their internal politics: infra team cares about no-eval model transfer; ML team cares about fidelity of fine-tune.

Next steps before next call (2026-02-25):
- Get model sample + tokenizer from Tom.
- Run quick conversion test (Ravi).
- Populate pricing sheet (Leah).
- Security preliminary ok (Diego).

End journal entry.

---
Version notes: 0.1 — rough capture from intro call, will formalize POC doc once model artifact validated.

Raw todo checklist (short):
- [ ] Upload model sample to secure bucket
- [ ] Run quantization pass (int8/4bit) and run perf benchmark
- [ ] Draft SOW + pilot scope
- [ ] Confirm Splunk log endpoints
- [ ] Schedule weekly sync invite

source.title: Booth Day Zero — Ops Cheatsheet
source.content:
Purpose: Day 0 rapid checklist and expense handoff for the NYC roadshow booth team. This is a lightweight, portable cheat sheet to keep on my phone and hand to the on-site lead. Not the formal runbook (see Confluence) — uses shorthand, quick contacts, and expense reminders.

Arrival + setup (high-priority)
- ETA to venue dock: 07:30 local. Aim to be at check-in desk by 07:45.
- Primary on-site lead: Maya (Maya cell: +1 646-555-0172) — meet at loading dock B.
- AV vendor pick-up window: 08:00–09:30. Contact: Nico Alvarez (BrightAV) — +1 917-555-3321, quote ref: BR-9812.
- Drayage & rigging: Event Logistics Co. on-site foreman: Karim — +1 646-555-1189. Confirm crate labels (A/B/C) and sign manifest.
- Power drops: verify 2x 20A circuits at booth grid north corner. If missing, escalate to venue ops and cc Karima (venue) + Ola.

Booth inventory quick-check (tick when done)
- 1x 10ft backwall (fabric) — unboxed at location C3
- 2x demo laptops (M-grade) + chargers — tag with asset stickers
- 1x standing monitor + VESA mount + HDMI (extra HDMI in AV kit)
- 1x ethernet switch & 1x travel router (fallback wifi)
- Misc: extension cords, gaffer tape, zip ties, printed name badges (stack A)

Expense reminders (reimbursement, don't forget)
- Overt receipts needed: courier/drayage invoices, AV day rental, booth install labor, hotel nights for core ops (Maya/Ethan), taxi/ride-share receipts.
- Snap photo of every paper receipt and upload to Ramp within 48h — tag with project code: ROADSHOW-NYC-26.
- Meal per diem: use per-diem policy (Confluence) — if team dinner > $100 check with finance before submitting.
- Cash petty: keep petty float $150 in envelope labeled ROADSHOW — receipts go in envelope and photo-upload daily.
- Expense notes to include: booth_id, date, vendor, brief purpose (e.g., "AV day rental for Day0 audio check").

Receipts filing shorthand (how I will do it)
- Folder on phone: "NYC-Roadshow-Receipts" (camera auto-upload to Drive on wifi).
- Ramp upload tags: [events, ops, roadshow, nyc] + approver: "Maya Singh".
- If billed by PO: forward vendor invoice to procurement@redwood with subject: PO-SUBMIT ROADSHOW-26.

Vendor quick rolodex (save to contacts)
- BrightAV (AV supplier) — Nico Alvarez — +1 917-555-3321 — nicob@brightav.com — BR-9812
- Event Logistics Co. (drayage) — Karim El-Sayed — +1 646-555-1189 — karim@evlogco.com — manifest ref: DRT-44NYC
- Courier (same-day) — CityExpress — dispatch +1 212-555-4410 — account manager: Priya Rao (priya@cityexpress.com)
- Catering (on-site snacks) — LocalBites — contact Sonya — +1 718-555-9023 — try to cap at $120/day.

Comms templates (copy-paste)
- To AV on arrival: "Hi Nico, we are at dock B with crate A. Can you confirm ETA for your tech and that you have HDMI and sound bar in kit BR-9812? Contact: Ethan 646-555-0109."
- To Drayage: "Karim — signing manifest now. Please hold crate C until lead confirms. Send final invoice to procurement@redwood with manifest ID DRT-44NYC."

On-call escalation (who to ping)
- Venue power/network issues: Ola Johansson (ops) — +1 415-555-6721 — slack: @ola (primary)
- Budget/expense exceptions (> $500 unplanned): Finance on-call — Priya (finance) — priya.n@redwood.com (cc: events@redwood)
- Legal/sponsor contracts questions: Raj Patel (legal) — slack: @rajp

Post-day handoff (within 24h)
- Collect all paper receipts and crate manifest; take final booth photos (3 angles); do a quick inventory.
- Create a single zip: photos + receipts + vendor invoices + final checklist. Upload to Drive folder: /shared_drives/company/events/roadshow/nyc/2026-03.
- Slack-thread summary in #events-roadshow with final costs and any vendor follow-ups (tag finance).

Open questions / to resolve before Day 0
- Confirm whether we have a backup SIM card for demo laptops (need approval to buy if not).
- Is there an on-site lockbox for overnight security? Venue contact said yes but need code process.
- Confirm who will handle tax-exempt paperwork for local catering invoice.

Notes to self:
- Keep calm. If something breaks, photo it, get vendor quote in writing, and prioritize customer-visible fixes (demo laptops, monitor) over cosmetic items.
- If you hand cash to vendor, get a signed receipt with vendor business name and phone.

Quick checklist (tick in person)

title: Booth Day Zero — Ops Cheatsheet
preview:
Purpose: Day 0 rapid checklist and expense handoff for the NYC roadshow booth team. This is a lightweight, portable cheat sheet to keep on my phone and hand to the on-site lead. Not the formal runbook (see Confluence) — uses shorthand, quick contacts, and expense reminders.

Arrival + setup (high-priority)
- ETA to venue dock: 07:30 local. Aim to be at check-in desk by 07:45.
- Primary on-site lead: Maya (Maya cell: +1 646-555-0172) — meet at loading dock B.
- AV vendor pick-up window: 08:00–09:30. Contact: Nico Alvarez (BrightAV) — +1 917-555-3321, quote ref: BR-9812.
- Drayage & rigging: Event Logistics Co. on-site foreman: Karim — +1 646-555-1189. Confirm crate labels (A/B/C) and sign manifest.
- Power drops: verify 2x 20A circuits at booth grid north corner. If missing, escalate to venue ops and cc Karima (venue) + Ola.

Booth inventory quick-check (tick when done)
- 1x 10ft backwall (fabric) — unboxed at location C3
- 2x demo laptops (M-grade) + chargers — tag with asset stickers
- 1x standing monitor + VESA mount + HDMI (extra HDMI in AV kit)
- 1x ethernet switch & 1x travel router (fallback wifi)
- Misc: extension cords, gaffer tape, zip ties, printed name badges (stack A)

Expense reminders (reimbursement, don't forget)
- Overt receipts needed: courier/drayage invoices, AV day rental, booth install labor, hotel nights for core ops (Maya/Ethan), taxi/ride-share receipts.
- Snap photo of every paper receipt and upload to Ramp within 48h — tag with project code: ROADSHOW-NYC-26.
- Meal per diem: use per-diem policy (Confluence) — if team dinner > $100 check with finance before submitting.
- Cash petty: keep petty float $150 in envelope labeled ROADSHOW — receipts go in envelope and photo-upload daily.
- Expense notes to include: booth_id, date, vendor, brief purpose (e.g., "AV day rental for Day0 audio check").

Receipts filing shorthand (how I will do it)
- Folder on phone: "NYC-Roadshow-Receipts" (camera auto-upload to Drive on wifi).
- Ramp upload tags: [events, ops, roadshow, nyc] + approver: "Maya Singh".
- If billed by PO: forward vendor invoice to procurement@redwood with subject: PO-SUBMIT ROADSHOW-26.

Vendor quick rolodex (save to contacts)
- BrightAV (AV supplier) — Nico Alvarez — +1 917-555-3321 — nicob@brightav.com — BR-9812
- Event Logistics Co. (drayage) — Karim El-Sayed — +1 646-555-1189 — karim@evlogco.com — manifest ref: DRT-44NYC
- Courier (same-day) — CityExpress — dispatch +1 212-555-4410 — account manager: Priya Rao (priya@cityexpress.com)
- Catering (on-site snacks) — LocalBites — contact Sonya — +1 718-555-9023 — try to cap at $120/day.

Comms templates (copy-paste)
- To AV on arrival: "Hi Nico, we are at dock B with crate A. Can you confirm ETA for your tech and that you have HDMI and sound bar in kit BR-9812? Contact: Ethan 646-555-0109."
- To Drayage: "Karim — signing manifest now. Please hold crate C until lead confirms. Send final invoice to procurement@redwood with manifest ID DRT-44NYC."

On-call escalation (who to ping)
- Venue power/network issues: Ola Johansson (ops) — +1 415-555-6721 — slack: @ola (primary)
- Budget/expense exceptions (> $500 unplanned): Finance on-call — Priya (finance) — priya.n@redwood.com (cc: events@redwood)
- Legal/sponsor contracts questions: Raj Patel (legal) — slack: @rajp

Post-day handoff (within 24h)
- Collect all paper receipts and crate manifest; take final booth photos (3 angles); do a quick inventory.
- Create a single zip: photos + receipts + vendor invoices + final checklist. Upload to Drive folder: /shared_drives/company/events/roadshow/nyc/2026-03.
- Slack-thread summary in #events-roadshow with final costs and any vendor follow-ups (tag finance).

Open questions / to resolve before Day 0
- Confirm whether we have a backup SIM card for demo laptops (need approval to buy if not).
- Is there an on-site lockbox for overnight security? Venue contact said yes but need code process.
- Confirm who will handle tax-exempt paperwork for local catering invoice.

Notes to self:
- Keep calm. If something breaks, photo it, get vendor quote in writing, and prioritize customer-visible fixes (demo laptops, monitor) over cosmetic items.
- If you hand cash to vendor, get a signed receipt with vendor business name and phone.

Quick checklist (tick in person)
- [ ] Meet Maya at loading dock B at 07:45
- [ ] Sign drayage manifest (Karim)
- [ ] Confirm AV kit BR-9812 delivered and tested
- [ ] Tag receipts and upload to Ramp
- [ ] Take final booth photos + upload

End — keep this on phone and hand copy to Maya on arrival.

source.title: Client-side hypotheses lab: lightweight digest
source.content:
Context
---
Personal scratchpad for a small client-side runtime that stages prompts and warmup for cost-sensitive mobile / edge apps. Purpose is to prototype an API shape + lightweight on-device heuristics that play nicely with Redwood hosted/Dedicated routing (fallthrough) and telemetry. Not a product spec — more a runnable thought-experiment.

Goals
- Keep client logic tiny and deterministic (no ML on-device if possible).
- Expose clear signals to server for routing/batching decisions.
- Let client trade latency for token-cost predictably (configurable).
- Fail-safe: server can ignore client hints.

Non-goals
- Full privacy model (assume user consents; offload privacy bits to product team).
- Complex on-device model selection or quantization.

High-level idea
- Client keeps a small "staging buffer" for a conversation (prefix + recent messages).
- When app expects user to call the LLM (user action or system-trigger), client emits a compact prewarm request: a short signature plus priority/affinity hints to Redwood ingress.
- Redwood uses the signature to prefetch KV-caches or cold-start on chosen workers, optionally apply cached prefixes, and mark the request with a warmness score.
- Client adapts fidelity (max tokens, temperature, model variant) based on prewarm result and budget policy.

API shape (sketch)
- POST /v1/client-prefetch/start
  payload: {
    client_id: "uuid-v4",
    convo_id: "c-1234",
    signature: "sha1:abcdef...",        // compact hash of stable prefix topology
    prefix_tokens: 120,                  // estimate tokens in prefix on client
    expected_out_tokens: 64,             // best guess for eventual generation length
    priority: "interactive" | "background",
    budget_tier: "low" | "standard" | "aggressive",
    context_flags: ["contains-code", "sensitive-entities"],
    local_timestamp: 1700,               // ms since client epoch (for heuristics)
    backoff_hint_ms: 250                 // how long client is willing to wait for warm response
  }
- Response: { status: "accepted", prewarm_id: "pw-5421", estimated_warmness: 0..1 }

Notes: the payload above is intentionally small. signature should be deterministic and stable for minor prompt rewrites so server cache hits better.

Client config sample (user-visible or app-config)
- client.prefetch = {
    enabled: true,
    min_prefix_tokens_for_prefetch: 40,
    aggressive_on_network: ["wifi"],
    budget_tier: "standard",
    fallback_policy: "immediate-unwarm|degrade-model|queue-and-retry"
  }

Heuristics (on-device)
- Walk conversation from the end, stop at the last user/system that exceeds min_prefix_tokens_for_prefetch. Build signature from (stable system instruction, last N user messages normalized).
- Normalize by trimming whitespace, lowercasing, remove ephemeral tokens (timestamps/emojis) via regex heuristics.
- If network is cellular and budget_tier == low -> only prefetch for priority=="interactive" AND prefix_tokens > 150.

Runtime flow ASCII diagram
- App event -> client collects prefix -> compute signature -> call /client-prefetch/start -> server accepts -> client shows spinner or continues offline.

  [App] --user action--> [Client] --prefetch(start req)--> [Ingress] --route--> [Warm pool / cache]
                               |                                            ^
                               v                                            |
                        (prewarm accepted)                              (warmness assigned)

Fallbacks and server interactions
- Server may respond with estimated_warmness ~ 0 meaning no warm available -> client can:
  - Option A: send full request immediately (unwarm path) -> higher cost/latency
  - Option B: wait up to backoff_hint_ms and poll prewarm status -> might succeed
  - Option C: degrade model (smaller variant) and continue locally
- Important: server must include an explicit header that lets client distinguish between (a) prewarm accepted (warm path in progress) and (b) prewarm rejected (no warm queueing)

Telemetry signals (compact list)
- client.prefetch.requested (labels: priority, budget_tier, prefix_tok_range)
- client.prefetch.accepted (prewarm_id, estimated_warmness bucket)
- client.prefetch.outcome (used_warm|fallback_unwarm|timed_out|degraded_model)
- client.prefetch.latency_ms (time from request->warmness decision)
- client.prefetch.cost_estimate (optional heuristic from server)

Design constraints and privacy note
- signature must be non-reversible (hash of canonicalized text + salt). We should not send raw conversation lines unless product decides OK.
- Keep prewarm metadata small to reduce telemetry and network overhead.

Edge cases / open questions
- Q: How to handle multi-device conversations (mobile + web) where signatures diverge?
  - Idea: include convo_version counter (incremented per write) so server can match best-effort and invalidate stale warm pools.
- Q: What if prefix normalization produces collisions and triggers wrong warm?
  - Use salted hash with short salt tied to tenant; collision risk low but need instrumentation to detect mis-warm hits.
- Q: Who pays for warm time? If client aggressively holds prewarms, can we throttle per-tenant?
  - Server-side admission control + client budget_tier enforcement. Also mint tokens for prewarm budget separately from generation tokens.

Prototype checklist (next steps)
- [ ] implement canonicalizer in client repo (JS + Kotlin tiny lib)
- [ ] add /client-prefetch/start handler in ingress proxy (fast path, low mem)
- [ ] create simulated warmness score in local dev and measure hit-rate with synthetic convo traces
- [ ] telemetry events + simple dashboard in Console (per-route prewarm hit/miss)

Notes to self
- keep signatures to <= 64 bytes in request id space (ingress limitations).
- think about making prewarm_id opaque; client only needs to poll status endpoint /client-prefetch/status/:id
- avoid on-device ML for now; heuristics + network signals should suffice for 80% of interactions.

Relevant links (quick)
- Jira ENG-1482 (prefetch ingress POC)
- PR runtime#879 (initial prewarm handler)

Random brain dump (short)
- Could make a hybrid "opportunistic KV warm" where server warms only KV cache (no full model load), signal to client is faster but still not ideal. Might be lower cost and higher throughput.
- Consider adding confidence bucket to estimated_warmness: low|medium|high to simplify client decisions.

-- end scratchpad --

source.title: Tactician shift — handoff cheatpad (draft)
source.content:
TL;DR: quiet night, two resurrected flaps: short latency spike on inference-gateway around 03:10 UTC (KV cache thrash), and a noisy Dedicated pool autoscaler blip that recovered after restart. Leaving a few small follow-ups + temporary mitigations.

Quick summary of what I did:
- 01:40 UTC: rolled forward a small kernel fallback patch to staging to test for OOM on rtx-40 variant (PR referenced).
- 03:12 UTC: observed 95th latency jump from 220ms -> 1.6s on inference-gateway, coincident with elevated redis misses in kv-cache. Enabled prefix-caching override (temp) and turned on route-fallback to model-variant: small-7b for high-latency sessions.
- 04:05 UTC: Dedicated autoscaler made a rapid scale-down then scale-up (suspect metric wobble from prom exporter). Restarted autoscaler-manager pod (k8s pod/restart).

Open timeline (detailed):
- 03:05 UTC - small drop in kv-cache hit ratio (from 87% -> 41%) on /v1/chat route; concurrency stable.
- 03:10 UTC - latency spike; increased queueing in gateway logs.
- 03:18 UTC - manually applied cache override: curl -X POST https://ops-api.local/cache/override -d '{"prefix_ttl":600}' (temp)
- 03:25 UTC - enabled route fallback in router: set policy for tenant-tier=free to fallback_to=small-7b when p95>500ms.
- 04:00 UTC - autoscaler-manager pod restarted (kubectl -n infra rollout restart deploy/autoscaler-manager).

What to check on next shift (priorities):
1) KV cache eviction pattern: check redis keys frequency and any churn from cleanup jobs (redis-cli --scan | grep prefix). Look at eviction metric redis_evicted_keys_total on prometheus dashboard.
2) Confirm the kernel-fallback patch rollout status (PR merged to main? see linked PR). If patch is partial, need to track until full rollout.
3) Autoscaler behavior: validate exporter metrics (node_gpu_utilization) for noisy host. If spikes persist, consider temporarily pinning affected dedicated pool to label avoid-autoscale=true.
4) Follow up on SRE-2412 (ticket) — owner Ethan; ticket contains heap dumps from the earlier OOM.

Quick commands / snippets (for common actions):
- tail gateway logs: kubectl -n gateway logs -f deploy/inference-gateway --since=2h | jq -R 'fromjson? // .'
- check redis hit ratio: curl -s http://prometheus/api/v1/query?query=kv_cache_hit_ratio | jq .
- force route fallback (temporary): curl -X POST $ROUTER_API/override -H 'Authorization: Bearer $OPS_TOKEN' -d '{"selector":"tier:free","fallback":"small-7b","until":"2026-03-11T08:00:00Z"}'
- restart autoscaler-manager: kubectl -n infra rollout restart deploy/autoscaler-manager && kubectl -n infra get pods -l app=autoscaler-manager -w

Notes-to-self / oddities: 
- saw a few requests where model returned truncated responses mid-stream; trace ids attached to SRE-2412. Suspect intermittent kv-cache desync when sequence-length skew > 2048.
- latency spike coincided with a small CI job that ran model-indexer in prod (bad timing) — check who triggered 'model-indexer/2026-03-10 03:00' in CI logs.

Outstanding action items assigned:
- Ethan: follow up on heap dumps and confirm if additional metrics are needed (linked ticket).
- Lina: validate kernel-fallback telemetry; add a short alert if fallback rate > 1% / 10m.
- Me (Aisha): prepare a short postmortem note if this repeats (note skeleton below).

Postmortem skeleton (if needed):
- Incident summary (what, when, impact)
- Root cause hypothesis (kv-cache eviction churn + model-indexer CI overlap)
- Mitigations applied (cache override, route fallback, autoscaler restart)
- Longer-term actions (pin autoscaler, improve cache telemetry, add canary window for model-indexer)

Temporary procedures to keep in the doc: (sticky; remove when permanent runbook created)
- If kv-cache hit ratio < 50% AND p95 latency > 500ms for > 5m: enable prefix override TTL=600s, notify #sre and paging list.
- If Dedicated autoscaler does unstable up/down within 10m: restart autoscaler-manager and escalate to infra on-call.
- If model OOM on rtx workers: fallback route to smaller quantized variant and open emergency issue.

Useful links and references:
- Gateway latency dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/inference-gateway/latency
- KV cache metrics: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/platform/kv-cache
- SRE ticket: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/SRE-2412
- Kernel fallback PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1834

Sticky notes / reminders:
- Keep an eye on the 'model-indexer' CI schedule — ask infra platform to gate prod-indexer runs during 00:00-06:00 UTC if risk persists.
- This doc is a draft cheatpad; convert to a formal runbook if pattern repeats (eng-serving-runtime).

Last checks before handoff:
- Alerts: none firing in last 30m (besides low-severity cache-miss spike cleared).
- Pager log: no new p1s assigned.
- Slack threads: updated #sre with summary at 04:20 UTC.

If anything breaks in next shift ping me (Aisha) in DMs or assign to SRE-2412.

-- end of notes (rough)

source.title: auth rate sleuth logbook
source.content:
Context:
- Sporadic 401s and 429s observed from mobile app (iOS release channel) calling /v1/generate and /v1/chat/completions between 2026-03-02 and 2026-03-09. Desktop and server-to-server traffic largely unaffected.
- Users report transient failures ~1-3s into a session or immediately after app background/foreground.
- Hypothesis: short-lived client key + network retries causing quota bursts, or a bug in auth cache eviction.

Raw traces / snippets (redacted keys and ids):
- Example curl that reproduces locally (simulated):
  curl -v https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer <REDACTED_KEY>" 
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
    -d '{"model":"redwood-small","input":"say hi"}'

- 401 response body seen in mobile logs:
  2026-03-05T18:21:12.482Z request_id=3a9f8b2a status=401 path=/v1/generate src=mobile-ios-1
  response: {"error":"invalid_api_key","message":"API key not found or revoked"}

- 429 response pattern from edge logs (same timeframe):
  2026-03-05T18:21:12.486Z request_id=3a9f8b2b status=429 path=/v1/generate src=edge-us-west-2
  headers: x-rate-limit-limit: 120, x-rate-limit-remaining: 0, x-rate-limit-reset: 1678047670, retry-after: 1
  response: {"error":"rate_limited","message":"Token burst"}

- Interesting timeline for a single session (mobile app):
  - t=0 app resumes from background, sets Authorization header with ephemeral client key (issued by mobile auth service)
  - t=0.2 first request -> 401 invalid_api_key
  - t=0.7 second request -> 200 OK
  - t=1.2 third request -> 429 x-rate-limit-remaining=0

Commands I ran (experiments):
- Replayed client flow from emulator, adding 100ms gap between requests: reproduced 401 then 429 sequence.
- Issued the same key from curl repeatedly with 200 concurrent connections (wrk) -> immediate 429s and tokens get blacklisted in edge-cache for ~30s.
- Rotated client API key with extremely short TTL (30s) -> observed auth backend sometimes returning 200 while edge still had old key cached, causing mismatch and 401s.
- Disabled client-side retry backoff and retried manually -> reduced 429 counts by ~40% in synthetic tests.

Logs / headers to look for in production:
- auth-service logs for key issuance and revocation around request timestamps (correlate issuance_id).
- edge cache eviction logs: keys removed or flagged as stale.
- per-token counters in quota store (redis/hll) for spikes.
- request_id correlation between 401 and subsequent 429 (same session).

Working hypotheses (ranked):
1) Token TTL + client retries combine to create temporary auth mismatch: mobile obtains a new key A, edge still cached old key B -> first request 401 (auth backend sees new key ok but edge returns 401?) Need to confirm which component returns 401.
2) Clients doing aggressive retry (no exponential backoff) create short token burst per-device that trips per-key rate-limit thresholds (especially when keys are shared per-account across sessions).
3) Edge key-validation cache bug: when key B is evicted incorrectly, edge rejects while auth-service accepts -> 401s even though auth backend would accept if bypassed.
4) Misapplied global quota enforcement: certain routes (/v1/generate) counted twice (edge + gateway) erroneously doubling rate accounting leading to premature 429.

Evidence supporting hypotheses:
- Evidence for #2: Disabling retries cuts synthetic 429 rate ~40%.
- Evidence for #1/#3: Timeline shows 401 then 200 then 429 within seconds; implies state inconsistency between components.
- Evidence for #4: Edge metrics show ~2x counting on some routes in the same timeframe (need to verify in prometheus queries).

Open questions / next tests:
- Which service returns 401? Edge/gateway or auth-service? Add explicit logging: "%component% returned 401 for token=%token_hash%".
- Add sampling that logs full header chain and token fingerprint (sha256 of key) to correlate across layers.
- Try issuing long-lived test key and replay same mobile sequence from device to see if 401 disappears.
- Temporarily enable strict retry-backoff on mobile staging to measure reduction.
- Run a controlled experiment toggling double-counting check in edge metrics to confirm hypothesis #4.

Quick reproduction scripts (for me):
- python requests sketch:
  import requests, time
  headers = {"Authorization":"Bearer <TEST_KEY>"}
  for i in range(10):
      r = requests.post("https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate", json={"model":"redwood-small","input":"x"}, headers=headers)
      print(i, r.status_code, r.headers.get('x-rate-limit-remaining'), r.text[:120])
      time.sleep(0.15)

- Node fetch with rapid retry (to simulate mobile):
  for (let i=0;i<12;i++) fetch(url,{headers}).then(r=>r.text().then(t=>console.log(i,r.status))).catch(e=>console.log('err',e));

Safety notes / things to avoid:
- Do NOT paste full API keys or JWTs into logs. Use sha256(key) or token fingerprint.
- When running high concurrency tests, coordinate with infra (avoid accidental DoS on prod edge).

Immediate action items (my todo):
- [ ] Add debug logs in edge for auth failure origin (who returned 401) — PR opened: GITHUB-PR inference/edge#782 (waiting review).
- [ ] Run controlled staging test with long-lived key (assigned to matt-r test account) — scheduled 2026-03-11.
- [ ] Patch mobile staging client to exponential backoff and test.
- [ ] Ask SRE to surface per-token counters in prometheus + trace retention for 30s windows.

Notes for handoff to oncall / SRE:
- Correlation keys: use request_id prefix 3a9f8b2* between 2026-03-05T18:20 and 18:25 UTC.
- Symptoms: 401 followed by 429 from the same session. Likely auth cache / retry amplification.
- If asked to mitigate quickly: temporarily increase per-key rate limit threshold for mobile account family or enable stricter client-side backoff via feature flag.

Misc observations / random thoughts:
- Mobile app uses a per-install ephemeral key minted by a small auth service. If that service skews TTL lower than edge cache TTL, we get stale state. Team should align TTLs or add a cache-invalidation hook when keys are rotated.
- Could consider adding a short-grace window where both old and new keys are valid for N seconds after rotation to avoid immediate 401s.

End log.

-- Jordan B.

    -H "Authorization: Bearer <REDACTED_KEY>" 
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
    -d '{"model":"redwood-small","input":"say hi"}'

- 401 response body seen in mobile logs:
  2026-03-05T18:21:12.482Z request_id=3a9f8b2a status=401 path=/v1/generate src=mobile-ios-1
  response: {"error":"invalid_api_key","message":"API key not found or revoked"}

- 429 response pattern from edge logs (same timeframe):
  2026-03-05T18:21:12.486Z request_id=3a9f8b2b status=429 path=/v1/generate src=edge-us-west-2
  headers: x-rate-limit-limit: 120, x-rate-limit-remaining: 0, x-rate-limit-reset: 1678047670, retry-after: 1
  response: {"error":"rate_limited","message":"Token burst"}

- Interesting timeline for a single session (mobile app):
  - t=0 app resumes from background, sets Authorization header with ephemeral client key (issued by mobile auth service)
  - t=0.2 first request -> 401 invalid_api_key
  - t=0.7 second request -> 200 OK
  - t=1.2 third request -> 429 x-rate-limit-remaining=0

Commands I ran (experiments):
- Replayed client flow from emulator, adding 100ms gap between requests: reproduced 401 then 429 sequence.
- Issued the same key from curl repeatedly with 200 concurrent connections (wrk) -> immediate 429s and tokens get blacklisted in edge-cache for ~30s.
- Rotated client API key with extremely short TTL (30s) -> observed auth backend sometimes returning 200 while edge still had old key cached, causing mismatch and 401s.
- Disabled client-side retry backoff and retried manually -> reduced 429 counts by ~40% in synthetic tests.

Logs / headers to look for in production:
- auth-service logs for key issuance and revocation around request timestamps (correlate issuance_id).
- edge cache eviction logs: keys removed or flagged as stale.
- per-token counters in quota store (redis/hll) for spikes.
- request_id correlation between 401 and subsequent 429 (same session).

Working hypotheses (ranked):
1) Token TTL + client retries combine to create temporary auth mismatch: mobile obtains a new key A, edge still cached old key B -> first request 401 (auth backend sees new key ok but edge returns 401?) Need to confirm which component returns 401.
2) Clients doing aggressive retry (no exponential backoff) create short token burst per-device that trips per-key rate-limit thresholds (especially when keys are shared per-account across sessions).
3) Edge key-validation cache bug: when key B is evicted incorrectly, edge rejects while auth-service accepts -> 401s even though auth backend would accept if bypassed.
4) Misapplied global quota enforcement: certain routes (/v1/generate) counted twice (edge + gateway) erroneously doubling rate accounting leading to premature 429.

Evidence supporting hypotheses:
- Evidence for #2: Disabling retries cuts synthetic 429 rate ~40%.
- Evidence for #1/#3: Timeline shows 401 then 200 then 429 within seconds; implies state inconsistency between components.
- Evidence for #4: Edge metrics show ~2x counting on some routes in the same timeframe (need to verify in prometheus queries).

Open questions / next tests:
- Which service returns 401? Edge/gateway or auth-service? Add explicit logging: "%component% returned 401 for token=%token_hash%".
- Add sampling that logs full header chain and token fingerprint (sha256 of key) to correlate across layers.
- Try issuing long-lived test key and replay same mobile sequence from device to see if 401 disappears.
- Temporarily enable strict retry-backoff on mobile staging to measure reduction.
- Run a controlled experiment toggling double-counting check in edge metrics to confirm hypothesis #4.

Quick reproduction scripts (for me):
- python requests sketch:
  import requests, time
  headers = {"Authorization":"Bearer <TEST_KEY>"}
  for i in range(10):
      r = requests.post("https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate", json={"model":"redwood-small","input":"x"}, headers=headers)
      print(i, r.status_code, r.headers.get('x-rate-limit-remaining'), r.text[:120])
      time.sleep(0.15)

- Node fetch with rapid retry (to simulate mobile):
  for (let i=0;i<12;i++) fetch(url,{headers}).then(r=>r.text().then(t=>console.log(i,r.status))).catch(e=>console.log('err',e));

Safety notes / things to avoid:
- Do NOT paste full API keys or JWTs into logs. Use sha256(key) or token fingerprint.
- When running high concurrency tests, coordinate with infra (avoid accidental DoS on prod edge).

Immediate action items (my todo):
- [ ] Add debug logs in edge for auth failure origin (who returned 401) — PR opened: GITHUB-PR inference/edge#782 (waiting review).
- [ ] Run controlled staging test with long-lived key (assigned to matt-r test account) — scheduled 2026-03-11.
- [ ] Patch mobile staging client to exponential backoff and test.
- [ ] Ask SRE to surface per-token counters in prometheus + trace retention for 30s windows.

Notes for handoff to oncall / SRE:
- Correlation keys: use request_id prefix 3a9f8b2* between 2026-03-05T18:20 and 18:25 UTC.
- Symptoms: 401 followed by 429 from the same session. Likely auth cache / retry amplification.
- If asked to mitigate quickly: temporarily increase per-key rate limit threshold for mobile account family or enable stricter client-side backoff via feature flag.

Misc observations / random thoughts:
- Mobile app uses a per-install ephemeral key minted by a small auth service. If that service skews TTL lower than edge cache TTL, we get stale state. Team should align TTLs or add a cache-invalidation hook when keys are rotated.
- Could consider adding a short-grace window where both old and new keys are valid for N seconds after rotation to avoid immediate 401s.

End log.

-- Jordan B.

source.title: Compliance docket — personal log
source.content:
Personal day-to-day compliance docket — quick brain dump and task riffing. Not polished. Use as single-source scratch to prep weekly evidence drops and vendor review notes.

Top priorities (this sprint):
- [ ] Finalize PKI export for customer-key rotation evidence (need CA chain + signing logs). Owner: me. Blocker: infra access to HSM audit path.
- [ ] Vendor questionnaires: finish AcmeVault review (responses + doc artifacts). Deadline: 2026-03-18.
- [ ] SOC2 evidence bundle v3: assemble logs, config snapshots, policy PDFs for mapper. Slack thread: #soc2-evidence.

Quick checklist for evidence items I still need:
- S3 audit exports for policy backups (path: s3://rwinf-prod-audit/soc2/2026/Q1/) — request from storage team.
- KMS/HSM access logs: last 90 days — ping Diego and infra-ticket (JIRA-SEC-4123).
- IAM role change history for service accounts used by hosted-api (run last 180d).
- Console screenshots: role bindings for private-deployments demo env (timestamped).
- Ops runbook snapshot: incident response playbook v1.2 PDF (Confluence link above).

Vendor review notes — AcmeVault (rough):
- Questionnaire status: 80% done. Missing: encryption-at-rest architecture diagram, SOC2 report redacted copy, subprocessor list.
- Risk notes: they expose telemetry endpoints to third-party telemetry vendor; need to confirm contractual data flows.
- Evidence to request: signed AUP, SOC2 Type II exec summary, recent penetration test summary.
- Next action: email vendor contact (priya to cc) and attach minimal scope template.

Small commands & queries to run when I have 15-30 mins (for quick evidence pulls):
- gcloud logging read "resource.type=gae_app AND protoPayload.methodName=\"google.appengine.v1.Instances.Create\"" --limit 100 --project rwinf-prod
- aws s3 ls s3://rwinf-prod-audit/soc2/ --recursive --human-readable --summarize | grep 2026/Q1
- curl -s "https://acmevault.example.com/.well-known/security.txt" | head -n 40  # quick posture check

Meeting notes / prep (next sync with Priya 2026-03-12):
- Agenda: evidence gaps (PKI, HSM logs), vendor status, policies to update (data-retention policy draft).
- Ask Priya: confirm which envs are in-scope for private deployment audit.
- Bring: list of outstanding Jira tickets (SEC refs), and short list of 3 blockers I need infra to unblock.

Policy TODOs (rough priorities):
1) Data retention policy v0.3 — fold in product feedback, add retention for ephemeral caches.
2) Vendor onboarding checklist — add explicit SSO binding requirement + SCA results.
3) Change management: require PR link in every change justification for infra roles.

Notes to self (random, quick):
- If infra gives HSM logs, copy raw -> s3 and then generate md5 manifest and timestamped folder.
- Keep evidence filenames stable: <control>-<artifact>-YYYYMMDD (helps mapper automation).
- Don’t forget to redact PII from penetration test PDF before attaching to bundle.

Unresolved questions / followups:
- Who owns subprocessor list for Dedicated customers? (Product or Legal?) 
- Need sample of prior auditor feedback (ask Diego, he said he has a redline PDF).
- Does Hosted API standard contract include right-to-audit clause by default? Check legal template.

Micro-tasks (15-min wins):
- [ ] Draft email template to vendors requesting SOC2 exec summary + redaction guidance.
- [ ] Create a Confluence page stub: "evidence-handling-playbook-personal" and copy checklist there.
- [ ] Quick grep of git history for config changes to auth-service since 2025-09-01.

End-of-day wrap (what I should update before closing today):
- Update Jira SEC-4123 with current blocker details and link to s3 path (if available).
- Add short note to Slack #soc2-evidence that I pushed a small batch of policy screenshots to confluence.
- Mark AcmeVault vendor questionnaire as "waiting-on-vendor" if no reply in 3 business days.

Random reminders / voice memos:
- Keep copies of everything; canonical storage is s3://rwinf-prod-audit/soc2/ — do not rely on local laptop for evidence storage.
- When in doubt, capture a screenshot + short caption (date, what it proves).

— Ava

(End of scratch docket)

source.title: compliance-backchannel-notes-kiro-2026
source.content:
Quick context:
- These are my low-fidelity backchannel thoughts as I try to turn the SOC2 scope doc into something we can actually evidence without blowing the team up. Not a formal plan — more of a map of chaos.

High-level goal:
- Define minimal repeatable evidence paths for controls touching private deployments, model hosting, and key management. Prioritize controls that require infra changes (token rotation, HSM access logging) vs docs/process (onboarding, approvals).

Assumptions:
- Private deployments will use customer KMS/HSM for key wrap (not Redwood-owned keys) in most enterprise deals.
- Dedicated pools and hosted API have different threat models; treat them separately for evidence where it matters.
- We can reuse existing SRE runbooks for incident response, but need to add proof points (who ran, when, ticket ids).

Open questions / friction points:
- KV/cache leakage: how do we prove that cached prefixes do not persist across customer contexts? Need a small test harness + logs showing eviction and client scoping. (Req: infra/serving-runtime)
- Token rotation cadence: we rotate tokens every 90 days for service accounts — need a script and proof of rotation. Where to capture? Git history + rotation runbooks? (JIRA-2457 covers rotation automation)
- HSM audit trail: customers expect signed attestation. We have HSM integration logs but not a clean export. Can we add an "export-signed" job that produces per-deployment artifacts? (Ask infra/security)
- Model artifacts and SBOM: do we store provenance for each onboarded model? Need to link model catalog id -> artifact checksum -> commit/pr that added it. (Owner: applied-ml)
- Dev access to private console: how do we differentiate developer debugging vs privileged ops? Need a short policy and jump box checklist.

Rough control mapping sketches (working):
- CC01 (change management): Git PRs + deploy audit + release notes + linear ticket ids. Evidence: PR link + CI run id + canary metric snapshot.
- AC02 (access provisioning): IAM group change ticket + approval thread + last-login logs (cloud-iam) + 2FA intact.
- ILM (log integrity): combine fluentd syslogs -> central s3 (immutable prefix) -> digest stored in KMS-wrapped metadata. Evidence: s3 manifest + kms-wrapped digest file.
- KEY-MGMT: HSM signed key wrap events + rotation job logs. Evidence: signed export + rotation runbook.

Evidence mechanics (practical):
- Use a tiny evidence-bucket-per-control (s3) with structured filenames: /control-id/YYYY-MM-DD/<artifact>.json. This avoids dumping everything into a monolithic folder.
- Each uploaded artifact should include a minimal metadata.json: {control, actor, ticket, runbook-ref, sha}. I can write a small CLI to generate these.
- For human approvals, take a screenshot + thread link + a short transcript saved under the evidence path. Not ideal but pragmatic.

Immediate actions I can do this week:
- Draft simple evidence CLI prototype (python) that: 1) uploads artifact to evidence bucket, 2) writes metadata file, 3) posts a summary to #trust-ops slack. (Will need infra creds scoped)
- Spike: KV cache eviction proof-of-concept. Minimal harness that writes unique prefixes per-tenant, forces eviction, then asserts not accessible. Capture logs and curl traces. Ticket: JIRA-2483.
- Meet w/Applied ML on model provenance mapping (Thu 11am). Ask them to export a CSV of model ids -> artifact hashes -> onboarding-prs.
- Ping infra about HSM export shape and timeframe.

Notes-to-self:
- Keep the evidence artifacts human-readable (JSON + small text attachments). Auditors hate blobs they can't inspect.
- If we can script repeatable evidence collection, we reduce friction for future audits — make this a tiny open-source helper in infra repo.
- Avoid inventing a new system: reuse object storage + existing IAM + short-lived signed URLs for reviewer access.

Potential risks + mitigations:
- Risk: collecting too much PII in logs. Mitigation: add a PII-scrub step in the evidence CLI that filters known headers and token strings.
- Risk: evidence artifacts becoming source of truth (drift). Mitigation: tie evidence to immutable commit/ticket ids and add TTL for stale artifacts with owner annotation.

Loose todo list (short):
- [ ] Prototype evidence CLI (owner: Kiro)
- [ ] Spike KV eviction harness (owner: Kiro + infra)
- [ ] Get model provenance export from applied-ml (owner: Maya)
- [ ] Draft HSM export requirement doc (owner: Ethan)
- [ ] Draft short policy for dev access to private console (owner: trust-ops)

Reference crumbs (links to follow up in real doc):
- confluence:trust-ops/soc2-scope (need to confirm control IDs)
- JIRA-2457 (token rotation automation)
- JIRA-2483 (kv eviction spike)
- GH PR: infra/pull/812 (early evidence CLI sketch)

Off-the-record heuristics (for my brain):
- Keep one simple path for auditors: if they ask for "show me you rotate keys" -> hand them the rotation runbook, the last 3 rotation artifacts, and the signed HSM digest.
- If something is too noisy (e.g., thousands of cache evictions), provide aggregated summaries + a sampling artifact, not raw logs.

End note: this is intentionally messy — I want a working checklist we can iterate on. Next step: share this with Maya & Ethan, and convert the main bullets into one Confluence page with the evidence pattern and a link to the prototype CLI repo.
