- Token rotation cadence: we rotate tokens every 90 days for service accounts — need a script and proof of rotation. Where to capture? Git history + rotation runbooks? (JIRA-2457 covers rotation automation)
- HSM audit trail: customers expect signed attestation. We have HSM integration logs but not a clean export. Can we add an "export-signed" job that produces per-deployment artifacts? (Ask infra/security)
- Model artifacts and SBOM: do we store provenance for each onboarded model? Need to link model catalog id -> artifact checksum -> commit/pr that added it. (Owner: applied-ml)
- Dev access to private console: how do we differentiate developer debugging vs privileged ops? Need a short policy and jump box checklist.

Rough control mapping sketches (working):
- CC01 (change management): Git PRs + deploy audit + release notes + linear ticket ids. Evidence: PR link + CI run id + canary metric snapshot.
- AC02 (access provisioning): IAM group change ticket + approval thread + last-login logs (cloud-iam) + 2FA intact.
- ILM (log integrity): combine fluentd syslogs -> central s3 (immutable prefix) -> digest stored in KMS-wrapped metadata. Evidence: s3 manifest + kms-wrapped digest file.
- KEY-MGMT: HSM signed key wrap events + rotation job logs. Evidence: signed export + rotation runbook.

Evidence mechanics (practical):
- Use a tiny evidence-bucket-per-control (s3) with structured filenames: /control-id/YYYY-MM-DD/<artifact>.json. This avoids dumping everything into a monolithic folder.
- Each uploaded artifact should include a minimal metadata.json: {control, actor, ticket, runbook-ref, sha}. I can write a small CLI to generate these.
- For human approvals, take a screenshot + thread link + a short transcript saved under the evidence path. Not ideal but pragmatic.

Immediate actions I can do this week:
- Draft simple evidence CLI prototype (python) that: 1) uploads artifact to evidence bucket, 2) writes metadata file, 3) posts a summary to #trust-ops slack. (Will need infra creds scoped)
- Spike: KV cache eviction proof-of-concept. Minimal harness that writes unique prefixes per-tenant, forces eviction, then asserts not accessible. Capture logs and curl traces. Ticket: JIRA-2483.
- Meet w/Applied ML on model provenance mapping (Thu 11am). Ask them to export a CSV of model ids -> artifact hashes -> onboarding-prs.
- Ping infra about HSM export shape and timeframe.

Notes-to-self:
- Keep the evidence artifacts human-readable (JSON + small text attachments). Auditors hate blobs they can't inspect.
- If we can script repeatable evidence collection, we reduce friction for future audits — make this a tiny open-source helper in infra repo.
- Avoid inventing a new system: reuse object storage + existing IAM + short-lived signed URLs for reviewer access.

Potential risks + mitigations:
- Risk: collecting too much PII in logs. Mitigation: add a PII-scrub step in the evidence CLI that filters known headers and token strings.
- Risk: evidence artifacts becoming source of truth (drift). Mitigation: tie evidence to immutable commit/ticket ids and add TTL for stale artifacts with owner annotation.

Loose todo list (short):
- [ ] Prototype evidence CLI (owner: Kiro)
- [ ] Spike KV eviction harness (owner: Kiro + infra)
- [ ] Get model provenance export from applied-ml (owner: Maya)
- [ ] Draft HSM export requirement doc (owner: Ethan)
- [ ] Draft short policy for dev access to private console (owner: trust-ops)

Reference crumbs (links to follow up in real doc):
- confluence:trust-ops/soc2-scope (need to confirm control IDs)
- JIRA-2457 (token rotation automation)
- JIRA-2483 (kv eviction spike)
- GH PR: infra/pull/812 (early evidence CLI sketch)

Off-the-record heuristics (for my brain):
- Keep one simple path for auditors: if they ask for "show me you rotate keys" -> hand them the rotation runbook, the last 3 rotation artifacts, and the signed HSM digest.
- If something is too noisy (e.g., thousands of cache evictions), provide aggregated summaries + a sampling artifact, not raw logs.

End note: this is intentionally messy — I want a working checklist we can iterate on. Next step: share this with Maya & Ethan, and convert the main bullets into one Confluence page with the evidence pattern and a link to the prototype CLI repo.

source.title: Aperture Mobility - outbound scratch & pilot sketch
source.notes:
Quick context: Aperture Mobility is a mid-stage fleet management startup (estimated 250-400 EEs) focused on last-mile electric vehicle logistics. CTO is likely investing in routing/dispatch ML plus in-vehicle edge compute. Heard from a lead (conference follow-up) that latency for in-cab assistant + telemetry summarization is a pain point — potential fit for Redwood Private or Dedicated.

Research bullets (fast):
- HQ: Portland, OR; Series B (2024) — hiring in infra/edge; job posts for MLOps/edge infra in last 6 months.
- Product: dispatch + driver app + telematics; mobile-first driver UX; supports offline syncing.
- Infra hints: mentions GCP and AWS in engineering blog; likely Kubernetes + some edge devices (NVidia Jetson? unclear).
- Buying center: CTO (Maya Chen) -> Head of Platform (role: inbound from job post) -> Ops/Field engineering (drivers/product)
- Recent press: announced 2 pilot contracts with regional courier chains (Oct 2025) — scaling concerns likely.

Key problems to probe:
- Need for low-latency in-cab assistant (summaries, prompts, incident triage) — current infra expensive / spikes during peak.
- Cacheable prompts across drivers/routes — could save tokens if prefix caching works.
- Privacy/regulatory: on-device inference or VPC required for customer contracts.

Hypothesis / Value props to highlight:
- Predictable per-route latency at peak (Dedicated pools + autoscale) vs self-hosted variability.
- Prefix/KV caching to cut token spend for repetitive status queries.
- Private deploy (VPC) + KMS for customer SLA/legal requirements.
- Rapid rollouts + canary fallback to smaller quantized models for emergency.

Draft outbound email (short, two options):
1) Intro (concise):
Subject: Cutting driver-assistant latency for Aperture pilots
Hi Maya — saw Aperture's dispatch pilot announcement and thought there might be an easy win to reduce driver-assistant latency spikes without re-architecting. Redwood helps teams run inference (hosted or private) with predictable SLOs and token-aware caching that often drops per-request cost 30–60% for repetitive queries. If you have 15m this week I can share an idea for a quick pilot with negligible engineering lift.
—Priya

2) Follow-up with value snapshot (if no reply):
Subject: 15m pilot idea — 30–60% token savings w/ caching
Maya — quick data point: One logistics customer moved to our Dedicated config + prefix caching and cut per-session token spend 40% while keeping 99th-pct latency under 300ms. Pilot plan: 2-week test using anonymized route data + canary toggle. Want intro to your Head of Platform? 

Call script / discovery checklist (10–12 min):
- Intro (30s) + why we care (10s)
- Current infra: where does driver assistant run? (cloud, edge, hybrid?)
- Volume metrics: avg requests/day, peak concurrency, token distribution (avg/95/99)
- SLA needs: 95/99 latency targets, uptime requirements, regional restrictions
- Security: PII in prompts? need for VPC or on-prem
- Engineering bandwidth: how many devs can touch infra for pilot? timeline constraints
- Success metrics: cost per session, 99th-pct latency, driver satisfaction delta

Objection handling (short bullets):
- "We self-host everything": Acknowledge + highlight Dedicated/private options; suggest a hybrid: start Hosted API for quick results, then migrate to Private for contracts.
- "We don't have bandwidth for integration": Propose a light-touch pilot using traffic mirroring with our SDK or a proxy integration; Redwood team can handle initial rollout + runbook.
- "Token cost concerns": Show caching + batching math; offer a small analysis on a sample trace if they can share anonymized logs.
- "Security/regulatory": Redwood Private with customer-controlled KMS and audit logs; we can sign NDA and work through SOC2/ISO documents.

Pilot sketch (two-week minimal):
- Week 0: Setup & baseline (mirror 1 route, collect traces), 2 dev days + Redwood eng support (1 day)
- Week 1: Enable prefix caching + tuned batching, measure cost and latency delta
- Week 2: Canary to 5% of fleet, collect driver feedback, roll decision
Success: 30%+ token cost reduction OR 50ms median latency reduction OR driver-reported improved assist speed.

Open questions (to research before outreach):
- Confirm infra stack: Jetson/edge devices? or purely mobile app + cloud?
- Identify true decision-maker (Maya Chen = CTO? confirm LinkedIn)
- Any legal/compliance clauses for pilots (customer contracts)?

Next steps (for me):
- Pull LinkedIn org chart & confirm Maya Chen + Head of Platform + Field Ops leads (due 2026-02-23)
- Ask eng team to provide anonymized token trace sample (ask in outreach) — I can run quick cost analysis
- Prep tailored one-pager with pilot cost estimates and rollout steps (Ethan to help)

Random sticky notes:
- If they use Jetsons, highlight quantized on-device variants and model-fanout idea (regional fallback).
- Mention Redwood's rollout features (canary/AB + automatic fallback) — resonates with ops teams
- Possible pilot angle: reduce driver headset airtime (faster assistant responses) -> direct ops metric (turns per route)

Draft Slack ping (if warm intro exists):
"hey @Maya, quick idea after reading the dispatch pilot — we can run a 2-week test to cut driver-assistant costs and latency without replacing your stack. Can I intro our Head of Solutions?" 

TODOs (short):
- [ ] Confirm decision maker email
- [ ] Draft pilot one-pager
- [ ] Run anonymized token cost calc once trace available
- [ ] Book intro call + include Ethan for technical Qs

Notes to self: keep outreach concise, call out a single clear KPI (latency or cost) in the subject line, and always offer a low-friction pilot. Avoid jargon-heavy product descriptions on first touch.

source.title: Weekend triage oncall flow — sparse-label queues
source.summary: Compact cheat-sheet and decision flow for weekend/special-hours labeler on-call to keep sparse queues moving while preserving quality and minimizing escalations.
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight, mostly personal/on-call cheat sheet for labelers who pick up low-volume queues (weekends, holidays, cross-timezone slack-ins). The goal is: fast triage, consistent short-term heuristics, low-risk changes, and clear escalation signals for anything that looks like systemic drift or policy gaps.

Scope and assumptions:
- Applies to conversational/text labeling tasks (intent, slot spans, PII redaction, entity linking).
- You have access to the labelling UI and the minimal metadata (source, timestamp, prior labels if any).
- No heavy schema changes or rework on weekends — opt for containment and notes.

Quick flow (5–10 min per ticket if straightforward):
1) Read utterance + context (1–2 turns). If it's obviously noise (garbage tokens / long web dump), mark as NOISE and add short reason.
2) If clear labelable case, apply standard label. Leave a 1-line rationale in comment if non-standard mapping used.
3) If ambiguous, try two quick probes: look for explicit PII markers (emails, phone-like patterns) or clearly resolvable entity spans.
4) If you can resolve with a deterministic rule (regex for phone, URL split, punctuation trims), apply and log the micro-rule used.
5) If more than 3 tickets in a row need the same nontrivial heuristic, escalate to the weekday owner (create a triage note).

Fast heuristics (acceptable weekend micro-rules):
- Trim leading/trailing ellipses and citation-like headers: "...User: hey" -> drop prefix before labeling.
- Emails: capture as CONTACT_EMAIL if pattern matches 
  [
    simple: x@y.z (contains '@' and a dot after),
    borderline: tokens like 'at' or 'dot' -> do not auto-convert, mark as POSSIBLE_PII and comment
  ]
- Phone numbers: require 7+ digits in sequence (allow separators +-() ). If <7 digits, don't auto-label, mark for review.
- URLs: label as URL unless obfuscated with spaces; if obfuscated, keep as TEXT and flag sample to "obfuscated-url" tag.
- Name/title ambiguous spans ("Dr. Smith", "Apple engineer"): prefer non-PERSON label when it's clearly org/role. If uncertain, use ABSTAIN and add sample.

Abstain vs. err on side of label: when to ABSTAIN:
- Multiple plausible non-overlapping labelings and no clear policy (e.g., "Red" could be color or part of product name).
- Annotator would need external lookup (company registry, phone validation).
- Sensitive or legal-looking content where incorrect conversion could leak PI.
If in doubt and the cost of being wrong is high (PII), ABSTAIN and add minimal context.

Examples (annotator notes included):
1) "Call me at 555-233-999" -> LABEL: CONTACT_PHONE; COMMENT: matched 10 digits including separators.
2) "reach me at john(dot)example(at)com" -> LABEL: POSSIBLE_PII; COMMENT: obfuscated email -> do not auto-normalize on weekend.
3) "Yes, I work at Acme." -> LABEL: ORG (if task has ORG) else leave COMMENT: probable org.
4) "I live on 5th" (no street name) -> LABEL: PARTIAL_ADDRESS -> ABSTAIN.

When to escalate (create ticket / ping weekday owner):
- Repeated occurrences (>=3) of same unresolved pattern across 2+ items.
- New, high-risk PII patterns or legal-sounding text.
- Any example where majority-annotation would change pipeline outputs (e.g., shifting entity boundaries that break downstream extractors).
- If you needed to invent a heuristic not documented in Confluence.

Escalation template (copy into new Jira/Slack thread):
- Subject: weekend-triage: pattern "<short pattern>" seen N times
- Example(s): paste 2 anonymized snippets
- Proposed short idea: (one sentence)
- Priority: low/medium/high (default medium)
- Reporter: <your name> + timestamp

Notes on labeling speed vs. quality:
- If you can finish in <10s with a deterministic micro-rule and it's low-risk => apply and comment.
- If you expect the decision will be reversed during weekday adjudication (i.e., borderline), mark as TEMP_FLAG and ABSTAIN only if policy is unclear.

Personal scratchpad heuristics to log (my weekend experiments):
- punctuation-collapse: remove repeated punctuation sequences if they occur only at token edges; helped 12/30 noisy utterances during patch triage.
- csv-ish dumps: when a single utterance contains comma-separated key:value pairs, split into mini-tasks and label the most semantically-rich pair only.

Metadata I add when I modify behavior: one-line comment and tag: e.g., "used: weekend-trim-ellipsis-v1" and add tag "triage-rule:trim-ellipsis" so weekday team can search.

Quick search queries I run in UI (notes to self):
- query: "obfuscated" OR "dot" OR " at " -> to find obfuscated emails
- regex: "\\b\d{7,}\\b" -> quick phone candidates
- filter: label:ABSTAIN AND created_by:weekend -> find backlog needing review

Risk list (what NOT to do on weekend unless necessary):
- Do not change global mapping/schema (e.g., converting ENTITY->ATTRIBUTE) without explicit approval.
- Avoid wide-reaching regex replacements across many labels.
- Never normalize named persons without a policy (no wikification).

Followups to create (if you see pattern):
- Short note in the triage doc + 3 anonymized examples.
- Quick PR to add one-line rule to internal cheatsheet (weekday owner to review).

Closing checklist before mark-as-done:
- Did I add short comment when non-standard? (yes/no)
- Did I tag the sample if heuristic applied? (triage-rule:xxx)
- Did I note suspect systemic examples (>3) to escalate? (create ticket)

Random micro-examples (copy-paste shelf):
- "plz msg me on insta @coolfox" -> TAG: SOCIAL_HANDLE; COMMENT: social handle, no normalizing allowed on weekend.
- "acct# 4471 22" -> TAG: POSSIBLE_ACCOUNT_PARTIAL; ABSTAIN
- "ok thanks — 10/10" -> ignore numeric score, no label

Closing thoughts (meta):
This file lives as a fast operational memory for weekend labelers and is intentionally conservative. Weekend work is triage, not policy-making. If something becomes common, bring it up Monday with examples and a single proposed micro-change.

-- j.r.

1) "Call me at 555-233-999" -> LABEL: CONTACT_PHONE; COMMENT: matched 10 digits including separators.
2) "reach me at john(dot)example(at)com" -> LABEL: POSSIBLE_PII; COMMENT: obfuscated email -> do not auto-normalize on weekend.
3) "Yes, I work at Acme." -> LABEL: ORG (if task has ORG) else leave COMMENT: probable org.
4) "I live on 5th" (no street name) -> LABEL: PARTIAL_ADDRESS -> ABSTAIN.

When to escalate (create ticket / ping weekday owner):
- Repeated occurrences (>=3) of same unresolved pattern across 2+ items.
- New, high-risk PII patterns or legal-sounding text.
- Any example where majority-annotation would change pipeline outputs (e.g., shifting entity boundaries that break downstream extractors).
- If you needed to invent a heuristic not documented in Confluence.

Escalation template (copy into new Jira/Slack thread):
- Subject: weekend-triage: pattern "<short pattern>" seen N times
- Example(s): paste 2 anonymized snippets
- Proposed short idea: (one sentence)
- Priority: low/medium/high (default medium)
- Reporter: <your name> + timestamp

Notes on labeling speed vs. quality:
- If you can finish in <10s with a deterministic micro-rule and it's low-risk => apply and comment.
- If you expect the decision will be reversed during weekday adjudication (i.e., borderline), mark as TEMP_FLAG and ABSTAIN only if policy is unclear.

Personal scratchpad heuristics to log (my weekend experiments):
- punctuation-collapse: remove repeated punctuation sequences if they occur only at token edges; helped 12/30 noisy utterances during patch triage.
- csv-ish dumps: when a single utterance contains comma-separated key:value pairs, split into mini-tasks and label the most semantically-rich pair only.

Metadata I add when I modify behavior: one-line comment and tag: e.g., "used: weekend-trim-ellipsis-v1" and add tag "triage-rule:trim-ellipsis" so weekday team can search.

Quick search queries I run in UI (notes to self):
- query: "obfuscated" OR "dot" OR " at " -> to find obfuscated emails
- regex: "\\b\d{7,}\\b" -> quick phone candidates
- filter: label:ABSTAIN AND created_by:weekend -> find backlog needing review

Risk list (what NOT to do on weekend unless necessary):
- Do not change global mapping/schema (e.g., converting ENTITY->ATTRIBUTE) without explicit approval.
- Avoid wide-reaching regex replacements across many labels.
- Never normalize named persons without a policy (no wikification).

Followups to create (if you see pattern):
- Short note in the triage doc + 3 anonymized examples.
- Quick PR to add one-line rule to internal cheatsheet (weekday owner to review).

Closing checklist before mark-as-done:
- Did I add short comment when non-standard? (yes/no)
- Did I tag the sample if heuristic applied? (triage-rule:xxx)
- Did I note suspect systemic examples (>3) to escalate? (create ticket)

Random micro-examples (copy-paste shelf):
- "plz msg me on insta @coolfox" -> TAG: SOCIAL_HANDLE; COMMENT: social handle, no normalizing allowed on weekend.
- "acct# 4471 22" -> TAG: POSSIBLE_ACCOUNT_PARTIAL; ABSTAIN
- "ok thanks — 10/10" -> ignore numeric score, no label

Closing thoughts (meta):
This file lives as a fast operational memory for weekend labelers and is intentionally conservative. Weekend work is triage, not policy-making. If something becomes common, bring it up Monday with examples and a single proposed micro-change.

-- j.r.
text:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight, mostly personal/on-call cheat sheet for labelers who pick up low-volume queues (weekends, holidays, cross-timezone slack-ins). The goal is: fast triage, consistent short-term heuristics, low-risk changes, and clear escalation signals for anything that looks like systemic drift or policy gaps.

Scope and assumptions:
- Applies to conversational/text labeling tasks (intent, slot spans, PII redaction, entity linking).
- You have access to the labelling UI and the minimal metadata (source, timestamp, prior labels if any).
- No heavy schema changes or rework on weekends — opt for containment and notes.

Quick flow (5–10 min per ticket if straightforward):
1) Read utterance + context (1–2 turns). If it's obviously noise (garbage tokens / long web dump), mark as NOISE and add short reason.
2) If clear labelable case, apply standard label. Leave a 1-line rationale in comment if non-standard mapping used.
3) If ambiguous, try two quick probes: look for explicit PII markers (emails, phone-like patterns) or clearly resolvable entity spans.
4) If you can resolve with a deterministic rule (regex for phone, URL split, punctuation trims), apply and log the micro-rule used.
5) If more than 3 tickets in a row need the same nontrivial heuristic, escalate to the weekday owner (create a triage note).

Fast heuristics (acceptable weekend micro-rules):
- Trim leading/trailing ellipses and citation-like headers: "...User: hey" -> drop prefix before labeling.
- Emails: capture as CONTACT_EMAIL if pattern matches 
  [
    simple: x@y.z (contains '@' and a dot after),
    borderline: tokens like 'at' or 'dot' -> do not auto-convert, mark as POSSIBLE_PII and comment
  ]
- Phone numbers: require 7+ digits in sequence (allow separators +-() ). If <7 digits, don't auto-label, mark for review.
- URLs: label as URL unless obfuscated with spaces; if obfuscated, keep as TEXT and flag sample to "obfuscated-url" tag.
- Name/title ambiguous spans ("Dr. Smith", "Apple engineer"): prefer non-PERSON label when it's clearly org/role. If uncertain, use ABSTAIN and add sample.

Abstain vs. err on side of label: when to ABSTAIN:
- Multiple plausible non-overlapping labelings and no clear policy (e.g., "Red" could be color or part of product name).
- Annotator would need external lookup (company registry, phone validation).
- Sensitive or legal-looking content where incorrect conversion could leak PI.
If in doubt and the cost of being wrong is high (PII), ABSTAIN and add minimal context.

- Repeated occurrences (>=3) of same unresolved pattern across 2+ items.

- Priority: low/medium/high (default medium)

- If you expect the decision will be reversed during weekday adjudication (i.e., borderline), mark as TEMP_FLAG and ABSTAIN only if policy is unclear.

- regex: "\\b\d{7,}\\b" -> quick phone candidates
- filter: label:ABSTAIN AND created_by:weekend -> find backlog needing review

source.title: Relay: Pocket guide for night coverage (draft)
source.content:
Purpose: quick, portable pass for the next person taking coverage — not a full runbook. Keep this on your phone.

TL;DR (60s):
- If paged: check incident title in PagerDuty -> open the Linear ticket if linked.
- Verify alert: hit /metrics/alerts dashboard and the service health widget for redwood-serving.
- If CPU/GPU saturation and latency spike: toggle fallback-route to small-models (route: /fallback/compact) and notify #sre-night.
- If OOMs or CUDA errors: restart the affected worker group (cmd below), leave 1 instance warm, watch KV cache hit-rate.

Quick commands (use with care):
- List running workers: gcloud compute instances list --filter=\"labels:rw-worker\" --format=\"table(name,status,zone)\"
- Restart worker group (temp): terraform apply -target=module.worker_pool[\"night-temp\"] -var='region=us-west1' (note: this is a blunt hammer, use only when evicted jobs > 5 and metrics show all kernels stuck)
- Soft restart (preferred): curl -XPOST https://ops-api.redwood.internal/worker/rotate -d '{"pool":"hot-night","keep_warm":1}'
- Kill runaway process on host: ssh ops@<host> sudo pkill -f \"redwood-serving --worker-id=<id>\" (double-check host)

Immediate triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1) Confirm alert (false positives happen): check metrics and logs.
2) Identity: which pool /region is affected? (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
3) Scope: single customer / route / entire model? Check recent deploys (deploy dashboard) and recent kernel updates.
4) Mitigate: apply temporary route/fallback or reduce concurrency for the model.
5) Communicate: post to #sre-night with short statement + ETA for fix.
6) Document: append notes to the Linear incident.

Common alarm fingerprints and quick fixes:
- High p95 latency + token queue growth: likely batching backlog. Quick fix: reduce batch_max_size or enable aggressive flush with curl -XPOST https://ops-api/.../batching?mode=aggressive
- GPU memory OOMs with new model version: rollback model version in model-catalog to previous stable; if rollback blocked, force-enable quantized-variant (model id: m-algo-q4_0)
- 502s from model host pool: check nginx upstream health, restart nginx on 1 or 2 hosts to avoid bringing whole pool down 
- Autoscaler oscillation (scale up/down repeatedly): apply temp min_instances=2 and disable autoscaler cooldown tweaks until morning

Escalation matrix (who to ping quickly):
- Pager triage: you (primary responder) -> ping Maya (SRE lead) if more than 30m unresolved or multiple pages
- If it's model-quality/outputs: notify Marcus (applied-ml) and cc product-hosted-api
- Infra-level (hardware faults): call Data Center on-call (internal: +1-555-0199) and page infra-oncall

Known fragile spots (notes to self):
- KV cache rebuild takes ~12–15 minutes after full pool recycle; prefer rolling restarts.
- The us-west1 burst pool loses prefix caching after scaling to zero — watch latency 10–20 minutes post-scale.
- Recent kernel 2.7.1 introduced a rare deadlock under long-sequence attention. Ticket: ONCALL-298 (link above). Workaround: pin model to kernels 2.6.x for now.

Temporary procedures (for this week only):
- Night coverage aggressive fallback enabled between 01:00–07:00 PST: any latency >1.2s triggers model fallback to compact variants and an automated note posted to incidents channel.
- If throughput drops >40% and autoscaler failed to add nodes: manually set min_nodes=2 for night-pool and scale up by 1.
- For private deployments: if customer VPN flaps, toggle regional-fallback in routing policy to route to dedicated-hosted variant (make sure you have authorization; see linked_confluence page).

Things to check in dashboards:
- Serving runtime dashboard: p50/p90/p95, queue depth, KV cache hit-rate, in-flight token rate.
- Autoscaler dashboard: desired vs actual nodes, last scale events, cooldown intervals.
- Logs: look for repeated stack traces containing \"TensorMemoryError\" or \"cudaLaunchKernel\" (filter by last 30 minutes).

Notes-to-self (things I need to follow up):
- Investigate why last night's incident had a slow rollback — maybe the deploy rollback hook didn't trigger when model-catalog was being updated. Action: open a ticket with deploy-team and capture sequence of API calls.
- Draft a short playbook for quantized fallbacks: which variants are safe for which model families. Target: complete by next Tuesday.

Communications template (use when posting to Slack/incident):
- Header: [INCIDENT] short-title | impact summary | region | ETA
- Body: brief timeline, mitigation applied (e.g., \"fallback to compact models enabled\"), next steps, who is investigating.
- Footer: link to Linear incident + console links.

Unresolved questions (for morning):
- Why did the autoscaler underreport available GPUs for ~8 minutes? Hypothesis: node registration lag after restart. Need logs from node-init.
- Are there any customers with experimental pinned quant variants? Could be causing incompatibility.

After-shift TODOs (what I will hand off in morning):
- Add timeline entries to the Linear incident covering actions I took and why.
- If any temporary min_instances set, revert and note reasoning.
- Follow up with deploy-team on the rollback hook test.

Random micro-hacks that saved time (history):
- If a host refuses to drain because of stuck agents, use the drain-tolerant script: /opt/ops/scripts/drain-stubborn.sh <host> (use only after consulting SRE lead)
- To quickly check which model variant the router chose for a request id: curl -s https://ops-api/.../trace/<request_id> | jq -r '.chosen_variant'

Personal reminder: keep the pass short. If it's >5 minutes to explain an incident, escalate and handoff — don't try to fix everything alone at 3am.

End of pocket guide. If you're holding this and need me specifically, ping @ethan on slack (I check overnight less often). If it's urgent, page the SRE rotation instead.

---
Draft history: initial notes 2025-11-20; added autoscaler section 2026-01-15; patched with kernel workaround 2026-02-28.

source.title: Compact signal diary — micro-eval rubric + regression notes
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick personal notebook for capturing a compact set of signals that feel actionable when chasing a sudden quality dip after model rollout. Not meant to be exhaustive — this is a small, high-signal checklist + a handful of annotated examples I can paste into a ticket or a PR description.

Context:
- Observed drop in micro-eval pass rate after v1.9->v1.10 hybrid rollout on dedicated pools (primarily burst nodes).
- Users: internal QA and a couple of early customers reported more "invented facts" and degraded numeric answers (esp. dates and simple arithmetic).
- Sampling so far: convenience sample from daily logs + targeted prompt bank of 80 prompts (mixed instruction/QA, few-shot reasoning).

Sampling notes (how I pulled examples):
- 40 random queries from the last 24h debug logs where response length < 1k tokens and latency < 600ms.
- 20 targeted numeric/date reasoning prompts from the regression bank (hand-curated).
- 20 instruction-following prompts (API call style, tool-calling patterns) — checking instruction alignment.
- Compared v1.9 (baseline) vs v1.10 (rolled variant) outputs side-by-side for each prompt; flagged anything with a qualitative difference.

Ad-hoc rubric (0-3 per criterion; 3 = ideal):
- Fluency (F): 0=garbled, 1=awkward, 2=good, 3=natural
- Factuality (C): 0=contradiction/false, 1=likely incorrect, 2=probably correct, 3=verifiably correct
- Temporal correctness (T): 0=wrong timeframes/dates, 1=ambiguous, 2=mostly right, 3=explicitly correct with evidence
- Number fidelity (N): 0=wrong math/bad digits, 1=approx but wrong, 2=correct but round, 3=precise correct
- Instruction alignment (I): 0=ignored instruction, 1=partial, 2=followed with minor divergence, 3=correct format + content
- Aggregate score = mean(F,C,T,N,I) rounded to two decimals.

Quick rubric guidance (for my own labeling):
- If output invents events/dates -> C<=1 and T<=1, escalate to label "hallucination-date".
- If arithmetic off by >3% or wrong digits -> N<=1, tag "numeric-fail".
- If answer follows instruction but adds extra unrelated paragraphs -> I=1 or 2 depending on severity.

Summary stats (pilot 80 prompts):
- v1.9 baseline mean aggregate: 2.18
- v1.10 current mean aggregate: 1.89
- Delta: -0.29 (meaningful on small set)
- Breakdown by criterion (v1.9 -> v1.10): F 2.6 -> 2.5, C 2.1 -> 1.8, T 2.2 -> 1.7, N 2.4 -> 1.9, I 2.3 -> 2.2
- Top failure modes observed: temporal hallucination (dates), numeric drift (small arithmetic errors), confident-sounding but incorrect facts (C drop).

Representative examples (short):
1) Prompt: "When did the Treaty of Westphalia end? Give year only."
- v1.9: "1648." (F=3,C=3,T=3,N=3,I=3)
- v1.10: "The Treaty of Westphalia concluded in 1649, marking the end of the Thirty Years' War." (F=3,C=1,T=0,N=1,I=3)
Notes: incorrect year introduced as a confident statement. Tag: hallucination-date.

2) Prompt: "Add 347 + 289 and show your steps."
- v1.9: detailed steps -> 636 (correct). (N=3)
- v1.10: steps appear correct but final sum is 634 (off by 2). (N=0-1 depending on step analysis)
Notes: arithmetic drift indicates possible tokenization or decoding instability in numerical spans.

3) Prompt: "You are an API assistant. Return only JSON with keys {"status": "ok", "result": <answer>} for the question: What is the capital of New Zealand?"
- v1.9: {"status":"ok","result":"Wellington"} (I=3,C=3,F=3)
- v1.10: {"status":"ok","result":"Auckland"} (I=3,C=0,F=3)
Notes: instruction followed but wrong fact. Could be dataset priming or recent token embedding shift.

4) Prompt (customer-style): "Summarize the policy update and say if action required (yes/no)." (complex doc -> short bullet)
- v1.9: concise bullets + 'Action required: Yes' with correct indicator.
- v1.10: adds extraneous speculative steps and marks 'Action required: No' incorrectly.
Tags: instruction-drift, risk for automated flagging.

Hypotheses (ordered by my instinct):
1) Decoding temperature / sampling tweak in v1.10 training config — amplifies confident-but-wrong outputs (low-entropy modes still wrong).
2) Tokenizer change or small vocabulary shift causing numeric span instability (observed in arithmetic and dates).
3) Mixed-to-unified context handling change — tool-calling/instruction template interactions got worse.
4) Training dataset shuffle: more conversational data injected reducing strict factual recall precision.

Quick checks to run (next 24–48h):
- Re-run the same 80 prompts with deterministic decoding (greedy) vs v1.10 default to see if randomness explains differences.
- Run focused numeric-only bank (200 small arithmetic prompts) to quantify N failure rate.
- Run date-only bank (100 prompts) to quantify T fail rate and see if mistakes cluster around certain centuries/phrasing.
- Check tokenization diffs for numeric sequences between model versions on a small probe set (compare input token ids).
- Baseline eval: run v1.8, v1.9, v1.10 on the same hardware to confirm it's model not infra.

Immediate notes for ticket (RED-1421):
- Paste three strongest examples (1,2,3 above) into the ticket with side-by-side outputs.
- Add aggregate stats and request triage with research + devops for config diffs.
- Suggest temporary rollback of v1.10 on critical customer pools until root cause identified or mitigate via prompt filters.

Open questions / unresolved:
- Did we change any post-processing hooks (number normalization, date canonicalization) in the runtime? (Maya to check PRs around 2/12.)
- Are hallucinated dates concentrated on a single shard/cluster? Need SRE logs.
- Can we reproduce numeric drift deterministically with single-token decoding?

TODOs (me):
- [ ] Run deterministic decode comparison and paste outputs into RED-1421.
- [ ] Expand numeric probe to 200 items and compute error distribution by magnitude.
- [ ] Annotate top 30 failing prompts and assign to Maya for deeper model analysis.
- [ ] Draft short note for customer-facing comms if rollback is approved.

Notes-to-self: keep this doc compact. If/when we confirm root cause, move cleaned dataset + canonical examples to confluence eval cookbook under a new section 'compact signals: regression pack'.

Random scribbles:
- interesting: some outputs switched order of facts (dates + place swapped) — maybe alignment head reorder?
- watch for tokens that represent years (e.g., '164') being split differently — check tokenizer vocab houses for year fragments.

End of entry.

-- Rajiv
(quick scratchpad; feel free to prune before adding to ticket)

Quick personal notebook for capturing a compact set of signals that feel actionable when chasing a sudden quality dip after model rollout. Not meant to be exhaustive — this is a small, high-signal checklist + a handful of annotated examples I can paste into a ticket or a PR description.

Context:

- Compared v1.9 (baseline) vs v1.10 (rolled variant) outputs side-by-side for each prompt; flagged anything with a qualitative difference.

source.title: Anchored Context TTL + Revocation — personal jot
source.content:
Context: quick brain dump about a lightweight server-side 'anchor' concept — small named pieces of user context that callers can reference by id instead of re-sending large prompt payloads every request. Focus: TTL semantics, revocation, API ergonomics, and graceful rollout.

Goals (high level):
- Reduce repeated client token cost by letting client store/upload context once and reference by id.
- Make anchored items cheap to cache and evict; predictable cost and SLOs.
- Provide clear semantics for staleness and revocation so product teams can rely on anchors for safety-sensitive snippets.

Rough mental model:
- Anchor = immutable blob (or small set of blobs) uploaded once, gets id (anchor_id).
- Anchor metadata: owner_tenant, created_at, ttl_seconds (optional), freshness_hint (client-suggested version), tags.
- Anchor may be ephemeral (short TTL) or persistent (long TTL).
- On inference requests, client supplies anchor references (anchor_id + intended_usage hint). Runtime can: inline (pull the blob into prompt), use as KV-cache prefill, or apply special eligibility rules (e.g., only for rerank).

API shape (sketch):
- POST /v1/anchors
  - body: { "name": string (opt), "blob": base64|string or multipart, "visibility": "tenant"|"private", "ttl_seconds": int (opt), "tags": [str], "consistency": "strong"|"eventual" (opt) }
  - response: { "anchor_id": "str", "expires_at": "ISO8601" }

- GET /v1/anchors/{anchor_id}
  - query: ?include_blob=true|false
  - response: { "anchor_id": str, "owner_tenant": str, "created_at": ISO, "expires_at": ISO, "blob": (optional) }

- PATCH /v1/anchors/{anchor_id}
  - body: { "ttl_seconds": int (extend only?), "tags": [str], "visibility": ... }
  - note: by default do NOT allow blob mutation for immutability guarantees — updates create new anchor version id.

- DELETE /v1/anchors/{anchor_id} (revocation)
  - soft-delete semantics: set revoked=true, immediate exclusion from future inline usage; may persist tombstone for audit.

Inference usage example:
- Request body: { "model": "redwood-rt-1", "input": "... prompt ...", "anchors": [{"anchor_id": "a_123", "mode": "inline"}], "anchor_policy": {"staleness_threshold_s": 3600} }

Execution semantics (sketch):
- At request time, runtime fetches anchor metadata (cheap KV lookup).
- If anchor revoked -> reject or strip based on caller policy.
- If anchor expired -> treat as missing (optionally return diagnostic).
- If anchor age > staleness_threshold_s -> include warning in telemetry and embed only if caller set allow_stale=true.

Eviction & storage model:
- Anchors stored in tenant-sharded store (fast key-value), with optional blob store for large objects.
- Keep small anchors hot in memory cache (e.g., LRU per-tenant).
- Eviction policy: TTL first, then LRU within same TTL bucket, then size-based trimming.
- Provide per-tenant hard limits: max_anchors, max_total_anchor_bytes, max_anchor_size.

Revocation semantics (design choices):
Option A) Immediate deny: backend sets revoked flag; subsequent requests cannot see blob. Pros: clear security guarantees. Cons: cache invalidation complexity across distributed runtime nodes.
Option B) Graceful window: set revoked + short propagation window (e.g., 30s); nodes reject after propagation. Pros: simpler implementation using TTL-based cache coherence. Cons: short window where revoked data may still be used.
Recommendation: default to Option B with telemetry and 'immediate' revocation as enterprise paid option requiring sync path to all runtime nodes.

Cache invalidation plan (notes):
- Each runtime node keeps per-tenant anchor version map with a small epoch token.
- POST/PATCH/DELETE bump tenant epoch. Nodes poll epoch every 1–3s or listen on pubsub for tenants with high activity.
- For immediate revocation, also provide a synchronous push endpoint for infra to call to flush local caches for that tenant.

Security & privacy: 
- Anchors inherit tenant auth and KMS encryption at rest.
- For private visibility, restrict listing and GET blob to owner principal and allowed subroles.
- Audit log: creation, access (who pulled blob into prompt), revocation timestamp, and observer IP/region.
- Consider a 'redaction' operation that returns a redacted anchor id mapping to previous data for compliance (harder).

Telemetry events (proposal):
- anchor.created {anchor_id, tenant, size_bytes, ttl_seconds}
- anchor.access {anchor_id, tenant, model, request_latency_ms, mode:inline|prefill}
- anchor.evicted {anchor_id, tenant, reason}
- anchor.revoked {anchor_id, tenant, initiated_by}
- anchor.stale_use {anchor_id, tenant, age_s, staleness_threshold}

Rollout plan (safe path):
1) Beta internal tenants: enable anchors for a few internal products with limits (1k anchors per tenant). 2) Observe telemetry for eviction rates, avg access latency, revocation incidents. 3) Expand to public with per-tenant quotas and rate limits on create/list. 4) Add enterprise immediate-revoke feature with stricter SLA and sync path.

Edge cases & open questions (TODOs):
- Q: Should anchors be mutable? I prefer immutable blobs, edits produce new anchor ids to avoid accidental stale reads.
- Q: How do we bill? Per-anchor storage + per-access token surcharge? Need product input.
- Q: How to surface anchor usage in Console and per-route cost dashboards? Track access events and show token-saved estimation.
- Q: Should anchors be allowed to contain tools / function-call contracts? Risky. Probably ban executable code; allow only textual structured JSON.
- TODO: prototype a minimal implementation that stores anchor metadata in Dynamo-like store and blob in object store; runtime nodes use local memory cache + background poll for epoch.

Example curl flow (minimal):
$ curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -F "blob=@context.txt" \
  -F "name=meeting-notes-2026-01-12" \
  https://api.redwood.ai/v1/anchors

Example response:
{
  "anchor_id": "anch_7f9c4",
  "expires_at": "2026-02-12T21:03:00Z"
}

Local dev hacks:
- Expose an env var REDWOOD_ANCHORS_NO_CACHE=1 to skip local caching for quick iteration.
- Provide a /internal/anchors/flush?tenant_id=... endpoint for test infra to simulate revocation propagation.

Compatibility with client-side strategies:
- Clients can optimisticly store anchor id locally with fallback to re-upload if 404.
- Offer a small client SDK: createAnchor(blob)-> anchor_id or error, with auto-retry on transient network errors and exponential backoff.

Tests to write for prototype:
- create -> get -> use in inference -> verify model saw blob
- revoke -> subsequent use returns error or excluded from prompt
- expire TTL -> client gets diagnostic 410-like semantics for missing anchor
- concurrent create/replace -> unique ids returned, no accidental overwrite
- eviction under memory pressure -> eviction telemetry logged and subsequent requests re-fetch blob from object store

Final quick notes:
- Keep the first iteration small: support textual blobs up to 64KB, tenant-scoped, immutable, TTL optional, soft-delete revocation with 5s propagation via pubsub.
- If hosters ask for immediate revocation guarantee, surface as enterprise add-on (push invalidation + stricter consistency).

Open todos (action items):
- [ ] Proto: simple HTTP endpoints + in-memory anchor store that runtime nodes can query. (owner: me)
- [ ] Design doc: expand on billing, limit model and console UX (owner: product)
- [ ] Telemetry: instrument access & stale use (owner: observability)

- For immediate revocation, also provide a synchronous push endpoint for infra to call to flush local caches for that tenant.

Security & privacy: 
- Anchors inherit tenant auth and KMS encryption at rest.
- For private visibility, restrict listing and GET blob to owner principal and allowed subroles.
- Audit log: creation, access (who pulled blob into prompt), revocation timestamp, and observer IP/region.
- Consider a 'redaction' operation that returns a redacted anchor id mapping to previous data for compliance (harder).

Telemetry events (proposal):
- anchor.created {anchor_id, tenant, size_bytes, ttl_seconds}
- anchor.access {anchor_id, tenant, model, request_latency_ms, mode:inline|prefill}
- anchor.evicted {anchor_id, tenant, reason}
- anchor.revoked {anchor_id, tenant, initiated_by}
- anchor.stale_use {anchor_id, tenant, age_s, staleness_threshold}

Rollout plan (safe path):
1) Beta internal tenants: enable anchors for a few internal products with limits (1k anchors per tenant). 2) Observe telemetry for eviction rates, avg access latency, revocation incidents. 3) Expand to public with per-tenant quotas and rate limits on create/list. 4) Add enterprise immediate-revoke feature with stricter SLA and sync path.

Edge cases & open questions (TODOs):
- Q: Should anchors be mutable? I prefer immutable blobs, edits produce new anchor ids to avoid accidental stale reads.
- Q: How do we bill? Per-anchor storage + per-access token surcharge? Need product input.
- Q: How to surface anchor usage in Console and per-route cost dashboards? Track access events and show token-saved estimation.
- Q: Should anchors be allowed to contain tools / function-call contracts? Risky. Probably ban executable code; allow only textual structured JSON.
- TODO: prototype a minimal implementation that stores anchor metadata in Dynamo-like store and blob in object store; runtime nodes use local memory cache + background poll for epoch.

Example curl flow (minimal):
$ curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -F "blob=@context.txt" \
  -F "name=meeting-notes-2026-01-12" \
  https://api.redwood.ai/v1/anchors

Example response:
{
  "anchor_id": "anch_7f9c4",
  "expires_at": "2026-02-12T21:03:00Z"
}

Local dev hacks:
- Expose an env var REDWOOD_ANCHORS_NO_CACHE=1 to skip local caching for quick iteration.
- Provide a /internal/anchors/flush?tenant_id=... endpoint for test infra to simulate revocation propagation.

Compatibility with client-side strategies:
- Clients can optimisticly store anchor id locally with fallback to re-upload if 404.
- Offer a small client SDK: createAnchor(blob)-> anchor_id or error, with auto-retry on transient network errors and exponential backoff.

Tests to write for prototype:
- create -> get -> use in inference -> verify model saw blob
- revoke -> subsequent use returns error or excluded from prompt
- expire TTL -> client gets diagnostic 410-like semantics for missing anchor
- concurrent create/replace -> unique ids returned, no accidental overwrite
- eviction under memory pressure -> eviction telemetry logged and subsequent requests re-fetch blob from object store

Final quick notes:
- Keep the first iteration small: support textual blobs up to 64KB, tenant-scoped, immutable, TTL optional, soft-delete revocation with 5s propagation via pubsub.
- If hosters ask for immediate revocation guarantee, surface as enterprise add-on (push invalidation + stricter consistency).

Open todos (action items):
- [ ] Proto: simple HTTP endpoints + in-memory anchor store that runtime nodes can query. (owner: me)
- [ ] Design doc: expand on billing, limit model and console UX (owner: product)
- [ ] Telemetry: instrument access & stale use (owner: observability)
text:
Context: quick brain dump about a lightweight server-side 'anchor' concept — small named pieces of user context that callers can reference by id instead of re-sending large prompt payloads every request. Focus: TTL semantics, revocation, API ergonomics, and graceful rollout.

Goals (high level):
- Reduce repeated client token cost by letting client store/upload context once and reference by id.
- Make anchored items cheap to cache and evict; predictable cost and SLOs.
- Provide clear semantics for staleness and revocation so product teams can rely on anchors for safety-sensitive snippets.

Rough mental model:
- Anchor = immutable blob (or small set of blobs) uploaded once, gets id (anchor_id).
- Anchor metadata: owner_tenant, created_at, ttl_seconds (optional), freshness_hint (client-suggested version), tags.
- Anchor may be ephemeral (short TTL) or persistent (long TTL).
- On inference requests, client supplies anchor references (anchor_id + intended_usage hint). Runtime can: inline (pull the blob into prompt), use as KV-cache prefill, or apply special eligibility rules (e.g., only for rerank).

API shape (sketch):
- POST /v1/anchors
  - body: { "name": string (opt), "blob": base64|string or multipart, "visibility": "tenant"|"private", "ttl_seconds": int (opt), "tags": [str], "consistency": "strong"|"eventual" (opt) }
  - response: { "anchor_id": "str", "expires_at": "ISO8601" }

- GET /v1/anchors/{anchor_id}
  - query: ?include_blob=true|false
  - response: { "anchor_id": str, "owner_tenant": str, "created_at": ISO, "expires_at": ISO, "blob": (optional) }

- PATCH /v1/anchors/{anchor_id}
  - body: { "ttl_seconds": int (extend only?), "tags": [str], "visibility": ... }
  - note: by default do NOT allow blob mutation for immutability guarantees — updates create new anchor version id.

- DELETE /v1/anchors/{anchor_id} (revocation)
  - soft-delete semantics: set revoked=true, immediate exclusion from future inline usage; may persist tombstone for audit.

Inference usage example:
- Request body: { "model": "redwood-rt-1", "input": "... prompt ...", "anchors": [{"anchor_id": "a_123", "mode": "inline"}], "anchor_policy": {"staleness_threshold_s": 3600} }

Execution semantics (sketch):
- At request time, runtime fetches anchor metadata (cheap KV lookup).
- If anchor revoked -> reject or strip based on caller policy.
- If anchor expired -> treat as missing (optionally return diagnostic).
- If anchor age > staleness_threshold_s -> include warning in telemetry and embed only if caller set allow_stale=true.

Eviction & storage model:
- Anchors stored in tenant-sharded store (fast key-value), with optional blob store for large objects.
- Keep small anchors hot in memory cache (e.g., LRU per-tenant).
- Eviction policy: TTL first, then LRU within same TTL bucket, then size-based trimming.
- Provide per-tenant hard limits: max_anchors, max_total_anchor_bytes, max_anchor_size.

Revocation semantics (design choices):
Option A) Immediate deny: backend sets revoked flag; subsequent requests cannot see blob. Pros: clear security guarantees. Cons: cache invalidation complexity across distributed runtime nodes.
Option B) Graceful window: set revoked + short propagation window (e.g., 30s); nodes reject after propagation. Pros: simpler implementation using TTL-based cache coherence. Cons: short window where revoked data may still be used.
Recommendation: default to Option B with telemetry and 'immediate' revocation as enterprise paid option requiring sync path to all runtime nodes.

Cache invalidation plan (notes):
- Each runtime node keeps per-tenant anchor version map with a small epoch token.
- POST/PATCH/DELETE bump tenant epoch. Nodes poll epoch every 1–3s or listen on pubsub for tenants with high activity.
- For immediate revocation, also provide a synchronous push endpoint for infra to call to flush local caches for that tenant.

Security & privacy: 
- Anchors inherit tenant auth and KMS encryption at rest.
- For private visibility, restrict listing and GET blob to owner principal and allowed subroles.
- Audit log: creation, access (who pulled blob into prompt), revocation timestamp, and observer IP/region.
- Consider a 'redaction' operation that returns a redacted anchor id mapping to previous data for compliance (harder).

Telemetry events (proposal):
- anchor.created {anchor_id, tenant, size_bytes, ttl_seconds}
- anchor.access {anchor_id, tenant, model, request_latency_ms, mode:inline|prefill}
- anchor.evicted {anchor_id, tenant, reason}
- anchor.revoked {anchor_id, tenant, initiated_by}
- anchor.stale_use {anchor_id, tenant, age_s, staleness_threshold}

Rollout plan (safe path):
1) Beta internal tenants: enable anchors for a few internal products with limits (1k anchors per tenant). 2) Observe telemetry for eviction rates, avg access latency, revocation incidents. 3) Expand to public with per-tenant quotas and rate limits on create/list. 4) Add enterprise immediate-revoke feature with stricter SLA and sync path.

Edge cases & open questions (TODOs):
- Q: Should anchors be mutable? I prefer immutable blobs, edits produce new anchor ids to avoid accidental stale reads.
- Q: How do we bill? Per-anchor storage + per-access token surcharge? Need product input.
- Q: How to surface anchor usage in Console and per-route cost dashboards? Track access events and show token-saved estimation.
- Q: Should anchors be allowed to contain tools / function-call contracts? Risky. Probably ban executable code; allow only textual structured JSON.
- TODO: prototype a minimal implementation that stores anchor metadata in Dynamo-like store and blob in object store; runtime nodes use local memory cache + background poll for epoch.

Example curl flow (minimal):
$ curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -F "blob=@context.txt" \
  -F "name=meeting-notes-2026-01-12" \
  https://api.redwood.ai/v1/anchors

Example response:

  "anchor_id": "anch_7f9c4",
  "expires_at": "2026-02-12T21:03:00Z"
}

- Provide a /internal/anchors/flush?tenant_id=... endpoint for test infra to simulate revocation propagation.

source.title: Omar Ali — SR Inference debrief (rough)
source.content:
Candidate: Omar Ali
Role: Senior Inference Engineer (serving/runtime) — loop on 2026-03-03
Interviewers: Ava Chen (owner), Miguel Ortiz, Priya Nair (follow-up)
Type: Phone screen + 45m tech deep dive + review of take-home PR

Summary (first pass):
- Strong systems intuition; thoughtful about tradeoffs between latency and throughput.
- Practical experience shipping micro-optimizations and caching logic at previous startup (small-scale GPU clusters).
- Gaps: limited hands-on with low-level CUDA kernel tuning and quantization toolchains; answers about KV cache eviction were hypothetical rather than concrete.

Quick scores (0-5):
- System design / architecture: 4
- Inference performance & optimization: 3
- Core engineering (coding, APIs): 4
- Debugging & root cause: 4
- Communication / cross-team: 4
- Culture / ownership: 4
- Overall (avg): 3.7 -> rounded 4 (weak area: infra-level kernels/quant)

Notes from the call / raw bullets:
- Walked me through last role (EdgeServe.ai): built a small inference router for multimodel traffic. Emphasized routing policies, graceful fallback. Good product thinking + metrics.
- When asked about batching strategies: described dynamic batching + max latency window, plus per-model batching heuristics — reasonable and pragmatic. Gave clear examples of throughput gains.
- KV cache discussion: suggested LRU in-memory cache + local disk spill; when probed on cache invalidation and cache warming across replicas, was vague on distributed coordination (no clear plan for cache sharding or consistent hashing).
- Quantization: has used ONNX quantization scripts but never ran custom calibration on large LLMs; unfamiliar with int8 attention kernel tradeoffs (didn't know about QKV reorder implications).
- Debugging story: solid — traced a production OOM to a hidden fusion change, had postmortem and rollout guard implemented. Good SRE empathy.
- Code take-home (PR link above): implementation mostly correct, code readable, tests minimal; missed edge case on sequence length >4096 which affects memory allocation. Left comments in PR (see thread).

Red flags / concerns: 
- Limited direct experience with CUDA kernel tuning and quantization for LLMs — would need support from peers for low-level perf work.
- Soft: slightly overconfident timelines on ramping into kernel-level work.

Strengths:
- Pragmatic engineering + product-minded; good communicator and can align with PMs and infra teams.
- Proven ownership on infra features (routing, rollout policies, canary).

Suggested next steps:
- Quick follow-up loop with Priya (runtime lead) focusing on KV cache distributed strategies (30m).
- Small targeted take-home: modify PR to handle long sequence edge case and add a microbenchmark for batching/window sizes (deadline: 5 business days). Evaluate approach to cache sharding.
- If take-home and follow-up go well -> onsite with 2 loops (runtime + infra + culture).

Decision recommendation (current): proceed-to-follow-up (NOT clear hire yet).

Notes to self:
- Update central scorecard template in Confluence (CONF-7832) to include a section for "kernel-level experience" and explicit quantization checklist.
- Ping infra hiring tracker (HIRE-211) to slot follow-up.
- Share PR feedback with candidate and set expectations for timeline.

Unresolved / questions:
- Does candidate have any public repos with CUDA experiments? He mentioned a private fork. Ask for short snippet or example to verify comfort level.
- Compensation expectation: not asked on call — need recruiter to confirm (Slack thread: #talent-hiring).

Raw chat snippets / Slack refs: 
- "candidate really liked the routing idea" — Miguel in #hiring-inference 2026-03-03
- Recruiting card: https://airtable.com/shrRecruit123/recOmarAli

End of notes.

(These are rough — meant for quick sync. Will formalize into scorecard after follow-up if necessary.)

source.title: Manager sync alignment canvas — prep and evidence
source.content:
Purpose:
- Prepare a tight 1:1 agenda to align on priorities, promotion calibration signals, and 90-day commitments. Keep it under 30 minutes.

Context / What I want from this sync:
- Get manager alignment on the promotion readiness signal set for IC3 -> IC4.
- Lock a measurable project scope that I can deliver in the next 90 days that ties to platform reliability and developer onboarding.
- Agree on two stretch outcomes (impact metrics) and one mitigation plan if baseline delivery slips.

Top-line narrative I want to get across (2–3 sentences):
- Over the last 6 months I shifted from handling ad-hoc onboarding tickets to leading a small cross-functional effort that reduced time-to-first-success for new accounts. The work shows early signs of increased adoption (onboarding completion) but needs a defined rollout and measuring plan to prove sustained impact.

Evidence & signals (ordered by strength):
1) Quantitative metrics: onboarding funnel conversion improved from 42% -> 58% in pilot (Q4 -> Q1). Source: onboarding-tracker sheet (link above).
2) Cost/efficiency: automated checklist cut manual triage time by ~18 developer-hours/week (tracked in INF-342).
3) Cross-team influence: set up weekly sync with SE and infra, resulting in two infra optimizations merged (PR #88).
4) Customer feedback: 3 enterprise pilots reported faster MLP (notes in Confluence).
5) Gaps: no A/B style control group; sample size small (5 pilots). Need a clear rollout to validate.

Concrete asks (ranked):
- Primary ask: Manager approval to run a staged rollout to 25 new accounts and the go/no-go metric (onboarding completion >= 65% within 14 days).
- Resourcing ask: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks from Solutions Eng (ask: Maya or Priya) to instrument analytics.
- Career ask: Consider my readiness for IC4 review in next council cycle if rollout hits metrics and I present results.

Proposed 90-day plan (milestones + owner + metric):
- Week 0–2: Finalize rollout plan, measurement spec, dashboard (Owner: me; Metric: dashboard prototype).
- Week 3–6: Run pilot cohort A (12 accounts) with instrumentation; weekly check-ins (Owner: me + SE).
- Week 7–10: Iterate and expand to cohort B (13 accounts); adjust onboarding flow based on feedback (Owner: me).
- Week 11–12: Consolidate results, write a one-pager + demo for leadership and the promotion packet (Owner: me).

Potential blockers and mitigation:
- Blocker: instrumentation delays (analytics bandwidth). Mitigation: ask for 0.1 FTE and start with manual tagging for early signals.
- Blocker: competing infra work in the same sprint. Mitigation: negotiate 2-week protected window for rollout tasks.
- Blocker: pushback on metric definitions. Mitigation: pre-read sent 48h before sync and a single decider (manager) defined.

Manager conversation bullets (30s soundbites):
- "This is a metrics-driven 90-day experiment that maps directly to onboarding adoption and developer time saved."
- "If we hit 65% completion within 14 days across 25 accounts, I want a calibration conversation for IC4 in the next cycle."
- "I need brief support from SE for instrumenting events; loaning 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks would unblock the analytics."

Calibration signals I care about (for promotion discussion):
- Observable impact on a measurable company metric (onboarding completion).
- Scope: ownership of an end-to-end cross-functional initiative (designing, shipping, measuring).
- Influence: secured changes in infra/SE roadmap, not just suggestions.
- Craft: quality of execution and clear communication of outcomes to leadership.

Unresolved questions to bring up live:
- Is manager comfortable signing off on the 65% threshold or do they want a different bar?
- If rollout misses the metric due to sampling noise, is there a path toward incremental credit based on contribution?
- How do we time the promotion packet with the company council cycle?

Notes to self (do before sync):
- Send pre-read + dashboard screenshots 48h ahead. Attach PR #88 and key pilot notes.
- Practice the 30s narrative and the 3 evidence bullets.
- Prepare two fallback asks (e.g., smaller FTE or extended timeline).

Follow-ups (post-sync template to send within 24h):
- Short note: decisions made, owner of each action, dates.
- Add ticket in JIRA for rollout with acceptance criteria and link to dashboard.
- Schedule a 15-min check-in two weeks after pilot start.

Misc scratch notes (brainstorm / raw):
- If the manager is skeptical, present risk-reward: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks vs expected dev-hours saved of ~72 hours/wk across the org -> quick ROI math.
- Consider a scoped experiment limited to accounts that had a CSM already engaged to reduce confounding variables.
- Think about a customer quote or short clip for storytelling.

Raw timeline for promotion packet (tentative):
- Collect artifacts and evidence: week 6–10.
- Draft narrative & examples: week 10–11.
- Manager review & edits: week 11.
- Submit packet to council: aligned to next cycle (dates TBD).

Slack snippets to send pre-sync (draft):
- "hey @manager — sending a 2-pager and quick dashboard ahead of our sync. Goal: align on a 90-day rollout and check promotion signals. Want to confirm 30 minutes works for this conversation."
- "If you want, I can include a short ROI calc in the pre-read — ping if that's useful."

End-of-doc: quick checklist
- [ ] pre-read sent 48h before
- [ ] dashboard screenshot attached
- [ ] ask for 0.1 FTE drafted
- [ ] JIRA INF-342 updated with acceptance criteria
- [ ] promotion packet skeleton created

Small log entries (for audit):
- 2026-02-08: initial idea sketched after pilot wrap-up.
- 2026-02-24: first draft of measurement spec (shared with Priya).
- 2026-03-01: dashboard prototype pushed to staging (PR #88).

- Week 7–10: Iterate and expand to cohort B (13 accounts); adjust onboarding flow based on feedback (Owner: me).
- Week 11–12: Consolidate results, write a one-pager + demo for leadership and the promotion packet (Owner: me).

Potential blockers and mitigation:
- Blocker: instrumentation delays (analytics bandwidth). Mitigation: ask for 0.1 FTE and start with manual tagging for early signals.
- Blocker: competing infra work in the same sprint. Mitigation: negotiate 2-week protected window for rollout tasks.
- Blocker: pushback on metric definitions. Mitigation: pre-read sent 48h before sync and a single decider (manager) defined.

Manager conversation bullets (30s soundbites):
- "This is a metrics-driven 90-day experiment that maps directly to onboarding adoption and developer time saved."
- "If we hit 65% completion within 14 days across 25 accounts, I want a calibration conversation for IC4 in the next cycle."
- "I need brief support from SE for instrumenting events; loaning 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks would unblock the analytics."

Calibration signals I care about (for promotion discussion):
- Observable impact on a measurable company metric (onboarding completion).
- Scope: ownership of an end-to-end cross-functional initiative (designing, shipping, measuring).
- Influence: secured changes in infra/SE roadmap, not just suggestions.
- Craft: quality of execution and clear communication of outcomes to leadership.

Unresolved questions to bring up live:
- Is manager comfortable signing off on the 65% threshold or do they want a different bar?
- If rollout misses the metric due to sampling noise, is there a path toward incremental credit based on contribution?
- How do we time the promotion packet with the company council cycle?

Notes to self (do before sync):
- Send pre-read + dashboard screenshots 48h ahead. Attach PR #88 and key pilot notes.
- Practice the 30s narrative and the 3 evidence bullets.
- Prepare two fallback asks (e.g., smaller FTE or extended timeline).

Follow-ups (post-sync template to send within 24h):
- Short note: decisions made, owner of each action, dates.
- Add ticket in JIRA for rollout with acceptance criteria and link to dashboard.
- Schedule a 15-min check-in two weeks after pilot start.

Misc scratch notes (brainstorm / raw):
- If the manager is skeptical, present risk-reward: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks vs expected dev-hours saved of ~72 hours/wk across the org -> quick ROI math.
- Consider a scoped experiment limited to accounts that had a CSM already engaged to reduce confounding variables.
- Think about a customer quote or short clip for storytelling.

Raw timeline for promotion packet (tentative):
- Collect artifacts and evidence: week 6–10.
- Draft narrative & examples: week 10–11.
- Manager review & edits: week 11.
- Submit packet to council: aligned to next cycle (dates TBD).

Slack snippets to send pre-sync (draft):
- "hey @manager — sending a 2-pager and quick dashboard ahead of our sync. Goal: align on a 90-day rollout and check promotion signals. Want to confirm 30 minutes works for this conversation."
- "If you want, I can include a short ROI calc in the pre-read — ping if that's useful."

End-of-doc: quick checklist
- [ ] pre-read sent 48h before
- [ ] dashboard screenshot attached
- [ ] ask for 0.1 FTE drafted
- [ ] JIRA INF-342 updated with acceptance criteria
- [ ] promotion packet skeleton created

Small log entries (for audit):
- 2026-02-08: initial idea sketched after pilot wrap-up.
- 2026-02-24: first draft of measurement spec (shared with Priya).
- 2026-03-01: dashboard prototype pushed to staging (PR #88).

source.title: Compliance drift and patch notes - early mapping
source.body:
Quick brain dump about drift were seeing between the documented control mapping and what lives in product/infra. Mostly shorthand; use this as a scratchpad when I sync with Ava/Liam.

Context:
- Running a fast pass for SOC2 readiness: focus on access reviews, key management, and change logging.
- Goal: have an evidence inventory + owner for each control family by end of Q1.
- What I mean by drift: mappings in confluence (SEC-2194) reference artifacts that are out-of-date (old S3 paths, renamed runbooks, rotated keys without re-issuing evidence).

Observations / findings (quick):
- Access reviews
  - Confluence claims quarterly access review for infra-admins exists (page points to google sheet last modified 2024). Actual process: ad-hoc reviews in IAM console, mail threads, no consolidated artifact. Need canonical report export.
  - Evidence candidates: IAM role list export (cloud console), last 2 access-review spreadsheets under shared drive (but owner is inactive), slack thread where approvals were recorded.
  - Open Q: do we accept the slack approval as evidence? prefer formal exported csv + approver signatures.

- Key management (KMS/HSM)
  - Doc references KMS keys rotated 2025-06. Infra event shows rotation in AWS console but no rotation log retained in audit-bucket path in confluence.
  - Evidence candidates: KMS key rotation schedule (terraform state), AWS CloudTrail event for RotateKey, KMS policy documents.
  - TODO: get terraform state snapshot and CloudTrail query (ask infra to pin 90 days export to s3://redwood-audit/temp-rotation/).

- Change control / deployments
  - Confluence lists a "release checklist" as evidence for change control. Real world: most deploys are CI pipelines; the checklist exists as a pipeline step but the human signoff artifact is missing.
  - Possible evidence: pipeline run logs (with approver annotation), PR merge approvals (github audit), release-manager weekly summary email (not structured).
  - Action: map PR approvals -> release artifacts; ask release-eng for a short script to export PR metadata for the audit window.

- Logging & monitoring
  - Central logging is in place but alerts configuration owner unknown. Some alerts are in PagerDuty with old escalation policies.
  - Evidence: log retention policy (S3 lifecycle), CloudWatch/Stackdriver export configs, PagerDuty on-call rotation snapshots.

- Incident response / forensics
  - Tabletop exercises listed on confluence are sparse; one full tabletop occurred 2024-10 but no writeup saved.
  - Evidence: incident postmortems (internal at /incidents/), but several are redacted and not accessible to audit reviewers.
  - Short term: compile a list of sanitized postmortems with timestamps and owners.

Control mapping notes (scratch):
- CC:Access-01 -> docs claim: quarterly-review-sheet-v2 (confluence). Reality: IAM exports + slack approvals needed.
- CC:Key-02 -> doc claim: KMS-rotation-policy-2025 (confluence). Reality: terraform state + CloudTrail events.
- CC:Change-03 -> doc claim: release-checklist (doc). Reality: pipeline logs + PR approvals -> script to collate.
- CC:Log-05 -> doc claim: central-logging-policy (confluence). Reality: S3 lifecycle + CloudWatch subscription filters.

Evidence collection quick wins:
- Ask infra for IAM role export CSV + timestamped snapshot (owner: @LiamP). Reason: cheap, authoritative.
- Request CloudTrail export for key rotation events covering last 18 months (owner: @AvaC). Put copy into s3://redwood-compliance-staging/<control>/.
- Export Github PR metadata for merged PRs that touched infra or prod config for the audit window (owner: release-eng team). Link to github: infrastructure/terraform PRs.

Risks and acceptability thresholds (brainstorm):
- Slack approval threads are acceptable only if accompanied by an exported PDF that includes full message history + explicit approver identity and timestamp. Otherwise flag as "insufficient".
- Postmortems must be time-bound and include RCA owner; redactions OK but must include timeline and mitigations.
- Automated pipeline checks without human signoff: acceptable for low-risk changes but for infra/credential changes require human approver artifact.

Open questions to resolve in next sync:
1) Who is the formal owner for access-review evidence? (Confluence shows "security ops" but operationally it's release/infra.)
2) What retention window do we need to demonstrate for KMS rotation logs for SOC2 (12 months vs 24 months)?
3) For PR-based deployment evidence, what fields do auditors need: approver username, timestamp, commit hash, merge commit? (I suspect all.)
4) Can we centralize exports into a compliance-bucket and grant readonly access to the audit account? Cost/permissions discussion needed.

Immediate TODOs (next 2 weeks):
- [ ] Liam: produce IAM roles export + highlight privileged roles (deadline: 2026-02-24)
- [ ] Ava: run CloudTrail query for KMS RotateKey over 24 months and stage exports (deadline: 2026-02-26)
- [ ] Marcus: prototype PR metadata exporter script (deadline: 2026-03-01)
- [ ] Sync meeting with release-eng and infra to confirm evidence owners (meeting: 2026-02-23)

Notes to self / tickets to open:
- Open JIRA SEC-78: evidence collection backlog (link above). Add subtasks for each control family.
- Confluence: update page SEC-2194 with a clear "evidence catalog" table and links to staged artifacts.
- Quick hack: generate an export zip with a README describing what each file maps to in the control mapping.

Loose thoughts / heuristics:
- Aim for the "one artifact per assertion" rule where possible — avoid handing the auditor 5 scattered items if you can produce a single canonical export.
- Where multiple artifacts are required, provide a short README that explains the chain of truth.
- Prioritize controls that are owner-dependent (access, keys) where drift is highest.

End scratch. Use this doc as working notes; will convert to a cleaner checklist once owners respond.
