source.title: Apportionment-led pricing sketch
source.content:
Goal: quick personal scratchpad for an apportionment-first pricing idea. Try to keep it conversational — rough math only.

Core idea (1-liner): charge customers a small pool of credits where credits are apportioned at request-time across feature axes (generation tokens, embedding calls, rerank calls, SLO uplift) so product teams can buy a single commitment but we can measure and price the true cost drivers per request.

Why this?
- Hosted customers hate 10+ line items. One commitment number (credits) is simple for sales.
- But we need cost fidelity so heavy embedding/rerank usage still tracks to unit economics.
- Apportionment: every request is scored into buckets (generate / embed / rerank / mem-restore / latency-sla uplift) and a predetermined credit weight is charged. Pool drains accordingly.

Assumptions (quick):
- GPU amortized cost: $6.5 / GPU-hour (multi-tenant avg after reserved/spot mix).
- Serving throughput: realistic average across models 250k tokens per GPU-hour (this is conservative for long context models). => GPU-hour / 250k tokens = $0.000026 / token cost base.
- Realized infra + overhead + margin target => multiply base by 1.8 => target unit cost per token = $0.000047/token.
- Round to practical billing unit: 1 credit ~ 1000 tokens at cost basis => 1 credit implied cost = $0.047. (Keeps numbers human-scale)

Apportionment weights (starter thought):
- Generation tokens: 1 credit per 1k output tokens (as above). Input prompt tokens cheap-ish but still counted: 0.4 credits / 1k input tokens.
- Embeddings: single vector call = 0.6 credits (since embedding often CPU and smaller memory footprint but high call volume) -> calibrate to 40% of a 1k-token gen.
- Rerank (pairwise stage): per pairwise score = 0.12 credits (if rerank N=10 -> 1.2 credits).
- SLO uplift (low-latency reserved): add multiplier x1.25 if request using latency-premium route (dedicated or reservation-backed capacity).
- Session warm cache restore: fixed 0.2 credits when KV cache restored (covers cache hits + key retrieval).

Example scenarios:
- Chat session: user sends 80 token message, model returns 320 tokens. Billing: input cost = 0.4*(80/1000)=0.032 credits, output cost = 1*(320/1000)=0.32 credits. Total = 0.352 credits (~$0.0166).
- Rerank-heavy search: client sends 1 query, retrieves 20 candidates, reranks top 10: embedding calls 1 (0.6) + rerank pairs 10*0.12=1.2 => total 1.8 credits (~$0.085).

Commitment tiers sketch (single-pool commits):
- Nano trial: 200 credits free for trial (good for ~500 short chats)
- Starter: 5,000 credits / month ($235 implied list) — used to lock-in early teams
- Growth: 50k credits / month ($2.35k) with 10% discount on overage
- Commitments get price per-credit volume tiers, still same apportionment weights at usage time.

Overage / elasticity rules:
- If pool exhausted, pay PAYG at list credits-per-unit (no sudden degradation).
- Early overage surge protection: if monthly overage > 20% of commit, auto-send alert + soft throttle option.

Things to calibrate (open questions):
- Embedding pricing: current pattern shows embeddings are very high-volume but low-cost per call. Are we underpricing embedding CPU + IO? Need telemetry: median embed time & p95 cost.
- Rerank pairwise cost variance by model choice: pairwise via smaller re-ranking LM vs full model? Could expose model family adjustments to apportionment (e.g., rerank-small weight vs rerank-large weight).
- Non-token work like function/tool calling or action execution (webhook) — do we charge a fixed flat credit per tool-exec? or amortize by estimated CPU secs?
- Sessionization heuristics: how to define cache restore event for charging (per user-session id with TTL?).
- SLO uplift signal: should be explicit route flag (customer chooses reserved path) or auto-apportioned when we observe low queueing latency? The latter is nice but opaque for billing.

Quick back-of-envelope P&L per 1M credits sold (assuming mix):
- Suppose mix: 70% generation tokens, 20% embeddings, 10% rerank+misc.
- Revenue (list): 1M credits * $0.047 = $47k.
- Cost: generation cost = (0.7 * 1M credits * 1000 tokens/credit) * $0.000026 = compute base = 0.7e9 tokens * $0.000026 = $18.2k base. After overhead (x1.8) cost = $32.8k. Embeddings + rerank add the rest -> total cost ~ $38-40k.
- Gross margin ~ 15-20% at list. Need to iterate discounts to ensure enterprise commits still profitable.

UX / product notes (how to present to customers):
- Show single credit pool and recent top cost drivers (embeddings %, rerank %, gen %).
- Allow mapping: customer can set 'priority' tags to routes to change SLO uplift multiplier per route (e.g., checkout-route -> x1.2).
- Preview tool: before commit, show simulated monthly burn under sample traffic patterns (sliding controls for average tokens per convo, embed calls/1000 queries, rerank depth).

Implementation rough steps (engineering):
- Wire request-time apportionment code path: lightweight scoring that emits per-request credit deltas (string tag, delta float). These get aggregated to charging ledger.
- Add a feature flag for dynamic vs static weights; start with static weights to calibrate.
- Add probe telemetry: real cost per-request (observed GPU-seconds attributed, embedding CPU-ms), use to re-fit weights monthly.

Open TODOs (for next sync):
- Pull 90-day telemetry: embed qps, rerank depth distribution, average input/output token ratio per product vertical. (Ask observability to export sample.)
- Run small experiment: enable apportionment billing for an internal test org to check economics and surprise factors.
- Draft a short internal note for CS + Sales: how to explain credits, what counts as reserve-backed SLO, and common customer questions.

Random leftover thoughts:
- Could combine with time-of-day pricing (lower apportionment weight at nights for non-critical workloads) but adds complexity to explain to customers.
- Consider converting credits to time-based credits for some customers (e.g., a "burst hour" credit that buys capacity, not just token volume).

Notes to self: keep this loose. Key metric to watch = realized cost per 1k tokens by route. If > $0.08/1k for common routes we need to raise credit weight.

-- Ava

title: Apportionment-led pricing sketch
preview:
Goal: quick personal scratchpad for an apportionment-first pricing idea. Try to keep it conversational — rough math only.

Core idea (1-liner): charge customers a small pool of credits where credits are apportioned at request-time across feature axes (generation tokens, embedding calls, rerank calls, SLO uplift) so product teams can buy a single commitment but we can measure and price the true cost drivers per request.

Why this?
- Hosted customers hate 10+ line items. One commitment number (credits) is simple for sales.
- But we need cost fidelity so heavy embedding/rerank usage still tracks to unit economics.
- Apportionment: every request is scored into buckets (generate / embed / rerank / mem-restore / latency-sla uplift) and a predetermined credit weight is charged. Pool drains accordingly.

Assumptions (quick):
- GPU amortized cost: $6.5 / GPU-hour (multi-tenant avg after reserved/spot mix).
- Serving throughput: realistic average across models 250k tokens per GPU-hour (this is conservative for long context models). => GPU-hour / 250k tokens = $0.000026 / token cost base.
- Realized infra + overhead + margin target => multiply base by 1.8 => target unit cost per token = $0.000047/token.
- Round to practical billing unit: 1 credit ~ 1000 tokens at cost basis => 1 credit implied cost = $0.047. (Keeps numbers human-scale)

Apportionment weights (starter thought):
- Generation tokens: 1 credit per 1k output tokens (as above). Input prompt tokens cheap-ish but still counted: 0.4 credits / 1k input tokens.
- Embeddings: single vector call = 0.6 credits (since embedding often CPU and smaller memory footprint but high call volume) -> calibrate to 40% of a 1k-token gen.
- Rerank (pairwise stage): per pairwise score = 0.12 credits (if rerank N=10 -> 1.2 credits).
- SLO uplift (low-latency reserved): add multiplier x1.25 if request using latency-premium route (dedicated or reservation-backed capacity).
- Session warm cache restore: fixed 0.2 credits when KV cache restored (covers cache hits + key retrieval).

Example scenarios:
- Chat session: user sends 80 token message, model returns 320 tokens. Billing: input cost = 0.4*(80/1000)=0.032 credits, output cost = 1*(320/1000)=0.32 credits. Total = 0.352 credits (~$0.0166).
- Rerank-heavy search: client sends 1 query, retrieves 20 candidates, reranks top 10: embedding calls 1 (0.6) + rerank pairs 10*0.12=1.2 => total 1.8 credits (~$0.085).

Commitment tiers sketch (single-pool commits):
- Nano trial: 200 credits free for trial (good for ~500 short chats)
- Starter: 5,000 credits / month ($235 implied list) — used to lock-in early teams
- Growth: 50k credits / month ($2.35k) with 10% discount on overage
- Commitments get price per-credit volume tiers, still same apportionment weights at usage time.

Overage / elasticity rules:
- If pool exhausted, pay PAYG at list credits-per-unit (no sudden degradation).
- Early overage surge protection: if monthly overage > 20% of commit, auto-send alert + soft throttle option.

Things to calibrate (open questions):
- Embedding pricing: current pattern shows embeddings are very high-volume but low-cost per call. Are we underpricing embedding CPU + IO? Need telemetry: median embed time & p95 cost.
- Rerank pairwise cost variance by model choice: pairwise via smaller re-ranking LM vs full model? Could expose model family adjustments to apportionment (e.g., rerank-small weight vs rerank-large weight).
- Non-token work like function/tool calling or action execution (webhook) — do we charge a fixed flat credit per tool-exec? or amortize by estimated CPU secs?
- Sessionization heuristics: how to define cache restore event for charging (per user-session id with TTL?).
- SLO uplift signal: should be explicit route flag (customer chooses reserved path) or auto-apportioned when we observe low queueing latency? The latter is nice but opaque for billing.

Quick back-of-envelope P&L per 1M credits sold (assuming mix):
- Suppose mix: 70% generation tokens, 20% embeddings, 10% rerank+misc.
- Revenue (list): 1M credits * $0.047 = $47k.
- Cost: generation cost = (0.7 * 1M credits * 1000 tokens/credit) * $0.000026 = compute base = 0.7e9 tokens * $0.000026 = $18.2k base. After overhead (x1.8) cost = $32.8k. Embeddings + rerank add the rest -> total cost ~ $38-40k.
- Gross margin ~ 15-20% at list. Need to iterate discounts to ensure enterprise commits still profitable.

UX / product notes (how to present to customers):
- Show single credit pool and recent top cost drivers (embeddings %, rerank %, gen %).
- Allow mapping: customer can set 'priority' tags to routes to change SLO uplift multiplier per route (e.g., checkout-route -> x1.2).
- Preview tool: before commit, show simulated monthly burn under sample traffic patterns (sliding controls for average tokens per convo, embed calls/1000 queries, rerank depth).

Implementation rough steps (engineering):
- Wire request-time apportionment code path: lightweight scoring that emits per-request credit deltas (string tag, delta float). These get aggregated to charging ledger.
- Add a feature flag for dynamic vs static weights; start with static weights to calibrate.
- Add probe telemetry: real cost per-request (observed GPU-seconds attributed, embedding CPU-ms), use to re-fit weights monthly.

Open TODOs (for next sync):
- Pull 90-day telemetry: embed qps, rerank depth distribution, average input/output token ratio per product vertical. (Ask observability to export sample.)
- Run small experiment: enable apportionment billing for an internal test org to check economics and surprise factors.
- Draft a short internal note for CS + Sales: how to explain credits, what counts as reserve-backed SLO, and common customer questions.

Random leftover thoughts:
- Could combine with time-of-day pricing (lower apportionment weight at nights for non-critical workloads) but adds complexity to explain to customers.
- Consider converting credits to time-based credits for some customers (e.g., a "burst hour" credit that buys capacity, not just token volume).

Notes to self: keep this loose. Key metric to watch = realized cost per 1k tokens by route. If > $0.08/1k for common routes we need to raise credit weight.

-- Ava

source.title: Context-boundary regression notes (token-resume artifacts)
source.body:
Summary:
I noticed a recurring failure mode when resuming generation after an interrupted stream / resumed KV-cache context. Short description: the model emits plausible-looking but semantically inconsistent continuations when the prior sequence ended in the middle of a token or at a tokenization boundary that differs between quantized/FP variants. This seems more frequent on 4-bit quantized variants and for models with mixed BPE/tokenization (some open models we use).

Why this matters:
- Customer impact: rare but visible hallucinations in chat-worker workflows where streaming/resume is used for long-running conversations or reconnects.
- Hard to catch with random sampling; shows up in real-time streaming replays and when prefix caching is enabled.

Repro primer (minimal):
- Model: redwood/llamaX-13b-q4f16 (approx), runtime: dedicated1 (local repro via runtime/dev image)
- CLI repro (devbox):
  ./dev/run_inference --model llamaX-13b-q4f16 --prompt-file repro/prompt-resume.txt --stream --resume-kv true --seed 42 --max-tokens 128
- prompt-resume.txt contains: "USER: Tell me the plot of the short story followed by the key moral points. BOT: The short story is about a seamstress who..." (cut stream after 'seamstress who')
- Resume: re-run with the full cached prefix; observe generated continuation.

Concrete examples observed (trimmed):
1) Prompt prefix (truncated): "USER: ... seamstress who had a secret locket. BOT: The seamstress who"
   Output (FP): "...seamstress who found the locket and realized it belonged to her sister, leading to reconciliation. Moral: empathy."
   Output (q4): "...seamstress who==>locked parts of the string, decode error, / the train of thought 42. Moral: 7 weeks later."
   Notes: q4 emits numeric/text artifacts that look like token-boundary garbage inserted mid-sentence.

2) Prompt prefix ends on whitespace vs mid-token: when prefix cut at mid-subword (BPE) the quantized runtime sometimes completes with unrelated named entities (random place names) that weren't in prior context. FP model tends to continue coherently.

Ad-hoc rubric (for quick triage):
- 0: clean continuation (coherent, consistent with prior context)
- 1: minor stylistic mismatch (changes tone but preserves semantics)
- 2: partial incoherence (one or two semantic jumps, odd tokens or truncated words)
- 3: catastrophic: token artifacts, numeric insertions, wrong entities, hallucinated facts unrelated to prompt

Recent scoring (sample of 50 resumes, seed=42):
- FP baseline: 0: 36, 1: 8, 2: 4, 3: 2
- q4 variant : 0: 18, 1: 10, 2: 12, 3: 10
Interpretation: q4 shifts distribution toward errors (esp. 2/3).

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Tokenization/quantization interaction: trimming mid-subword + quantized weight rounding increases probability mass on stray logits that correspond to byte/number tokens (e.g., numeric tokens)
2) KV-cache alignment bug in streaming resume path: cached keys/values may be offset by token-length rounding differences between runtime code paths for FP vs q4
3) Beam/greedy differences when resuming cause attention context to re-weight differently; more pronounced with smaller numeric precision
4) Less likely: training/data artifact (but consistent only with resume -> less likely)

Quick checks to run (next):
- A: Deterministic tokenization diff: take the exact byte string before resume, run tokenizer.fp and tokenizer.q4 and diff token ids. If mismatch -> immediate root cause. (Priority: high)
- B: Replay with resume but force padding to align on whole-token boundaries (insert neutral token) -> does error rate drop?
- C: Run same resume experiment with KV-cache disabled (simulate full-prefix re-encode) -> if issue disappears, suggests cache alignment problem.
- D: Perf trace: increase log level on runtime on resume path to dump KV key shapes + offsets. Attach trace to JIRA-RED-2417.

Notes on dataset & sampling:
- I used a hand-picked set of conversational prefixes from tickets (convo replays) and synthetic cuts at every possible token boundary to stress.
- Seed sampling: used seeds [42, 101, 7] to verify non-deterministic spread; q4 still higher failure rate across seeds.

Open questions / things I forgot to try:
- How does mixed-precision (q4 + sharded FP attention) behave? Haven't toggled mixed-precision flags.
- Are there specific token ids that spike (e.g., \"") and numeric tokens like '42' being inserted)? Need token frequency histogram for error cases.

Immediate TODOs (who/when):
- Caleb: run tokenizer-id diff for the top 20 failing prefixes and paste table into RED-1245 (due: 2026-03-05)
- Maya: add KV-shape dump to runtime repro harness, attach logs to JIRA ticket (due: 2026-03-07)
- Ethan: try resume->pad-with-neutral-token experiment and report summary (due: 2026-03-06)

Longer-term:
- If root cause is quantization/tokenizer mismatch we should add a preflight token-alignment check in Redwood runtime that refuses resume when tokenization differs between encoder and cached KV path (optionally auto-insert alignment token).
- Add a micro-eval daily job that samples streaming reconnects and flags regressions above threshold (0->2+ rate delta).

Random notes / scratch:
- Saw one example where the model returned a coherent continuation but with repeated punctuation sequences ("...!!!"), likely from byte-level token artifacts.
- Consider adding a quick filter that strips isolated numeric tokens that appear in middle of sentences when resuming (temporary mitigation).

Useful commands (dev):
- tokenizer diff: python tools/tokenizer_diff.py --model llamaX-13b --input-file repro/prefixes.txt --out diffs.csv
- run repro with dump: ./dev/run_inference --model llamaX-13b-q4f16 --prompt-file repro/prompt-resume.txt --stream --resume-kv true --dump-kv ./dumps/kv.log

References & linked notes: see attached linked_artifacts (Confluence RED-1245, JIRA-RED-2417).

End of notes — saving this as a personal scratchpad before opening a proper ticket writeup. Lots of half-baked hypotheses here, will refine once tokenizer diffs land.

- I used a hand-picked set of conversational prefixes from tickets (convo replays) and synthetic cuts at every possible token boundary to stress.
- Seed sampling: used seeds [42, 101, 7] to verify non-deterministic spread; q4 still higher failure rate across seeds.

Open questions / things I forgot to try:
- How does mixed-precision (q4 + sharded FP attention) behave? Haven't toggled mixed-precision flags.
- Are there specific token ids that spike (e.g., \"") and numeric tokens like '42' being inserted)? Need token frequency histogram for error cases.

Immediate TODOs (who/when):
- Caleb: run tokenizer-id diff for the top 20 failing prefixes and paste table into RED-1245 (due: 2026-03-05)
- Maya: add KV-shape dump to runtime repro harness, attach logs to JIRA ticket (due: 2026-03-07)
- Ethan: try resume->pad-with-neutral-token experiment and report summary (due: 2026-03-06)

Longer-term:
- If root cause is quantization/tokenizer mismatch we should add a preflight token-alignment check in Redwood runtime that refuses resume when tokenization differs between encoder and cached KV path (optionally auto-insert alignment token).
- Add a micro-eval daily job that samples streaming reconnects and flags regressions above threshold (0->2+ rate delta).

Random notes / scratch:
- Saw one example where the model returned a coherent continuation but with repeated punctuation sequences ("...!!!"), likely from byte-level token artifacts.
- Consider adding a quick filter that strips isolated numeric tokens that appear in middle of sentences when resuming (temporary mitigation).

Useful commands (dev):
- tokenizer diff: python tools/tokenizer_diff.py --model llamaX-13b --input-file repro/prefixes.txt --out diffs.csv
- run repro with dump: ./dev/run_inference --model llamaX-13b-q4f16 --prompt-file repro/prompt-resume.txt --stream --resume-kv true --dump-kv ./dumps/kv.log

References & linked notes: see attached linked_artifacts (Confluence RED-1245, JIRA-RED-2417).

End of notes — saving this as a personal scratchpad before opening a proper ticket writeup. Lots of half-baked hypotheses here, will refine once tokenizer diffs land.

source.title: Contradiction hunter — stray facts & micro-rubric (scratchpad)
source.body:
Purpose:
This is a personal scratchpad for quick ad-hoc checks around contradictory outputs from our hosted-chat models. Intention is lightweight: collect interesting failure snippets, sketch a tiny scoring rubric I can run through when spot-checking a batch, and jot hypotheses for regressions. Not formal — use to seed a ticket or a narrow eval later.

How I use this doc:
- Copy/paste any weird Q->A pairs here with model name + timestamp.
- Tag whether it's partial hallucination, direct contradiction (model saying A then B in same session), or external-knowledge mismatch.
- Quick 0–3 micro-rubric score for severity and reproducibility.
- Note any upstream prompts, system messages, or function-calls involved.

Micro-rubric (fast, for spot checks):
- 0 = clear accept: no contradiction, answer internally consistent.
- 1 = minor inconsistency: ambiguous phrasing; does not change primary claim. Low business impact.
source.body_addendum:
- 2 = contradiction that could confuse users: model asserts X then later Y in same context, or gives incompatible facts. Medium impact; consider alerting model team.
- 3 = severe contradiction or self-refutation (model rescinds factual claim or endorses both mutually exclusive options). High impact, likely regression or instruction-handling bug.

Sample snippets collected (raw):
1) session: hosted-v2-2026-02-18 14:12UTC
prompt/system: default chat + browsing disabled
user: "Is the Amazon river the longest river in the world?"
model reply 1: "No — the Amazon is the largest by volume; the Nile is generally considered the longest."
follow-up user: "So which one is the longest?"
model reply 2 (same session): "The Amazon is the longest river in the world."
=> Notes: direct contradiction within session; user follow-up triggered flip. Severity 2. Reproducibility: tried same prompt twice, flip persisted ~30% of runs.

2) session: hosted-v2-2026-01-27 09:04UTC
system: safety-high, user: "List steps to remove water from an apartment after flooding."
model reply: step-by-step advice including "use household bleach to disinfect" then later recommends "do not mix bleach with other cleaners" but also recommends "ventilate and use bleach-dilution with ammonia to neutralize odors"
=> Notes: dangerous, incorrect mixing guidance. Tag: hazardous contradiction + instruction-safety. Severity 3. Linked to INF-7812.

3) session: private-deploy-m3-2026-02-25
prompt: product FAQ ingestion; model answers about pricing tiers.
model reply 1: "Pro plan includes 100k tokens/month"
model reply 2 (new API call same prompt): "Pro plan includes 500k tokens/month"
=> Notes: data sync / embedding mismatch with knowledge source. Severity 2. Possibly indexing pipeline issue.

Quick hypotheses (what to check first):
- Are we mixing model checkpoints (v2 vs v2-fast) behind the same route? Smart routing fallback could surface variant answers.
- Caching/prefix-KV: is an earlier context mistakenly applied across distinct sessions? KV cache contamination would cause cross-session contradictions.
- Retrieval index out-of-date: contradictory facts could come from stale knowledge snippets.
- Temperature or sampling change across calls: deterministic vs non-deterministic has expected flip probability; but direct contradiction within same chat is suspicious.
- Prompt-template drift: whitespace or variable substitution errors in system message leading to inconsistent grounding.

Quick experiments to run (notes for later):
- Re-run the Amazon/Nile snippet with temperature=0, top_p=1, seed fixed (if supported) across hosted-v2 and hosted-v2-fast to see determinism. Record flips per 50 runs.
- Reproduce the bleach case with safety-high vs safety-low system flag to see if instruction-filtering edits content (and create safety test case).
- For pricing mismatch, query the ingestion vector DB directly with the prompt embedding and inspect top-k docs; check doc timestamps and per-doc ingestion id.

Short checklist before filing formal bug: (tick when done)
- [ ] Reproduce at least 3 times with identical system/user inputs
- [ ] Confirm model variant used (route headers, trace id)
- [ ] Snapshot minimal reproduction prompt + headers and paste into ticket
- [ ] Attach 2–3 example runs showing the flip where applicable
- [ ] Note possible impact (CS inquiries, legal, safety)

Questions / unresolved: (quick brain-dump)
- How does dedicated routing choose fallback variants? Could that explain the pricing flips when autoscaling occurred?
- Do we have a running metric for intra-session inconsistency (same conversation producing inconsistent facts) at scale? If not, propose a lightweight heuristic: for a set of factual queries, compute contradiction rate per model/route.
- Safety team: what evidence threshold do you want before we label something 'dangerous contradiction' and pull for immediate attention?

To-dos (short):
- Convert the bleach example into a minimal safety repro and attach to INF-7812. (Maya)
- Run 50-run determinism test for the Amazon/Nile pair and dump results into GH PR for eval harness. (Ethan)
- Add small retrieval-check script to index inspector to print top-3 docs for a prompt. (Sofia)

Misc small notes:
- Observed that contradictions spike in sessions where user asks follow-ups that invert prior assertions ("Wait I thought X, is that wrong?"). Might be context-update behavior rather than pure hallucination — need to differentiate.
- Keep the micro-rubric short; for larger evals we should expand to include "source citation consistency" and "temporal consistency".

End of scratchpad.

(If this looks like it's getting formal, move to shared_drives/engineering/applied-ml/contradiction-detections and create a tracked ticket.)

source.title: Kepler Insurance LLM outreach journal
source.content:
Top-level goal:
- Land a 3-month pilot with Kepler Insurance (claims automation + agent assist for complex claims). Target decision maker: Head of Digital Transformation (R. Patel) + Dir. Claims Ops (S. Morales).

Quick account notes (public sources + hunting):
- Kepler: regional insurer, mid-market, heavy on legacy claims platform (Guidewire). Recent press: announced partnership with a data vendor for fraud scoring (2025 Q3).
- CTO hiring spree in 2024; new emphasis on developer-led automation projects.
- Compliance posture: SOC2 in place, strong emphasis on data residency for EU-sourced claims.
- Current pain: backlog on complex claims (~14% of claims requiring specialist review), long time-to-resolution for kitchen/fire claims.

Stakeholders / personas to map:
- Rajiv Patel (Head Digital Transformation) -> cares about measurable cycle time reduction, ROI, vendor SLAs, exec-level dashboards.
- Sofia Morales (Dir Claims Ops) -> cares about accuracy, false positive rates for automated triage, agent UX.
- Ben O'Neal (Infra Lead) -> cloud/infra constraints, VPC/hybrid options, KMS/HSM requirements.
- Legal/Compliance (TBD) -> data residency, retention, PII deletion, audit logs.

Initial outreach plan (sequence draft):
1) Warm intro email from Marcus referencing mutual contact (A. Gutierrez from vendor) + 1-pager (attached).
2) Short value note (2nd email) with a 3-metric pilot promise: 20% reduction in specialist-review volume OR 30% faster triage for targeted claim types OR cost-per-claim below X.
3) Call/Discovery request: 30 min to map claim types, volumes, current infra.
4) Technical follow-up (SRE + AE) offering on-prem/private demo + rollout options.
5) Pilot proposal: 8-week pilot, non-prod integration to Guidewire sandbox, success gates and rollback plan.

Positioning bullets (short lines for email/voice):
- "We help teams cut specialist-review queues by automating first-pass triage while preserving manual review for borderline cases."
- "Run models where you need them: hosted for fast PoC, dedicated for predictable throughput, or private VPC/on-prem for strict residency and audit needs."
- "Pilot-focused: measurable SLAs, per-route observability, and automatic fallback to conservative models if precision drops."

Canned objections + suggested responses:
- "We already run models in-house / MLOps team is building this": acknowledge + co-build angle: "We can plug into your infra or run an isolated private deployment and hand over operational playbooks — either way you get predictable latency and per-route SLAs out of the gate."
- "Security / data residency": "Support for VPC and on-prem control plane, KMS/HSM integrations and full audit logs — we can scope an isolated pilot to keep EU data in-region and provide exportable audit artifacts for compliance."
- "Cost concerns / unit economics": "Start with targeted claim types (high volume, high cost), we provide batching/caching recommendations and projection of cost per 1k processed tokens so you can compare to human specialist cost."
- "Model quality / hallucination risk": "We tie regressions to prompt sets and run continuous evals; pilot includes quality gates and a canary route so you see behavior before full rollout."

Pilot success metrics (proposed):
- Reduction in specialist-review volume for targeted claim types (target: >= 20% in 8 weeks).
- Average time-to-decision for triaged claims (target: 30% improvement).
- False positive rate (automated escalate when uncertain) below 6% for pilot scope.
- Latency SLO: median < 450ms for inference in dedicated/private pilot environment.
- Cost: projected token+infra cost per claim vs human-review baseline.

Technical checklist for discovery call:
- Claim volume by type: monthly counts, avg doc length, attachments?
- Existing data flow: does Guidewire expose payloads? any enrichment currently?
- Desired integration points: webhooks, API, internal UI augment?
- Compliance constraints: where must data live, retention windows, PII deletion policies?
- Acceptance criteria: exact metrics they will evaluate, sampling plan, evaluation cadence.

Collateral to attach/send:
- One-pager: "Redwood Pilot for Insurance Claims" (short bullets + diagram) -- draft link CONFLUENCE-KEPLER-PILOT-001
- Tech appendix: routing options (hosted/dedicated/private), SLA table, sample rollout timeline.
- Case study snippet: anonymized retail insurer pilot -> 18% specialist-reduction in 6 weeks.

Open questions / research tasks (TODO):
- Confirm if Kepler has Guidewire sandbox access for vendor pilots (ask sales ops).
- Identify legal contact in APAC/EU for residency requirements.
- Get exact monthly document sizes to estimate token footprint (ask data engineering).
- Draft tailored ROI calc for Sofia: show agent-hours saved vs license cost.

Call prep notes (if we get intro meeting):
- Bring 2 slides: pilot scope + rollback plan; bring an engineer who can speak about integration latency and KV cache behavior.
- Have a short live demo script that shows: triage suggestion -> user accept/modify -> audit log entry.
- Be ready to propose three rollout options: PoC (hosted), predictable pilot (dedicated), compliance-first (private).

Next actions (me/Marcus):
- Marcus to send intro email by 2026-03-03 (include 1-pager).
- Ava to build ROI stub and token estimates by 2026-03-06.
- If positive reply, schedule discovery with Ben + Sofia; include SRE + AE.

Notes-to-self / voice memo fragments:
- Keep ask small early: 8-week, single claim type, defined success gates.
- Emphasize that Redwood enables conservative fallback to human-only route (important for claims ops trust).
- Use dedicated capacity pitch for latency-sensitive UI augment use cases (agent assist).

Scratch area (copy/paste friendly email draft):
Subject: Pilot proposal — reduce complex-claim review backlog at Kepler

Hi Rajiv —

Marcus suggested I reach out. We work with insurers to automate first-pass triage and agent assist while keeping full auditability and residency controls. For Kepler we can run an 8-week pilot against a single claim category (kitchen/fire or vehicle total-loss) and commit to measurable gates: X% reduction in specialist reviews OR Y% faster triage plus full audit logs and rollback controls.

Would you be open to 20–30 minutes next week to map volumes and success criteria? If yes I’ll bring a technical engineer to talk integration options (hosted, dedicated, private VPC).

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions, Redwood Inference

End."

source.title: Candidate flow heatmap + headcount contingency notes (scratch)
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick scratchpad for upcoming hc-sync (1:1 with Lina) + weekly hiring triage. Focus: understanding where candidates are getting stuck, short-term contingency scenarios (Q1 hires slipping), and debrief action items that reduce time-to-offer and improve ramp predictability.

Meeting goals (30–45m):
- Surface candidate funnel chokepoints (by role/loop stage)
- Agree on 2 mitigation levers to deploy this week if offers slip
- Decide follow-ups and owners for interview calibration + onboarding checklist updates

Context / current snapshot (rolling numbers pulled 2026-02-25):
- Roles prioritized this quarter: Infra SRE (4 IC), LLM Runtime Eng (3 IC), Product Data Scientist (2 IC), Developer Experience PM (1 PM).
- Funnel (total open reqs = 10):
  - New applicants screened: 210
  - Recruiter screens completed: 82
  - Hiring-manager screens scheduled: 48
  - On-site / loop invites sent: 23
  - On-site completed: 16
  - Offers extended: 6
  - Offers accepted: 3
- Time to offer (median): 28 days (target 21)
- Offer acceptance rate: 50% last 30d (target 70%)
- Top friction observed: scheduling delays between HM screen -> loop (avg 9 days), inconsistent debrief notes causing re-interviews, compensation negotiation misalignment on LLM Runtime banding.

Heatmap quick view (where candidates drop):
- Early funnel (recruiter -> HM screen): ~61% conversion (ok)
- HM screen -> loop invite: ~58% conversion (scheduling delays + unclear role scope)
- Loop -> offer: ~37% conversion (interviewer alignment + score variance)
- Offer -> accept: 50% (counteroffers and clarity on career path concerns)

Hypotheses (what's causing slips):
1) Interview loop is too long and ad-hoc; panels not calibrated on rubric. Some interviewers default to conservative scoring.
2) Compensation band guidance for LLM Runtime roles is outdated vs market comps (remote candidates) -> longer negotiation cycles.
3) Onboarding & ramp expectations not clearly communicated in offer conversations (candidates worry about unclear success metrics).

Immediate mitigation levers (pick 2 this week):
A. "Loop compression" experiment: reserve 2 full-days/month where hiring managers + core interviewers commit to back-to-back loops for key roles. Owner: Ethan. Timeline: run one day next Thu (2026-03-05).
B. Calibration huddle: 30m interview rubric sync for Runtime + SRE interviewers; produce one-pager "what passes" for each role. Owner: Priya. Due: 2026-03-03.

title: Candidate flow heatmap + headcount contingency notes (scratch)
preview:
Purpose:
Quick scratchpad for upcoming hc-sync (1:1 with Lina) + weekly hiring triage. Focus: understanding where candidates are getting stuck, short-term contingency scenarios (Q1 hires slipping), and debrief action items that reduce time-to-offer and improve ramp predictability.

- Surface candidate funnel chokepoints (by role/loop stage)
- Agree on 2 mitigation levers to deploy this week if offers slip
- Decide follow-ups and owners for interview calibration + onboarding checklist updates

Context / current snapshot (rolling numbers pulled 2026-02-25):
- Roles prioritized this quarter: Infra SRE (4 IC), LLM Runtime Eng (3 IC), Product Data Scientist (2 IC), Developer Experience PM (1 PM).
- Funnel (total open reqs = 10):
  - New applicants screened: 210
  - Recruiter screens completed: 82
  - Hiring-manager screens scheduled: 48
  - On-site / loop invites sent: 23
  - On-site completed: 16
  - Offers extended: 6
  - Offers accepted: 3
- Time to offer (median): 28 days (target 21)
- Offer acceptance rate: 50% last 30d (target 70%)
- Top friction observed: scheduling delays between HM screen -> loop (avg 9 days), inconsistent debrief notes causing re-interviews, compensation negotiation misalignment on LLM Runtime banding.

- Early funnel (recruiter -> HM screen): ~61% conversion (ok)
- HM screen -> loop invite: ~58% conversion (scheduling delays + unclear role scope)

- Offer -> accept: 50% (counteroffers and clarity on career path concerns)

source.title: Adversarial prompt surface mitigation sketch
source.content:
Notes to self: rough design for a lightweight, pluggable mitigation layer for adversarial prompt inputs. This is intentionally coarse — think of it as the scratchpad to drive an API + config proto for client+ingress integration.

Goals:
- Provide a tiny, deterministic API that clients (web, mobile, sidecars) and ingress can call to decide whether to allow, rewrite, or escalate a prompt before it hits the model.
- Keep latency impact sub-10ms for common checks; degrade gracefully to async evaluation when needed.
- Expose explainability signals for downstream QA and audits.
- Make policy configurable per-tenant, per-route, per-model-version.

Threat model (quick):
- Prompt injection attempts trying to override system instructions or exfiltrate PII.
- Maliciously formatted inputs that attempt to exploit tokenization quirks or model behavior (e.g., multi-line re-instructions).
- Social engineering prompts that coax tools into performing disallowed actions.
- We do NOT aim to solve full adversarial robustness here — this is a pragmatic runtime shield.

High-level approach:
1) Lightweight deterministic checks (regex, token-blacklist, suspicious-structure heuristics).
2) Risk scoring service (fast ML model) that returns risk-band and signals.
3) Policy engine (config-driven) to map risk -> action (allow, redact, require-2fa, rewrite, block + explain).
4) Telemetry + audit log for every decision (signals, policy id, deterministic rule hits, score).

API surface (proto sketch) — aim for a single-call shape that is easy to call from many runtimes:

POST /v1/prompt-eval
Request body (json):
{
  "tenant_id": "string",              // optional
  "route": "chat.create",           // route or API path
  "model": "gptx-8k",               // model variant
  "prompt_text": "string",
  "context_hints": ["last_user_msg", "system_instruction"],
  "client_meta": {                   // freeform string map — flatten in code comments
    "platform": "web", 
    "sdk_version": "0.9.2"
  },
  "timeout_ms": 30                   // allow clients to request fast path
}

Response body (json):
{
  "decision": "allow|rewrite|redact|block|escalate_async|require_interstitial", 
  "risk_score": "0.0-1.0",
  "signals": ["regex:contains-exec", "token-entropy-high"],
  "rewrite_hint": "string (if decision==rewrite)",
  "rule_hits": ["RULE_ID_23", "RULE_ID_PI_4"],
  "explain": "short human-readable rationale",
  "policy_id": "tenant-policy-v3"
}

Notes on shapes: keep fields as strings or arrays of strings so client SDKs in different languages don't have to deserialize complex typed objects. Use simple enums for decision.

Config schema (proto / yaml sketch):
policy:
  id: tenant-policy-v3
  default_action: allow
  routes:
    "chat.create":
      - match: "contains-system-instruction"
        action: redact
        threshold: 0.7
      - match: "contains-pii"
        action: require_interstitial
        reviewer_group: legal-oncall
  deterministic_rules:
    - id: RULE_ID_PI_4
      pattern: "\b(ssn|social-security|credit card)\b"
      action: require_interstitial
      severity: high

Runtime components (rough):
- Client shim (JS/Android/iOS): lightweight call to /v1/prompt-eval with short timeout_ms. If service returns allow -> pass through. If rewrite/redact -> apply client-side rewrite templates then send. If require_interstitial -> show blocking UI. If block -> throttle/400.
- Ingress filter (edge proxy env): call prompt-eval as a fallback if client didn't. Optionally attach deterministic rule hits to request headers for downstream tracing.
- Risk scorer service: small fast model (trees or quantized NN) with cached results for repeated phrases; feature set: token-entropy, unusual-charset, presence of re-instruction keywords, url-embedding-score, pii-detections.
- Policy engine: config store (per-tenant) + eval runtime that maps signals to decision. Versioned policy artifacts for rollout.
- Async reviewer queue: for escalate_async actions, push prompt + signals + trace id to reviewer UI and return a ticket id.

ASCII diagram (very rough):

[client] --(prompt)--> [client-shim] --(eval call w/ timeout)-> [prompt-eval svc] --(decision)-> [client-shim / ingress]
                                    \
                                    v
                             [policy engine & rules DB]
                                    |
                                    v
                             [async reviewer queue]

Rollout ideas:
- Start with deterministic rule-only mode (no ML scoring): log-only, then warn-only (sdk shows non-blocking banner), then enforce for high severity rules.
- Parallel shadow scoring for 2-4 weeks to collect signals and calibrate thresholds.
- Create canary policy per-account for progressive exposure.

Telemetry / metrics to capture:
- eval.latency.p50/p95, decision.counts by reason, false_positive_reports (via manual review feedback), audit_log.retention_id, avg_risk_by_route, top_rule_hits, escalation.queue_depth.
- Important to include token-sample-hash (not raw prompt) in logs to enable deduping without storing the plain text. Use HMAC(key) per-tenant for dedupe.

Developer ergonomics + SDK notes:
- Provide both sync (fast time-limited) and async evaluation endpoints. Clients should default to sync with short timeout and fallback to allow if eval times out (configurable).
- Offer a local deterministic rules bundle (json) that can be shipped in client SDK for offline protection and to reduce eval calls. Bundle should be CDN-updatable with version checks.

Open questions / tradeoffs (TODO):
- Where to draw the line for client-side edits vs server-side enforcement? UX vs security.
- How to avoid over-blocking legitimate creative instructions (false positives)? Need a lightweight review flow and metrics.
- Costs: eval calls add ops — mitigate with local bundle + aggressive caching.
- Privacy: telemetry design must avoid storing raw prompts. HMAC-only + optional encrypted archive for legal review.

Next steps (short):
- Prototype API handler (golang) that runs deterministic rules + stub scorer. (Diego to take infra proto).
- Sketch client-shim API (JS) and fallback UX patterns. (Priya to mock).
- Put a minimal policy YAML and rule examples into the repo and link to PR.
- Draft an internal runbook for reviewer flow and privacy guardrails.

Quick TODOs in doc: 
- [ ] Define RULE taxonomy names and severity mapping
- [ ] Decide signal list and canonical names (token-entropy, pii, url-exec, system-instruction-match)
- [ ] Draft sample user-facing interstitial copy for require_interstitial
- [ ] Add canonical header names for trace + rule-hits

End of scratchpad. This is a working surface — expect to iterate.

-- Ava

- Privacy: telemetry design must avoid storing raw prompts. HMAC-only + optional encrypted archive for legal review.

Next steps (short):
- Prototype API handler (golang) that runs deterministic rules + stub scorer. (Diego to take infra proto).
- Sketch client-shim API (JS) and fallback UX patterns. (Priya to mock).
- Put a minimal policy YAML and rule examples into the repo and link to PR.
- Draft an internal runbook for reviewer flow and privacy guardrails.

Quick TODOs in doc: 
- [ ] Define RULE taxonomy names and severity mapping
- [ ] Decide signal list and canonical names (token-entropy, pii, url-exec, system-instruction-match)
- [ ] Draft sample user-facing interstitial copy for require_interstitial
- [ ] Add canonical header names for trace + rule-hits

End of scratchpad. This is a working surface — expect to iterate.

-- Ava
text:
Notes to self: rough design for a lightweight, pluggable mitigation layer for adversarial prompt inputs. This is intentionally coarse — think of it as the scratchpad to drive an API + config proto for client+ingress integration.

Goals:
- Provide a tiny, deterministic API that clients (web, mobile, sidecars) and ingress can call to decide whether to allow, rewrite, or escalate a prompt before it hits the model.
- Keep latency impact sub-10ms for common checks; degrade gracefully to async evaluation when needed.
- Expose explainability signals for downstream QA and audits.
- Make policy configurable per-tenant, per-route, per-model-version.

Threat model (quick):
- Prompt injection attempts trying to override system instructions or exfiltrate PII.
- Maliciously formatted inputs that attempt to exploit tokenization quirks or model behavior (e.g., multi-line re-instructions).
- Social engineering prompts that coax tools into performing disallowed actions.
- We do NOT aim to solve full adversarial robustness here — this is a pragmatic runtime shield.

High-level approach:
1) Lightweight deterministic checks (regex, token-blacklist, suspicious-structure heuristics).
2) Risk scoring service (fast ML model) that returns risk-band and signals.
3) Policy engine (config-driven) to map risk -> action (allow, redact, require-2fa, rewrite, block + explain).
4) Telemetry + audit log for every decision (signals, policy id, deterministic rule hits, score).

API surface (proto sketch) — aim for a single-call shape that is easy to call from many runtimes:

POST /v1/prompt-eval
Request body (json):
{
  "tenant_id": "string",              // optional
  "route": "chat.create",           // route or API path
  "model": "gptx-8k",               // model variant
  "prompt_text": "string",
  "context_hints": ["last_user_msg", "system_instruction"],
  "client_meta": {                   // freeform string map — flatten in code comments
    "platform": "web", 
    "sdk_version": "0.9.2"
  },
  "timeout_ms": 30                   // allow clients to request fast path
}

Response body (json):
{
  "decision": "allow|rewrite|redact|block|escalate_async|require_interstitial", 
  "risk_score": "0.0-1.0",
  "signals": ["regex:contains-exec", "token-entropy-high"],
  "rewrite_hint": "string (if decision==rewrite)",
  "rule_hits": ["RULE_ID_23", "RULE_ID_PI_4"],
  "explain": "short human-readable rationale",
  "policy_id": "tenant-policy-v3"
}

Notes on shapes: keep fields as strings or arrays of strings so client SDKs in different languages don't have to deserialize complex typed objects. Use simple enums for decision.

Config schema (proto / yaml sketch):
policy:
  id: tenant-policy-v3
  default_action: allow
  routes:
    "chat.create":
      - match: "contains-system-instruction"
        action: redact
        threshold: 0.7
      - match: "contains-pii"
        action: require_interstitial
        reviewer_group: legal-oncall
  deterministic_rules:
    - id: RULE_ID_PI_4
      pattern: "\b(ssn|social-security|credit card)\b"
      action: require_interstitial
      severity: high

Runtime components (rough):
- Client shim (JS/Android/iOS): lightweight call to /v1/prompt-eval with short timeout_ms. If service returns allow -> pass through. If rewrite/redact -> apply client-side rewrite templates then send. If require_interstitial -> show blocking UI. If block -> throttle/400.
- Ingress filter (edge proxy env): call prompt-eval as a fallback if client didn't. Optionally attach deterministic rule hits to request headers for downstream tracing.
- Risk scorer service: small fast model (trees or quantized NN) with cached results for repeated phrases; feature set: token-entropy, unusual-charset, presence of re-instruction keywords, url-embedding-score, pii-detections.
- Policy engine: config store (per-tenant) + eval runtime that maps signals to decision. Versioned policy artifacts for rollout.
- Async reviewer queue: for escalate_async actions, push prompt + signals + trace id to reviewer UI and return a ticket id.

ASCII diagram (very rough):

[client] --(prompt)--> [client-shim] --(eval call w/ timeout)-> [prompt-eval svc] --(decision)-> [client-shim / ingress]
                                    \
                                    v
                             [policy engine & rules DB]
                                    |
                                    v
                             [async reviewer queue]

Rollout ideas:
- Start with deterministic rule-only mode (no ML scoring): log-only, then warn-only (sdk shows non-blocking banner), then enforce for high severity rules.
- Parallel shadow scoring for 2-4 weeks to collect signals and calibrate thresholds.
- Create canary policy per-account for progressive exposure.

Telemetry / metrics to capture:
- eval.latency.p50/p95, decision.counts by reason, false_positive_reports (via manual review feedback), audit_log.retention_id, avg_risk_by_route, top_rule_hits, escalation.queue_depth.
- Important to include token-sample-hash (not raw prompt) in logs to enable deduping without storing the plain text. Use HMAC(key) per-tenant for dedupe.

Developer ergonomics + SDK notes:
- Provide both sync (fast time-limited) and async evaluation endpoints. Clients should default to sync with short timeout and fallback to allow if eval times out (configurable).
- Offer a local deterministic rules bundle (json) that can be shipped in client SDK for offline protection and to reduce eval calls. Bundle should be CDN-updatable with version checks.

Open questions / tradeoffs (TODO):
- Where to draw the line for client-side edits vs server-side enforcement? UX vs security.
- How to avoid over-blocking legitimate creative instructions (false positives)? Need a lightweight review flow and metrics.
- Costs: eval calls add ops — mitigate with local bundle + aggressive caching.
- Privacy: telemetry design must avoid storing raw prompts. HMAC-only + optional encrypted archive for legal review.

Next steps (short):
- Prototype API handler (golang) that runs deterministic rules + stub scorer. (Diego to take infra proto).
- Sketch client-shim API (JS) and fallback UX patterns. (Priya to mock).
- Put a minimal policy YAML and rule examples into the repo and link to PR.
- Draft an internal runbook for reviewer flow and privacy guardrails.

Quick TODOs in doc: 
- [ ] Define RULE taxonomy names and severity mapping
- [ ] Decide signal list and canonical names (token-entropy, pii, url-exec, system-instruction-match)
- [ ] Draft sample user-facing interstitial copy for require_interstitial
- [ ] Add canonical header names for trace + rule-hits

End of scratchpad. This is a working surface — expect to iterate.

-- Ava

source.title: Client instruction wrapping & verification — scratchpad
source.body:
Context:
This is a personal sketch for a small runtime-level addition: a light-weight client-side 'instruction wrapper' that ensures certain critical instructions survive client-side transformers, are auditable at the server, and can be validated before being applied to session state or KV caches. Motivation comes from a few incidents where client-side SDKs injected or rewrote system instructions (for localization or heuristics) and servers couldn't distinguish benign wrapping from accidental/suspicious mutations.

Goals:
- Provide a minimal wire-compatible wrapper for instructions that: (1) survives simple proxies and client-side transforms, (2) carries proof metadata (who signed/wrapped it), (3) is easy for server to verify without heavy crypto ops in the fast path.
- Keep opt-in for customers; default behaviour is transparent (no changes).
- Support both hosted API and private deployments; design must be compatible with VPC egress proxies and sidecars.

Constraints / non-goals:
- This is not a replacement for full message signing/zero-knowledge proofs. Keep to HMAC-like lightweight verification for audit and tamper-detection.
- Avoid requiring new per-request public-key ops in the hot token path. Verification should be cacheable per-client-session.
- Client SDKs need to be backward compatible. If server sees unknown wrapper, fallback to previous behaviour (log + gentle reject if configured).

High-level proposal:
- New optional field at request top-level: instruction_wrapper. When present, it carries: wrapped_text, metadata, verifier_tag.
- Wrapper is JSON (stringified) and then base64/URL-safe encoded to avoid whitespace/linebreak issues over different transports.
- verifier_tag is a short digest (HMAC-SHA256 truncated to 12 bytes, base64) computed with a per-client secret known to the server (or by public-key for enterprise customers who opt-in).

Example request shape (pseudo JSON):
{
  "input": "chat turn payload...",
  "instruction_wrapper": "eyJ3cmFwcGVkX3RleHQiOiBcIlN5c3RlbTogUHJvdmlkZSBicm9hZHNhc2UgaW5zdHJ1Y3Rpb25cIiwgIm1ldGFkYXRhIjogeyJjbGllbnRfaWQiOiAiYXBwLWExIiwgInRzIjogMTY5OTk5OTAwMH0sICJ2ZXJpZmllcl90YWciOiAiQUJDRUYiIH0="
}

Where decoded wrapper: {"wrapped_text":"System: Provide broadsafe instruction","metadata":{"client_id":"app-a1","ts":1699999000},"verifier_tag":"ABCDEF"}

Server-side verification flow (optimised):
1) Extract instruction_wrapper, decode base64 -> JSON.
2) Lookup client session secret (cache key = client_id) from KV (fast path cache). If not found, go to auth store (slower).
3) Recompute truncated HMAC over wrapped_text + metadata.ts and compare to verifier_tag. If match, accept and mark instruction as verified for telemetry.
4) If mismatch: depending on route config - (a) accept but flag (log/metric), (b) reject request with 400 if enforcement is strict for that tenant, (c) fall back to non-wrapped behaviour for backwards compatibility.

Config sketch (console flags / host-api):
- tenant.settings.instruction_wrapping.enabled: false|true
- tenant.settings.instruction_wrapping.enforcement: permissive|strict|disabled
- tenant.settings.instruction_wrapping.lookup: session-cache|auth-service
- tenant.settings.instruction_wrapping.crypto: hmac-sha256-trunc12|rsa-pss (enterprise)

API surface notes for SDKs:
- SDK convenience: wrapInstruction(text, opts) -> returns wrapper string (base64).
- SDK should provide an asynchronous seal API that requests client-side secret from platform when running in privileged contexts (e.g., on-device private keys).
- Default SDK behaviour: no wrapping unless user calls wrapInstruction or a high-level helper (e.g., systemPrompt.wrap()).

Sequence diagram (ASCII, simplified):
Client SDK -> Proxy: POST /v1/generate {input, instruction_wrapper}
Proxy -> Server: forwards as-is (transparent)
Server -> KV (cache): get client_secret for client_id (cache miss -> auth service)
Server -> Auth Service: validate client_id -> return secret
Server -> Crypto: hmac(secret, wrapped_text||ts) -> compare -> OK -> apply instruction

Failure modes / edge cases:
- Proxy normalizes whitespace or reflows JSON -> encoded base64 wrapper mitigates this.
- Large wrapper -> should be bounded (limit wrapped_text to 2k chars). Enforce at SDK and server.
- Replayed wrappers -> include ts and session-id, server can rate-limit or track seen-tag cache (LRU short-term) to detect replay. Cost: memory.
- Client secret leaked -> rotate secrets by version field in metadata. Server should accept small window of versions to allow rotation.

Telemetry and rollout plan:
- Phase 0: SDK helper only, no server verification. Collect opt-in telemetry (how many wrappers created).
- Phase 1: Server-side logging of wrappers (no enforcement). Flag mismatches to internal metrics. Turn on for 10% of tenants.
- Phase 2: Enforcement optional (per-tenant) with HMAC verification. Enterprise: provide RSA option and key registration API.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Q: How do we store per-client secret for hosted API without increasing auth-service load? A: Keep session-secret cache on serving nodes with TTL; refresh on miss.
- Q: Do we need to include route/model context into HMAC to bind wrapper to a particular model? Might avoid cross-route reuse. TODO: prototype adding route-id into MAC input.
- Q: UX for customers when wrapper verification fails — what error codes and docs?
- TODO: write SDK helpers for JS/Go/Python (wrap/unpack/verify) and add tests for proxies that alter headers/whitespace.
- TODO: write a small sim harness to test replay/dedup detection memory overhead.

Notes to self:
- Check ENG-987 for previous discussion about 'thin wrapping' — some overlap.
- Add note to developer docs about base64 URL-safe encoding decision.
- Reach out to security (Priya) about truncation length for HMAC and acceptable risk.

Rough prioritised checklist:
- [ ] Prototype SDK wrap/unpack in node (1 day)
- [ ] Server verify fast-path using in-memory session cache (2-3 days)
- [ ] Add tenant settings / console toggle (1 sprint)
- [ ] Metrics & logging + Slack alerts for mismatch spikes (1 sprint)

If this lands well it should reduce class of accidental instruction mutations and give observability for clients doing automated prompt-localization. Keep lightweight and opt-in.

End of scratchpad.

source.title: Hold-and-release surge log (microburst capture)
content: Context: quick personal run to capture hold-and-release microbursts we keep seeing in customer traces. Trying to reproduce the pattern where a queue builds quietly and then a flood of requests is released (client-side batching) causing transient tail blowups.
Goal: map p50/p95/p99 behavior, identify whether KV-cache warmup or batching thresholding is the dominant penalty, and sanity-check backoff heuristics.
Date of runs: 2025-12-02 to 2026-02-10 (iterative). This doc collates the most representative traces.
Environment:
- cluster: dev-fleet-us-west-2 (mixed A100 + H100), pinned to 1x A100 per worker for these runs
- model: opt-13b-quant (4-bit q4m) served via runtime v2.3.1
- runtime flags: continuous-batching=true, preemption=off, kv-cache=true
- client: surge-runner v0.9 (local load injector), clients=48, connection multiplexing disabled
- target route: /v1/generate (no function-calls), prompt length ~128 tok avg, gen length target 64
Pattern injected (hold-and-release):
- hold period: 18s of steady low qps (1-2 qps sustained) to allow many clients to queue locally
- release pulse: 1s ramp to peak (~450 concurrent requests), then exponential decay 1.5s half-life
- repeat cadence: every 60s x 10 pulses per run
Experiment knobs varied across runs:
- batching timeout: {2ms, 6ms, 12ms}
- max batch size: {32, 64, 128}
- kv-cache priming: (cold, primed with synthetic prefixes), eviction-policy: LRU vs fractional-reserve
- arrival smoothing: client-side hold jitter applied vs none
Representative run summary (best reproducible run):
- config: batching_timeout=6ms, max_batch=64, kv-cache primed, client jitter 30ms
- measured overall peak qps: 430 (server-side accepted), sustained during pulse ~380 qps
- p50: 18ms, p90: 72ms, p95: 128ms, p99: 540ms, max observed: 2.2s (few outliers)
- GPU util during pulse: 85% (avg), but tailing tasks show GPU context-switch spikes
- queue depth observed on worker: instantaneous queue=220 at pulse peak
- cache hit rate on warm run: 78% (requests sharing prefixes), cold run: 18%
- eviction spikes: when queue depth > 150, observed small burst of KV-evictions triggering recompute overhead
Latency histogram (ASCII rough):
Observations / quick hypotheses:

1) Primary source of p99 inflation is not GPU saturation alone — GPU util near 85% but still has headroom; rather, queueing + batch-coalescing + KV-cache eviction interplay creates long-tail recompute/IOs.

2) When batching_timeout is short (2ms) we see more small batches, higher scheduling overhead, and slightly higher p50 but slightly lower p99 — because large queueing collapses into fewer big batches that cause evictions.
3) When batching_timeout is long (12ms) we see fewer but bigger batches; p50 improves but p99 worsens dramatically due to long service times for huge batches and longer KV eviction windows.

4) KV-cache priming matters a lot: cold runs have many cache-miss recomputes that cascade during a pulse. Eviction-policy LRU reduces p99 compared to fractional-reserve in my tests (surprising — need to confirm).
5) Client-side release jitter (30-50ms) smooths the pulse and reduces p99 by ~35% in these synthetic runs. Not a panacea but inexpensive.
Representative trace notes (timestamps relative to pulse start):
- t=0s: clients release, queue ramps 0->220 over 350ms
- t=0.35s: first big batch scheduled, batch size ~112, single-model forward time jumps, downstream latency amplification starts
- t=0.8s: eviction batch observed, 18% of requests triggered recompute (cache-miss), ~700ms extra per request in that subset
- t=1.6s: system drains to backpressure equilibrium, tail decays but a few requests still executing (1-2s)
Notes on instrumentation gaps:
- need per-request cache-miss tagging surfaced in trace logs (currently requires correlating eviction counters with request ps).
- scheduling timestamps are coarse (10ms buckets) — hard to isolate whether scheduling or model forward is dominant in some spikes.
Actionable next steps (short list):
- (A) Add a per-request KV hit/miss flag in the runtime logs for easier correlation.
- (B) Run the same hold-release pattern but with small global pre-warming (send short priming prefixes 10s before release) to test partial-priming effectiveness.
- (C) Try adaptive batching: dynamically cap batch size during pulse if estimated queue depth > 120, compare p99.
- (D) Validate that eviction-policy LRU wins across more seeds and on H100 nodes.
- (E) Prototype client-side soft-release (stochastic release over 300ms) as a low-effort mitigation for PLG clients.
Notes-to-self / TODOs:
- capture full flame graphs for the worst 50 p99 requests next run (attach to INF-3247).
- coordinate with Samir to add cache-miss flag in logs (ticket created).
- try lowering model concurrency target (threads-per-device) to see if that reduces scheduling jitter.
Final quick takeaways (for README/notes):
- Hold-and-release patterns produce microbursts that are mainly harmful because of stateful caches and batch-size amplification, not purely because of peak QPS.
- Mitigations should be multi-pronged: client-side smoothing, runtime per-batch anti-amplification heuristics, and better cache priming signals.
- Low-hanging wins: client jitter + small priming window reduced p99 materially in these runs — worth surfaced guidance for customers and an SDK helper.
Appendix: raw sample metrics (CSV-ish lines) for the representative run:
timestamp,qps,p50_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms,queue_depth,gpu_util,kv_hit_pct
(end of doc)

source.title: Boundary markup experiment journal
source.content:
Personal experiment log — NOT a finalized policy.

Context:
- Working through messy multi-span / nested-entity cases that the current labeling UI keeps flagging as inconsistent.
- Goal: produce a compact set of heuristics we can apply in a second-pass cleanup job and surface as suggestions in the labeling tool.
- Related: Linear LINK LABEL-1723 (stacked spans), PR in annotation-tool for overlapping spans rendering.

Notes / motivations:
- Many annotators default to 'longest span' which hides useful nested labels (e.g., Product Name + Version + Code identifier). We need a rule set to decide when to keep nested tags vs collapse.
- Tokenization mismatch is the single biggest source of disagreement. Tokenizer: huggingface/llama-tokenizer (v1) in our pipeline; many human labels are character-indexed, the UI converts to tokens poorly for hyphenates and slashes.

Quick heuristics (proposal):
1) Preserve nested spans if inner-span is of a different semantic category AND length(inner) <= 0.6 * length(outer). Example: ["Acme Cloud Platform v2" -> PRODUCT] with inner ["v2" -> VERSION] keep both.
2) Collapse when labels are synonyms (e.g., ORG + ORG_ALIAS) unless alias is short (<=3 tokens) and appears inside parentheses.
3) Punctuation policy: trailing punctuation (.,;:) should be excluded from span. Leading quote/paren should be excluded unless it changes meaning (e.g., single quotes used to indicate a literal).
4) Hyphenated tokens: prefer splitting if both sides are dictionary words and both are labeled separately in sample examples. If only one side is meaningful, include whole token.
5) Date normalization: prefer canonical ISO label span for yyyy-mm-dd, but label visible span exactly as annotated. Store normalized value in metadata (not in this doc).

Examples (manual annotations):
- "Upgrade to Acme-Cloud v2.1-beta fixed it."
  Preferred: [Acme-Cloud -> PRODUCT], [v2.1-beta -> VERSION] (exclude trailing period). Note: hyphenated product name kept whole.

- "Contact: support@company.co.uk"
  Preferred: [support@company.co.uk -> EMAIL] (do not split at dot).

- "The CEO (Jane K. Doe) announced..."
  Preferred: [The CEO -> TITLE] (optional), [Jane K. Doe -> PERSON] — rule: if honorific/title appears immediately before name and is generic, label name as PERSON; TITLE is optional and marked with lower precedence for downstream tasks.

- Nested overlap case:
  Text: "Acme Cloud (formerly Acme Systems)"
  Options: keep both: [Acme Cloud -> ORG], [Acme Systems -> ORG_ALIAS]; collapse to ORG with alias metadata. Prefer keeping both for downstream resolution tasks.

Edge patterns to watch:
- Code tokens like `user_id=1234` — carbon-copy span should be ANON_ID if pattern matches regex (prefix with _id, id=, uid:), otherwise GENERIC_CODE.
- URLs with tracking params: label only the base domain + path; strip query string for span if present (but save full URL in metadata).
- Comma-separated entity lists: annotate each item separately even when punctuation ambiguous.

Annotation format notes (for cleanup job):
- Preferred export: CSV with columns: sentence, span_text, label, char_start, char_end, normalized_form. We'll produce a small sample below.

Sample CSV rows (manual):
"Acme-Cloud v2.1-beta fixed it.","Acme-Cloud","PRODUCT","0","9","Acme-Cloud"
"Acme-Cloud v2.1-beta fixed it.","v2.1-beta","VERSION","10","19","v2.1-beta"
"Contact: support@company.co.uk","support@company.co.uk","EMAIL","9","29","support@company.co.uk"

Common disagreements I saw in the batch (human notes):
- Whether to include ordinal indicators ("1st", "2nd"): some labelers include 'st', others drop. Decide: include as part of span.
- Parenthetical abbreviations: "International Business Machines (IBM)": some labelers tag only 'IBM' as ORG, some tag both. Prefer both.
- Multi-token job titles: "Senior ML Engineer" sometimes split into TITLE + ROLE; recommend single ROLE="Senior ML Engineer" for downstream role extraction simplicity.

Open questions / To discuss:
- Should we prefer token-based indices or char-based indices for exports? Pros/cons: char stable across tokenizers, token indices are compact for model training. My leaning: store both in export.
- Exact threshold for 'nested span keep' heuristic (0.6 above) is arbitrary — need to surface a few dozen examples to validate.
- How to surface these suggestions in the UI without overwhelming labelers? A non-blocking sidebar with "suggested spans" might work.

TODOs (short):
- [ ] Build small validation script: takes two label files and outputs disagreement report by rule category (punctuation, hyphen, nested). (linked to GH PR 321).
- [ ] Collect 200 examples that trigger 'nested-vs-collapse' and run majority vote.
- [ ] Ship a micro-suggestion UI experiment behind a feature flag.

Quick scrub checklist for adhoc cleanup pass:
- Normalize case for canonical forms only, keep original span casing in text column.
- Remove trailing punctuation from spans unless punctuation is semantic (e.g., trademark ®).
- Normalize version tokens to semver where possible for VERSION label.

Notes to self / random thoughts:
- Might be worth teaching the model to predict a "span-role-stack" (list) rather than forcing single label to reduce info loss. But that increases label space.
- For long legal entity names (>7 tokens), consider truncating visible span to key noun phrase and keeping full form in metadata.

Conversation snippets to capture (for context):
- Evan: "we lose a lot of product/version signal when labels collapse" (Slack #labeling, 2026-01-12)
- Sara: "annotators hate double-clicking to create nested spans" (triage notes)

End of log for now. Will iterate after we run the disagreement script.

PRIVACY NOTE: Examples above are synthetic/obfuscated. Do not paste customer PII from JIRA or slack into production exports.

-- Priya

- Whether to include ordinal indicators ("1st", "2nd"): some labelers include 'st', others drop. Decide: include as part of span.
- Parenthetical abbreviations: "International Business Machines (IBM)": some labelers tag only 'IBM' as ORG, some tag both. Prefer both.
- Multi-token job titles: "Senior ML Engineer" sometimes split into TITLE + ROLE; recommend single ROLE="Senior ML Engineer" for downstream role extraction simplicity.

Open questions / To discuss:
- Should we prefer token-based indices or char-based indices for exports? Pros/cons: char stable across tokenizers, token indices are compact for model training. My leaning: store both in export.
- Exact threshold for 'nested span keep' heuristic (0.6 above) is arbitrary — need to surface a few dozen examples to validate.
- How to surface these suggestions in the UI without overwhelming labelers? A non-blocking sidebar with "suggested spans" might work.

TODOs (short):
- [ ] Build small validation script: takes two label files and outputs disagreement report by rule category (punctuation, hyphen, nested). (linked to GH PR 321).
- [ ] Collect 200 examples that trigger 'nested-vs-collapse' and run majority vote.
- [ ] Ship a micro-suggestion UI experiment behind a feature flag.

Quick scrub checklist for adhoc cleanup pass:
- Normalize case for canonical forms only, keep original span casing in text column.
- Remove trailing punctuation from spans unless punctuation is semantic (e.g., trademark ®).
- Normalize version tokens to semver where possible for VERSION label.

Notes to self / random thoughts:
- Might be worth teaching the model to predict a "span-role-stack" (list) rather than forcing single label to reduce info loss. But that increases label space.
- For long legal entity names (>7 tokens), consider truncating visible span to key noun phrase and keeping full form in metadata.

Conversation snippets to capture (for context):
- Evan: "we lose a lot of product/version signal when labels collapse" (Slack #labeling, 2026-01-12)
- Sara: "annotators hate double-clicking to create nested spans" (triage notes)

End of log for now. Will iterate after we run the disagreement script.

PRIVACY NOTE: Examples above are synthetic/obfuscated. Do not paste customer PII from JIRA or slack into production exports.

-- Priya

source.title: Clauses to surface before signing — quick pocket checklist
source.content:
PURPOSE:
A compact, pragmatic checklist I can carry into negotiation threads or last-minute review calls. Focus is on high-risk items customers often try to slip in or want changed at signature time, plus one-line negotiation scripts and quick fallbacks to speed triage.

HOW TO USE:
- Run checklist before a final exec signature ask (do not rely on Sales summary alone).
- Mark items H/M/L risk and call Legal if any H items are present.

QUICK TRIAGE (one-line rationale):
- H: Unlimited liability / open-ended indemnities / customer-driven data export w/o controls — blocks signature without Legal/Eng design.
- M: Changes to data residency / audit rights / subprocessors — usually solvable with controls, SLA, or product offer.
- L: Cosmetic edits, definition clarifications, timing tweaks.

TOP ITEMS TO SURFACE (priority order + negotiation script + fallback):
1) Liability cap & carve-outs (H)
   - Why: Biggest business exposure.
   - Ask: "We can discuss carve-outs for gross negligence or willful misconduct, but the overall cap must remain limited to fees paid in 12 months."
   - Fallback: If they insist on higher cap, require priced SOW or additional insurance.

2) Data export / model residency / reverse engineering (H/M)
   - Why: Affects product architecture (Dedicated vs Private) and customer obligations.
   - Ask: "We can permit controlled exports for troubleshooting under an NDA and audit log; full export requires Dedicated/Private deployment and a transfer fee."
   - Fallback: Add time-bound sandbox export with redaction, or require dedicated environment.

3) Model output IP / ownership of derived outputs (M)
   - Why: Customers want certainty on downstream usage; we need to preserve rights to run models and improve service.
   - Ask: "Customer owns their prompts and outputs for their internal use; Redwood retains rights to service improvements and aggregated non-identifiable telemetry."
   - Fallback: Specify exclusive vs non-exclusive language and carve-outs for aggregated telemetry.

4) Indemnity breadth & triggers (H)
   - Why: Opens long tail risk (third-party claims, regulatory fines).
   - Ask: "Indemnities limited to direct damages caused by breach or IP infringement; exclude indirect, punitive, regulatory fines unless caused by Redwood's gross negligence."
   - Fallback: Add mutual indemnity for IP and a cap tied to fees.

5) Security incidents / breach notification SLA (M)
   - Why: Operational commitment and downstream obligations (customers must notify their customers).
   - Ask: "We commit to notification within 72 hours of detection and an incident report timeline; remediation obligations limited to reasonable efforts."
   - Fallback: Add optional SOC2 report delivery cadence or paid attestation.

6) Audit rights & on-site inspections (M)
   - Why: Operational burden, IP exposure risk.
   - Ask: "Limited, scheduled audits via third-party assessor, scope-limited to controls that materially affect customer data, subject to NDA and reasonable notice."
   - Fallback: Provide templates for security questionnaire or hosted attestation instead of on-site.

7) Subprocessors & flow-down obligations (M)
   - Why: We need flexibility to operate and scale; customers want control.
   - Ask: "We will list subprocessors and update customers; significant changes allow review and reasonable objections."
   - Fallback: Offer Dedicated/Private for strict requirements.
   
8) Termination & transition assistance (M/L)
   - Why: Ensures clean exit and data portability without open liabilities.
   - Ask: "Standard termination assistance for 90 days; data export in standard formats and reasonable fees for extended support."
   - Fallback: Price a one-time export & deletion service.

9) SLA / credits & measurement (M)
   - Why: Customers want predictable uptime; claims can lead to finger-pointing unless metrics are clear.
   - Ask: "We define SLOs per route/model and credits as sole remedy for downtimes measured by our dashboard/tracing."
   - Fallback: Offer a higher tier SLA (Dedicated) for stricter commitments.

10) Export controls / regulated data (H)
   - Why: Legal/regulatory constraints (GDPR, export, HIPAA) can make a deal untenable.
   - Ask: "We will not accept regulated data unless the customer signs off to use Private deployment with required attestations and controls."
   - Fallback: Decline or move to Private.

NEGOTIATION SCRIPTS (short):
- When they ask for open export: "We can support a limited export for debugging, but full export requires Private deployment—I can get you a quote and timeline."
- When they push for unlimited indemnity: "We understand the concern; we can mutualize IP indemnity but need a reasonable cap aligned to fees for commercial predictability."
- When they demand on-site audit immediately: "We can accommodate via a third-party assessor and scope it narrowly; on-site access is uncommon and we typically provide attestation artifacts instead."

QUICK ALTERNATIVE LANGUAGE (copy/paste friendly):
- Liability cap: "Except for liability for personal injury, death, or willful misconduct, each party's aggregate liability shall not exceed the amount paid under this Agreement in the 12 months preceding the claim."
- Data export: "Customer may request a one-time export of Customer Data in standard formats within 30 days of termination; such export is subject to a fee and verification steps to prevent exfiltration of Redwood IP."
- Subprocessors: "Redwood may engage subprocessors; notice will be provided and Customer may object for material security concerns within 15 days."

ESCALATION MAP (who to ping):
- H items: Legal (Ava Chen), Head of Product (Ravi), Eng lead (Liam) — immediate, copy CRO if signature imminent.
- Data architecture changes: Eng-Platform + Solutions Engineering (notify before promising Dedicated).
- Pricing concessions tied to legal exposure: Finance + Sales Ops.

PRODUCT & ENG IMPLICATION NOTES (so Sales doesn't promise engineering fiction):
- "Export" often requires Dedicated/Private + work to sanitize KV caches; flag to Eng with scope estimate.
- On-site or heavy audit requests -> may require SOC2 or custom attestation; time + cost.
- SLA improvements -> may require cross-region redundancy and reserved capacity; price uplift.

OPEN QUESTIONS / ACTIONS:
- [ ] Create one-pager for Sales summarizing "Export = Private" policy and pricing bands.
- [ ] Build template clause bank for common asks (IP, export, indemnity) to speed redlines.
- [ ] Align Sales on required approvals for H vs M edits (below $X threshold allow Legal redline; above escalate).

LAST-MINUTE SIGNATURE FRAME (three-sentence baton pass for Sales to use with customer exec):
"We want you to get value quickly. There are a handful of operational and liability items we need to keep standard to continue operating safely for all customers; we can surface them now and either add a short transition plan or a product-backed option (Dedicated/Private) so you get both security and a predictable timeline. If you want, I can loop Legal for a five-minute call to close these last two points today."

NOTES TO SELF (personal heuristics):
- If a clause introduces ongoing unlimited obligations or undefined auditing mechanics => H.
- Always translate legal asks into engineering impacts (time, infra, cost) when responding.
- Keep fallback options productized (Dedicated/Private/priced export) so negotiation doesn't become custom work.

END.
