source.title: Attestation nightcap notes — evidence hunt & cutover checklist
source.content:
Context: quick brain dump after the SOC2 pre-audit dry run (late shift). Goal is to capture loose evidence threads, quick mitigations we can do before the audit window, and a short cutover checklist for the on-call coverage during the actual attestation. This is messy, intentionally — use as a handoff to morning crew.

High-level takeaways from dry run:
- Evidence gaps concentrated around ephemeral infra (CI agents, ephemeral keys) and human processes (onboarding docs out-of-date).
- Observability evidence exists but scattered (traces in Splunk, traces in local dev accounts). Need canonical links.
- Incident log continuity: some older tickets were archived without postmortem; need at least summary notes for auditor.

Late-night evidence hunt plan (ordered by impact / time-to-finish):
1) SSO: generate consolidated export of SAML config + admin change history (last 24 months). Easy win: call out to identity-admin role, ask @ava for export (Slack).
2) KMS: verify key rotation logs. If rotation events are missing from the central KMS dashboard, fetch cloud-provider audit logs (region: us-west-2). Put proof in a single GDrive folder.
3) CI/CD: find last 6 months of pipeline run approvals for prod deploys. Some deploys used ephemeral runner tokens — need to map runner tokens to org service accounts. TODO: ask infra-team to tag runner service accounts with stable alias.
4) Secrets management: proof of least-privilege for secret scopes in repo X and repo Y. We have partial docs in confluence but not reconciled with IAM. Quick task: generate 'secrets-report.csv' from vault CLI (obtain from @samir).
5) Backups: validate that DB backups have both encryption and successful restore test in last 12 months. We have restore test notes but need a signed owner confirmation email for auditor.

Unresolved / tricky questions to ask auditor (draft):
- For ephemeral infra (k8s pod identities, GitHub Actions masks), is a running policy snapshot required or is infrastructure-as-code repo + logs sufficient? (Note: auditors vary)
- What level of detail on PR-based approvals is acceptable? commit hashes + approver + timestamp, or do they expect ticket link too?

Cutover / handoff checklist for morning crew (short actionable):
- [ ] Drop a single GDrive folder: /evidence/soc2/diego-nightcap-YYYYMM, copy in exports and name them with prefix "evidence-YYYYMM-<type>".
- [ ] Ping @ava in #trust-ops to request SAML admin export — paste resulting URL in the folder and mark done.
- [ ] Run vault secrets-report and upload CSV; cc @samir for validation.
- [ ] Pull KMS audit events for us-west-2, export to JSON, and place in folder.
- [ ] Collect last 6 months of prod pipeline approvals (circleci / jenkins / github actions), tag with pipeline name and date range.
- [ ] Draft short summary line for any archived incidents without PM: include date, owner, quick impact, and whether there was a remediation. Put under /evidence/incidents-summary.txt.

Quick snippets / commands I used (copyable):
- SAML admin history (identity-admin): gcloud iam roles get-iam-policy --project redwood-infra --filter="role:roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" --format=json > evidence/evidence-202511-saml-admin.json
- Vault export (needs vault CLI + token): vault list -format=json secret/ | jq . > evidence/evidence-202511-secrets.json
- KMS logs (aws): aws cloudtrail lookup-events --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=ResourceName,AttributeValue=alias/redwood-kms --start-time 2024-11-01 --end-time 2025-11-03 --output json > evidence/evidence-202511-kms.json

People to loop in (quick):
- Ava Chen (identity) — slack @ava — primary for SSO export.
- Samir Patel (infra/vault) — ask for secrets-report and runner-service-account map.
- Lila Nguyen (platform-eng) — backup restore confirmation.
- Priya Shah (devex) — help stitch docs and add links to console screenshots.

Notes to self / heuristics:
- Auditor will accept consolidated artifacts if we provide a clear provenance path (where it came from, who ran the export, command used, and a timestamped confirmation).
- For anything ephemeral: create a 'snapshot' artifact (export + text note describing how to reproduce). That reduces follow-up.

Low-effort confidence boosters (quick patches to do overnight or early morning):
- One-paragraph signed attestations from service owners confirming restore/test and rotation windows. Template: "I, <owner>, confirm that <component> had backups and successful restore on <date>. Evidence attached: <file>". Ask managers to sign in Slack thread.
- Small README in each evidence folder with a single line: provenance command + who ran it + Slack timestamp. Keeps auditors from hunting.

Open TODOs (assignments rough):
- Ava: SAML export (high) — due ASAP.
- Samir: vault secrets-report + runner-account map (high).
- Lila: backup restore confirmation email (med).
- Me (Diego): consolidate folder, add README, and run quick grep to ensure no PII leaks in exported text.

Random brainstorming (might be useful later):
- Could we produce a single 'attestation snapshot' container image that includes all exports plus a small index.html for auditors to click through? Portable and reproducible. Might be overkill but handy for future cycles.
- Consider tiny wrapper script that standardizes evidence file names and appends a metadata header (owner, command, timestamp).

Slack references / tickets:
- #trust-ops thread: pings at 2025-11-03T22:10Z — copy link later.
- Jira ticket: SOCAT-842 (dry run followups) — add artifacts as attachments.

End-of-night quick status: folder seeded with 3 exports (partial), commands documented above, owners pinged. Morning crew will finish. If I wake up before they do, I will try the KMS pull and vault export again.

——
Notes to future self: keep this lightweight — auditors want traces of process, not opulence. Small, well-documented artifacts beat big, messy bundles.

5) Backups: validate that DB backups have both encryption and successful restore test in last 12 months. We have restore test notes but need a signed owner confirmation email for auditor.

Unresolved / tricky questions to ask auditor (draft):
- For ephemeral infra (k8s pod identities, GitHub Actions masks), is a running policy snapshot required or is infrastructure-as-code repo + logs sufficient? (Note: auditors vary)
- What level of detail on PR-based approvals is acceptable? commit hashes + approver + timestamp, or do they expect ticket link too?

Cutover / handoff checklist for morning crew (short actionable):
- [ ] Drop a single GDrive folder: /evidence/soc2/diego-nightcap-YYYYMM, copy in exports and name them with prefix "evidence-YYYYMM-<type>".
- [ ] Ping @ava in #trust-ops to request SAML admin export — paste resulting URL in the folder and mark done.
- [ ] Run vault secrets-report and upload CSV; cc @samir for validation.
- [ ] Pull KMS audit events for us-west-2, export to JSON, and place in folder.
- [ ] Collect last 6 months of prod pipeline approvals (circleci / jenkins / github actions), tag with pipeline name and date range.
- [ ] Draft short summary line for any archived incidents without PM: include date, owner, quick impact, and whether there was a remediation. Put under /evidence/incidents-summary.txt.

Quick snippets / commands I used (copyable):
- SAML admin history (identity-admin): gcloud iam roles get-iam-policy --project redwood-infra --filter="role:roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" --format=json > evidence/evidence-202511-saml-admin.json
- Vault export (needs vault CLI + token): vault list -format=json secret/ | jq . > evidence/evidence-202511-secrets.json
- KMS logs (aws): aws cloudtrail lookup-events --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=ResourceName,AttributeValue=alias/redwood-kms --start-time 2024-11-01 --end-time 2025-11-03 --output json > evidence/evidence-202511-kms.json

People to loop in (quick):
- Ava Chen (identity) — slack @ava — primary for SSO export.
- Samir Patel (infra/vault) — ask for secrets-report and runner-service-account map.
- Lila Nguyen (platform-eng) — backup restore confirmation.
- Priya Shah (devex) — help stitch docs and add links to console screenshots.

Notes to self / heuristics:
- Auditor will accept consolidated artifacts if we provide a clear provenance path (where it came from, who ran the export, command used, and a timestamped confirmation).
- For anything ephemeral: create a 'snapshot' artifact (export + text note describing how to reproduce). That reduces follow-up.

Low-effort confidence boosters (quick patches to do overnight or early morning):
- One-paragraph signed attestations from service owners confirming restore/test and rotation windows. Template: "I, <owner>, confirm that <component> had backups and successful restore on <date>. Evidence attached: <file>". Ask managers to sign in Slack thread.
- Small README in each evidence folder with a single line: provenance command + who ran it + Slack timestamp. Keeps auditors from hunting.

Open TODOs (assignments rough):
- Ava: SAML export (high) — due ASAP.
- Samir: vault secrets-report + runner-account map (high).
- Lila: backup restore confirmation email (med).
- Me (Diego): consolidate folder, add README, and run quick grep to ensure no PII leaks in exported text.

Random brainstorming (might be useful later):
- Could we produce a single 'attestation snapshot' container image that includes all exports plus a small index.html for auditors to click through? Portable and reproducible. Might be overkill but handy for future cycles.
- Consider tiny wrapper script that standardizes evidence file names and appends a metadata header (owner, command, timestamp).

Slack references / tickets:
- #trust-ops thread: pings at 2025-11-03T22:10Z — copy link later.
- Jira ticket: SOCAT-842 (dry run followups) — add artifacts as attachments.

End-of-night quick status: folder seeded with 3 exports (partial), commands documented above, owners pinged. Morning crew will finish. If I wake up before they do, I will try the KMS pull and vault export again.

——
Notes to future self: keep this lightweight — auditors want traces of process, not opulence. Small, well-documented artifacts beat big, messy bundles.

source.title: Prompt Weave Tinkerpad
source.content:
Purpose and mental model:
This is my personal tinkerpad for the "weave" approach — building small composable prompt fragments (cues, constraints, neutralizers) and trying to stitch them together into robust few-shot templates. Goal is pragmatic: get reliable behavior across model variants and quantization profiles without exploding token budgets.

High-level hypotheses to validate:
- Short, labeled cues + 2 exemplars give more stable instruction-following than 1 long system message for our retrieval-style assistant tasks.
- Explicit "neutralizer" sentences reduce confident hallucinations in domain-limited Q&A (finance/legal) more than lowering temperature alone.
- Interleaving negative exemplars (bad outputs) helps models avoid repeating common failure modes when the domain has many ambiguous entities.

Core concepts (vocabulary used in notes below):
- cue: tiny instruction fragment (1–2 sentences) that sets tone/role
- exemplar: a single few-shot pair (input -> desired output)
- seam: the token boundary where two fragments join; seams are fragile
- neutralizer: short statement that tells model what not to do (avoid fabrication, say "I don't know")

Baseline templates (to iterate on):
1) Compact role + exemplars
Prompt: "You are a concise policy assistant. Follow examples exactly.
Example 1:
Q: What is XYZ policy?
A: [short factual answer with citation tag]
Example 2:
Q: How to escalate?
A: [step-by-step checklist]
Now answer:
Q: {user_question}
A:"
Notes: keep system cue one sentence. Use 2 exemplars that are structurally similar to the target.

2) Cue + neutralizer + exemplars
Prompt: "You are an internal compliance aide. If unsure, respond 'I don't know' and suggest a next step. Do not invent policies.
Example (good): ...
Example (bad): ...
Q: {user}
A:"
Notes: explicitly tested to reduce hallucination in banking prompts.

Few-shot exemplar set (local canonical bank) -- keep to 2–3 exemplars per template to limit tokens:
- Exemplar A (FAQ style):
  Input: "Is reimbursement allowed for conference travel?"
  Output: "Yes — standard policy: itemize expenses, submit within 30 days. See policy 4.2."
  Tag: good/concise
- Exemplar B (escalation):
  Input: "I found suspicious billing in vendor invoice"
  Output: "Stop payment, escalate to procurement@, open ticket INF-1202."
  Tag: actionable/links
- Negative exemplar C (what to avoid):
  Input: "Tell me the vendor's internal discount"
  Output: "Avoid guessing or fabricating vendor terms."
  Tag: negative

Planned stress tests and failure probes:
- Ambiguous-entity probe: swap names and dates; check whether model hallucinates specific vendor contracts. Expect neutralizer to cause 'I don't know.'
- Overfitting probe: vary wording slightly (synonyms, passive voice) to detect brittle seams.
- Injection probe: append an unrelated phrase that looks like an instruction; ensure model follows original cue, not injected text.
- Token-budget probe: measure output length variance when using 1 vs 3 exemplars across quantized models.

Sample failure cases we've seen (notes + root cause guesses):
- Fluent confabulation: model returns a plausible-looking clause with legalese and a fake clause number. Root cause: training priors favor fluent completion; prompt lacks explicit refusal pattern.
- Prototype copying: model reproduces an exemplar's phrasing instead of adapting. Root cause: exemplar too close to target or seam causes copying.
- Over-apologizing/hedging: model adds long hedges even when asked for concise answers. Root cause: system cue ambiguous about brevity.

Mitigations to experiment with (matrix):
- neutralizer placement: top vs just before question
- exemplar redundancy: unique tokens across exemplars vs templated overlaps
- negative exemplars: include 1 negative + small commentary about why it's bad
- seam spacing: add "--" or a short divider phrase between sections to reduce cross-contamination

Measurement plan (quick and dirty for local runs):
- Stability score: fraction of runs where model gives expected label within 3 attempts (temperature sweep 0.0, 0.2, 0.5).
- Hallucination rate: percent of answers with fabricated entity tokens (manual vet on sample of 200).
- Brevity index: mean tokens used for correct answers; target < 40 tokens for FAQ.
- Copy rate: percent of answers that verbatim match any exemplar.

Quick experiments to run (priority order):
1) Neutralizer top vs bottom (2 exemplars) across gpt-small/medium variants — measure hallucination & brevity.
2) Negative exemplar inclusion (1 negative) across temp 0.0, 0.2.
3) Seam divider experiment: replace newlines with "--" and compare copy rate.

Example prompts to paste into quick-run harness:
- Template A (compact):
"You are an internal policy assistant. Answer concisely using the same structure as examples.

EX1
Q: What is allowed under travel policy?
A: ...

EX2
Q: How to file receipts?
A: ...

Q: {user}
A:"

- Template B (neutralizer bottom):
"You are a helpful internal assistant.
EX1...
EX2...
If you do not have specific knowledge, say 'I don't know' and recommend how to check.
Q: {user}
A:"

Operational notes / gotchas:
- When moving from hosted to dedicated quantized endpoints, re-check exemplar tokenization: some rare ASCII variants split differently and break seam heuristics.
- Avoid long example dialogues; models tend to fold the last speaker's style into the answer.
- Watch out for "helpful but wrong" — sometimes lower temperature reduces hallucination but increases copy rate.

TODOs (short actionable list):
- [ ] Run neutralizer top/bottom experiment and log results to jira:INF-879 (owner: Ethan) due 2025-12-10
- [ ] Add 20 more negative exemplars harvested from support transcripts (owner: Lina)
- [ ] Create small eval set (n=200) for ambiguity probes and add to confluence PROJ-PROMPT-112
- [ ] Try seam divider "//" vs "--" and record copy rates on 3 model variants

Open questions / thought seeds:
- Is the negative exemplar acting as an explicit instruction to avoid something, or is it teaching by contrast? Are there cases where negative exemplars backfire?
- How does exemplar lexical overlap affect transfer when target asks for numeric formatting (dates/currency)?
- Can we compress exemplar signals into a single synthetic exemplar that preserves effect but saves tokens?

Scratchpad (raw prompt tinkering snippets):
- "Role: compliance assistant. Tone: terse. If unsure: 'I don't know' + 1 next step."
- "Divider idea: \" --- SEAM --- \" inserted between examples and target."
- Negative exemplar snippet: Q: 'Do we have vendor discounts?' A: 'Do not guess vendor terms; check contract or procurement.'

If you poke at this, please jot quick numbers (hallucination rate, copy rate, avg tokens) in the top of the doc. Saves time later.

End of tinkerpad entry. Will iterate as we get experimental results.
